EDCS-00380923.text_edition: \n Cohors VI Thracum turma Ve Iuli\n
EDCS-00380923.translation: The troop of the VI Cohort of Thracians, the fifth.
EDCS-00380923.summary: This inscription names the fifth troop of the VI Cohort of Thracians, likely part of a military standard or monument, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380924.text_edition: \n Aurelius Gaianus |(centurio) cohortis V Gallorum Antoninianae ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-00380924.translation: Aurelius Gaianus, a centurion of the V Gallorum Antoniniana Cohort, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-00380924.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Aurelius Gaianus, a centurion of the V Gallorum Antoniniana Cohort, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-03300802.text_edition: \n Lucius Antonius Luci filius Quirina Valens Sagalasso \n
EDCS-03300802.translation: Lucius Antonius Valens, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, from Sagalassos.
EDCS-03300802.summary: This is a personal inscription recording the name and tribal affiliation of Lucius Antonius Valens from Sagalassos, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300808.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Charitine vixit annos XLV Titus Claudius Valerianus miles legionis VII Claudiae stipendiorum IIII vixit annos XXII Titus Claudius Valens veteranus ex tesserario legionis supra scriptae coniugi et filio karissimis bene merentibus posuit et se vivo et Iuliae Valentiae et Titis Claudiis Valentino Valenti et Valentioni filis fecit\n
EDCS-03300808.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Charitine lived 45 years. Titus Claudius Valerianus, a soldier of the VII Claudia Legion with 4 years of service, lived 22 years. Titus Claudius Valens, a veteran discharged from the aforementioned legion as a tesserarius, erected this for his most beloved wife and son, and also made it for himself during his lifetime, and for his sons Iulia Valentia, Titus Claudius Valens, and Valento.
EDCS-03300808.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Iulia Charitine and Titus Claudius Valerianus, set up by the veteran Titus Claudius Valens for his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700710.text_edition: \n Somno C CI votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-03700710.translation: To Sleep, 201 vows fulfilled willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-03700710.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of 201 vows made to the god Somnus, possibly a dedication from a religious site in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05000713.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tattae Idati Ablabius et Pontia cum liberis\n
EDCS-05000713.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Ablabius and Pontia with their children.
EDCS-05000713.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Ablabius and Pontia and their children, likely from a private shrine during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-05200610.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Asclep<ia>des ex voto paterno aram posuit\n
EDCS-05200610.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Asclepiades set up this altar to Melas, fulfilling a paternal vow.
EDCS-05200610.summary: This inscription records an altar dedicated to Jupiter and Melas by Asclepiades, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100501.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto pro salute Marci Aureli Antonini Publius Flavius Felix Publi filius vicomagister consensu collegi vici sententia posuit ex vico Bubensium per Aelium Probum patrem\n
EDCS-06100501.translation: To Hercules Augustus for the safety of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Publius Flavius Felix, son of Publius, neighborhood leader, set up this monument with the consent of the college and by decree of the neighborhood, through Aelius Probus, the father, from the neighborhood of Bubensium.
EDCS-06100501.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Hercules Augustus for the emperor's safety, made by a local official and community in the Bubensium neighborhood, likely dating to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-06100502.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Duriacus Ithotsi filius annorum L Valerius Victor veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae bene merito posuit\n
EDCS-06100502.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Duriacus, son of Ithotsos, aged 50, Valerius Victor, a veteran of the IIII Flavia Legion, set this up for his well-deserving deceased.
EDCS-06100502.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Valerius Victor, a veteran of the IIII Flavia Legion, for Duriacus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000847.text_edition: \n Aelius Niceforus libertus ipse sibi et Bae con[iugi ------\n
EDCS-07000847.translation: Aelius Nicephorus, a freedman, for himself and for Bae, his wife [---
EDCS-07000847.summary: This fragmented inscription records a dedication made by Aelius Nicephorus, a freedman, for himself and his wife Bae, likely a funerary monument from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07600419.summary: This inscription likely refers to the Roman imperial settlement of Felix Romuliana, possibly indicating a dedication or a place name.

EDCS-08400457.summary: This inscription is a fragment, likely recording the name of Lucius Coelius Saturninus, possibly as a deceased individual or a benefactor.

EDCS-08400458.summary: This inscription is a fragment, likely recording the name of Lucius Coelius Ingenuus, possibly as a deceased individual or a benefactor.

EDCS-08400459.summary: This inscription is a fragment, likely recording the name of Lucius Coelius Primus, possibly as a deceased individual or a benefactor.

EDCS-08400460.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records Primitivus, a decurion (town councillor) of a colony, possibly as the subject of a dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-08400471.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Augusto Invicto milia passuum \n
EDCS-08400471.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Augustus, the Invincible, [a distance of X] miles
EDCS-08400471.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Trajan Decius, likely related to a public work such as a road, with the distance missing.

EDCS-08500592.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Cocceius Viator vixit nnos LV Ulpia Annia vixit nnos XXXV hic siti sunt Cocceius Ingenuus parentibus pientissimis posuit\n
EDCS-08500592.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Cocceius Viator lived 55 years. Ulpia Annia lived 35 years. Here they lie. Cocceius Ingenuus set this up for his most devoted parents.
EDCS-08500592.summary: This is an epitaph for Marcus Cocceius Viator and Ulpia Annia, who are honored by their son Cocceius Ingenuus, with the parents' ages and burial place indicated.

EDCS-08500596.text_edition: \n I a Ursula as servus vivis fecerunt\n
EDCS-08500596.translation: I and Ursula, as slaves, made this for the living.
EDCS-08500596.summary: This inscription records a dedication or building by two slaves, I and Ursula, for themselves while alive.

EDCS-08600630.text_edition: \n Cohors I Lusitanorum centuria Claudi Laedius\n
EDCS-08600630.translation: First Cohort of Lusitanians, century of Claudius Laedius
EDCS-08600630.summary: This inscription likely marks a standard or monument belonging to the first cohort of Lusitanians, under the command of Claudius Laedius.

EDCS-08900588.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Caius Iulius Victorinus miles legionis VII Claudiae beneficiarius consularis votum solvit\n
EDCS-08900588.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Gaius Iulius Victorinus, a soldier of the VII Claudia Legion, a beneficiarius consularis, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-08900588.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Iulius Victorinus, a soldier in the VII Claudia Legion, dedicating a vow to Jupiter and Juno.

EDCS-08901136.text_edition: \n Impperatores Caessares Caius Valerius Aurelius Diocletianus et Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus PPii FFelices Invicti Auggusti et Flavius Valerius Constantius et Galerius Valerius Maximianus nobilissimi Caesares Germanici maximi Sarmatici maximi profuturum in aeternum rei publicae praesidium constituerunt\n
EDCS-08901136.translation: The Emperors and Caesars Gaius Valerius Aurelius Diocletian and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Fathers of the Fatherland, Happy Invincible Augusti, and the most noble Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, great conquerors of the Germans and Sarmatians, have established a lasting defense for the state.
EDCS-08901136.summary: This inscription records an imperial decree by Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, establishing a permanent defense for the Roman state, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-08901148.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexti Valeri Sexti liberti Palatina Zotici vixit annos XLII hic situs est Sextus Valerius Hilarus Augustalis coloniae Ratiariae heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-08901148.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Sextus Valerius Zoticus, freedman of Sextus Valerius, of the Palatina tribe, lived 42 years, is buried here. Sextus Valerius Hilarus, Augustalis of the colony of Ratiaria, his heir, saw to its construction.
EDCS-08901148.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Valerius Zoticus, an enslaved person freed by Sextus Valerius, with the tomb erected by his heir, Sextus Valerius Hilarus, an official in Ratiaria, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08901149.text_edition: \n Divos testor Dis Manibus Antoniae Antoni libertae Nicopoli vixit nnos XXX hic sita est Marcus Antonius Glycon coniugi et libertae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-08901149.translation: I swear by the gods, to the Spirits of the Underworld. Antonia Nicopolis, freedwoman of Antonius, lived 30 years, is buried here. Marcus Antonius Glycon erected this for his wife and well-deserving freedwoman.
EDCS-08901149.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Antonia Nicopolis, a freedwoman, and is dedicated by her husband Marcus Antonius Glycon, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08901150.text_edition: \n Lucius Septimius Dulacenus ex signifero vixit annis XL militavit annis XXII Antoninus Aiacartus hic situs sunt\n
EDCS-08901150.translation: Lucius Septimius Dulacenus lived 40 years, served 22 years as a standard-bearer. Antoninus Aiacartus. They are buried here.
EDCS-08901150.summary: This epitaph commemorates Lucius Septimius Dulacenus, a standard-bearer who served for 22 years and lived to 40, and Antoninus Aiacartus, likely a soldier buried together, possibly from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08901151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Aemilio Cai liberto Sabiniano vixit annos LV hic situs est BA\n
EDCS-08901151.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Aemilius Sabinianus, son of Gaius Aemilius, lived 55 years, is buried here. BA [initials of the person erecting the monument].
EDCS-08901151.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Aemilius Sabinianus, son of Gaius Aemilius, who lived 55 years, with the monument erected by someone whose initials are BA, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08901153.translation: ------ Adiabeni, great conqueror of the Parthians ------
EDCS-08901153.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely recording military achievements, specifically victories against the Adiabeni and Parthians, possibly part of a triumphal monument or a soldier's epitaph.

EDCS-09100862.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio Illyrici pro salute Bellici Augusti nostri vilici stationis Petobionensis sic et Moesiae regionis Aquensis Castricius Bellici vikarius sic votum solvit libens merito anni XXV Kalendis Februariis Fusco II Dextro consulibus\n
EDCS-09100862.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of Illyricum, for the health of our Augustus Bellicus, the supervisor of the Petobiona station, and likewise of the Moesia Aquensis region. Castricius Bellici, his deputy, paid this vow freely and deservedly, in his 25th year, on the Kalends of February, under the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.
EDCS-09100862.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Castricius Bellici, a deputy supervisor, to Jupiter and the Genius of Illyricum for the health of his superior, Augustus Bellicus, dating to 148 CE based on the consuls.

EDCS-09100863.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Cidiesso Aelius Octavianus veteranus cum suis votum posuit\n
EDCS-09100863.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aelius Octavianus, a veteran, set up this vow with his family.
EDCS-09100863.summary: This inscription is a votive offering dedicated to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Aelius Octavianus, a military veteran, and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09100864.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valens sitae sibi coniugi Daedae Iunctae idemque Aniae Dasi vivae vivus posuit\n
EDCS-09100864.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valens erected this for his wife Daeda Iuncta, who is buried here, and for Ania Dasa, who is alive, while he himself is alive.
EDCS-09100864.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Valens to his wife Daeda Iuncta and to Ania Dasa, who was alive at the time of its erection, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09100865.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Possidonius Biti vixit annos LXX Iusta coniunx et heres marito et sibi viva bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-09100865.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Possidonius Biti lived 70 years. Iusta, his wife and heir, erected this for her husband and for herself, while alive, for their well-deserving status.
EDCS-09100865.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Possidonius Biti, who lived 70 years, and was erected by his wife and heir, Iusta, for both of them while she was still alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09100866.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Andius Austeri filius vixit annos LXX\n
EDCS-09100866.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Andius, son of Austerus, lived 70 years.
EDCS-09100866.summary: A tombstone records the death of Andius, who lived 70 years.

EDCS-09100867.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Caeanus miles legionis VII vexit sic annos XXV Desumena coniux\n
EDCS-09100867.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Caeanus, a soldier of the VII Legion, lived 25 years. Desumena, his wife [erected this].
EDCS-09100867.summary: A tombstone records the death of Aelius Caeanus, a soldier who lived 25 years, and his wife Desumena.

EDCS-09100868.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Petronia vixit annis XII Aurelius Papilus et Aurelia Caia vivi sibi et filiae bene merenti posuerunt hic sita est\n
EDCS-09100868.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Petronia lived 12 years. Aurelius Papilus and Aurelia Caia, while alive, set this up for themselves and their well-deserving daughter. She is buried here.
EDCS-09100868.summary: A tombstone records the death of Aurelia Petronia, aged 12, and was erected by her parents.

EDCS-09100869.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Clementilla vixit annos XVII summae intergritatis feminae Aurelius Maior ex protectoribus divini lateris coniugi obsequentissimae posuit\n
EDCS-09100869.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Clementilla lived 17 years, a woman of the highest integrity. Aurelius Maior, formerly of the protectors of the divine presence, placed this for his most obedient wife.
EDCS-09100869.summary: A tombstone records the death of Aurelia Clementilla, aged 17, and was erected by her husband, Aurelius Maior.

EDCS-09100870.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Caianu<n>s sal<gam=MAG>arius vixit annis Aurelia Alexandria vixit annis Z hic siti sunt collegius sic DEIRB ex testamento A<l=E>exandriae supra scriptae quot sic suis sumptibus fecerat per senatus consultum posuerunt sic \n
EDCS-09100870.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Caianus, a salt-maker, lived [X] years. Aurelia Alexandria lived [Y] years. They are buried here. The college, by a decree of the senate, erected this from their own funds, as stipulated in the will of the aforementioned Alexandria.
EDCS-09100870.summary: A tombstone records the deaths of Aurelius Caianus and Aurelia Alexandria, erected by their collegium according to Alexandria's will.

EDCS-09100871.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Stilo Babonis vixit annos L hic situs est Aurelia Ziza Merulae marito et Aurelius Aquilinus patri piissimo et sibi vivi faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09100871.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Stilo Babonis lived 50 years. He is buried here. Aurelia Ziza had this made while alive for her husband and Aurelius Aquilinus, her most dutiful father, and for herself.
EDCS-09100871.summary: A tombstone records the death of Aurelius Stilo Babonis, aged 50, and was commissioned by his wife and son for their family.

EDCS-09100872.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Iusta se viva posuit Ulpio Castino aedelicio sic vixit annis VLV filii Castus vivit sic annis XIIII Iovius annis XI Aurelianus annis X Ninis annis III Utinadius annis IIII Ulpia Castina coniugi bene merito opto sit terra levis\n
EDCS-09100872.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Iusta erected this for herself while alive, for Ulpius Castinus, holding the office of aedile, who lived 5 years; their son Castus lived 14 years, Iovius 11 years, Aurelianus 10 years, Ninis 3 years, Utinadius 4 years. May the earth lie lightly on Ulpia Castina, her well-deserving wife.
EDCS-09100872.summary: A tombstone erected by Aurelia Iusta records her death and the deaths of her husband Ulpius Castinus and their five young sons, along with a dedication to her as a deserving wife.

EDCS-09100873.text_edition: \n Caius Domitius Lucinianus filius beneficiarius consularis foro posuit \n
EDCS-09100873.translation: Caius Domitius Lucinianus, son of [X], a beneficiary of the governor, set this up in the forum.
EDCS-09100873.summary: This inscription records Caius Domitius Lucinianus, a beneficiary of a governor, setting up a monument in the forum.

EDCS-09100874.text_edition: \n Aurelius Caius centurio cohortis I Cretum Aurelius Caius centurio cohortis I Cretum\n
EDCS-09100874.translation: Aurelius Caius, centurion of the 1st Cohort of Cretians. Aurelius Caius, centurion of the 1st Cohort of Cretians.
EDCS-09100874.summary: This inscription, likely a duplication or a dedication, names Aurelius Caius as a centurion of the 1st Cohort of Cretians.

EDCS-09300644.text_edition: \n I colonia Singidunensium Quinto Ulpio DAP legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-09300644.translation: To the colony of Singidunum, for Quintus Ulpius [Dap...], legate of Augustus, propraetor.
EDCS-09300644.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the colony of Singidunum to Quintus Ulpius, a legate and propraetor.

EDCS-09401519.text_edition: \n hic sita est vixit annos XXVIII menses VI vita integerrima Iulius Mucianus actuarius coniugi \n
EDCS-09401519.translation: Here lies, who lived 28 years, 6 months, of the most upright life, Julius Mucianus, scribe, for his wife.
EDCS-09401519.summary: This is a tombstone recording the burial of Julius Mucianus, who was a scribe, for his wife, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09401520.text_edition: \n Flavia Secundina hic sita est vixit annos |(circiter XXX Phoebio coniu[gi ------\n
EDCS-09401520.translation: Flavia Secundina lies here, who lived approximately 30 years, for her husband Phoebius [---].
EDCS-09401520.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Flavia Secundina, who lived approximately 30 years, for her husband, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09401521.summary: This incomplete tombstone records the life span of Flavius Iustinianus, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09700324.text_edition: \n Aniensis Foro Iulio miles legionis I Italicae hic situs est nnum XVIII Lucius Octavius Pulcher |(centurio) legionis I Italicae cognato suo fecit\n
EDCS-09700324.translation: Lucius Octavius Pulcher, centurion of the I Italica Legion, made this for his kinsman Aniensis Foro Iulio, a soldier of the I Italica Legion, who lived 18 years.
EDCS-09700324.summary: This tombstone records a dedication made by a centurion of the I Italica Legion to his kinsman, a soldier of the same legion who died at 18, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09701192.text_edition: \n Caius Antonius Eutyches Antoniae Claudiae patronae vivae posuit\n
EDCS-09701192.translation: Caius Antonius Eutyches erected this for his patroness Antonia Claudia, while she was alive.
EDCS-09701192.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Caius Antonius Eutyches to his patroness Antonia Claudia during her lifetime, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-10000137.translation: Caius Valerius Crescens, [from the] century of Veri.
EDCS-10000137.summary: This is a very brief inscription identifying Caius Valerius Crescens as belonging to the century of Veri, likely Roman military.

EDCS-10000139.summary: This is a very brief inscription indicating the century of Axsi, likely Roman military.

EDCS-10000140.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Domitia Panthia vixit nnos XVIIII menses IIII dies XX quae sine data morte mortua est pater pientissimus se vivo memoriam posuit filiae pientissimae fec(it)\n
EDCS-10000140.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Domitia Panthia lived 19 years, 4 months, 20 days, who died without a date of death; her most devoted father, during his own lifetime, set up this memorial for his most devoted daughter.
EDCS-10000140.summary: This tombstone records the death of Domitia Panthia at the age of 19 years, 4 months, and 20 days, with a memorial erected by her devoted father, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-10000141.text_edition: \n Deam Hecaten sic Triviae Triformi Valerius Crescentio miles legionis IIII Flaviae Maximinianae pro se et suis votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-10000141.translation: Valerius Crescentio, soldier of the IIII Flavia Maximiniana Legion, placed this vow for himself and his family to the goddess Hecate, also called Trivia, Triform, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10000141.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Valerius Crescentio, a soldier of the IIII Flavia Maximiniana Legion, to the goddess Hecate for himself and his family, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-10000144.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attia Soteris vixit annos V hic sita est\n
EDCS-10000144.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Attia Soteris lived 5 years. She lies here.
EDCS-10000144.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Attia Soteris, who lived for 5 years, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-10000152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Saturninus vixit annos V menses III Flavia Cornelia mater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10000152.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Flavius Saturninus lived 5 years and 3 months. Flavia Cornelia, his mother, saw to its construction.
EDCS-10000152.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the short life of Titus Flavius Saturninus, erected by his mother.

EDCS-10000154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaius Acilius Crispus vixit annis XXXV hic situs est Gaius Acilius Dexter filio pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10000154.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Acilius Crispus lived 35 years and is buried here. Gaius Acilius Dexter erected this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-10000154.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Acilius Crispus, erected by his father.

EDCS-10000175.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Drusi filio Caesare Augusto Germanico pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VI consule designato IIII legio IIII Scythica legio V Macedonica montibus excisis factisque anconibus sub cura Marti Macri legati Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-10000175.translation: For Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, designated consul for the 4th time, by the Fourth Scythian legion and the Fifth Macedonian legion, through the excavation of mountains and construction of harbors, under the supervision of Marcus Martius Macri, legate of the emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-10000175.summary: A dedication related to military construction projects (mountain excavation, harbor building) overseen by a Roman legate, likely during the reign of Emperor Claudius.

EDCS-10000179.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque dis deabus huiusce loci pro salute domini nostri M Ucenteus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum solvit\n
EDCS-10000179.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all the gods and goddesses of this place, for the safety of our lord. M. Ucenteus, a beneficiarius consularis of the Seventh Claudian legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000179.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a vow to Jupiter and local deities for the well-being of an imperial official, made by a soldier.

EDCS-10000181.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Nemesi Reginae Genio stationis M Dardaniae Septimius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-10000181.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Nemesis Regina, and to the Genius of the M. Dardania station. Septimius Vitalis, a beneficiarius consularis of the Seventh Claudian legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000181.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a vow to Jupiter, Nemesis, and the Genius of a military station, made by a soldier.

EDCS-10000484.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Tarae veterano cohortis III Campestris vexillario vixit annos LX Ulpia Marcia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10000484.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Tarae, a veteran of the Third Campestres cohort, a vexillarius, lived 60 years. Ulpia Marcia placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-10000484.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Tarae, a veteran soldier, erected by his wife.

EDCS-10000485.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caerelia Quintilla vixit aannos XL \n
EDCS-10000485.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Caerelia Quintilla lived 40 years.
EDCS-10000485.summary: A simple funerary epitaph recording the age of Caerelia Quintilla.

EDCS-10000486.text_edition: \n Crispus D domo Inaquiese vixsit sic annis XXX meruit annis VI miles cohortis I Cantabrorum heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10000486.translation: Crispus from Dinaquise lived 30 years, served for 6 years as a soldier in the First Cantabrian cohort. His heir saw to its construction.
EDCS-10000486.summary: A funerary epitaph for Crispus, a soldier from Dinaquise, recording his age and length of service.

EDCS-10000487.text_edition: \n Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens Marci filius vixit nnos V hic situs est memoriam fecit\n
EDCS-10000487.translation: Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens, son of Marcius, lived 5 years. He is buried here. [Someone] made this memorial.
EDCS-10000487.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young boy named Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens, recording his age.

EDCS-10000488.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Pompeius Isauricus ex votu sic posuit\n
EDCS-10000488.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus. Pompeius Isauricus placed this in fulfillment of his vow.
EDCS-10000488.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a monument to Jupiter Dolichenus, made by Pompeius Isauricus.

EDCS-10000490.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate V pater patriae consul IIII ob periculum cataractarum derivato flumine tutam Danuvi navigationem fecit\n
EDCS-10000490.translation: Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power V, Father of the Fatherland, Consul for the fourth time, by diverting the river, made safe navigation of the Danube for the danger of the cataracts.
EDCS-10000490.summary: This inscription records a public works project by Emperor Trajan to improve Danube navigation.

EDCS-10000491.text_edition: \n Genio sancto paterno Daciarum Antonius Maximus |(centurio) legionis princeps praetorii voto libenter soluto\n
EDCS-10000491.translation: To the sacred ancestral Genius of Dacia, Antonius Maximus, centurion of the principal legion, happily fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000491.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the Genius of Dacia by a centurion named Antonius Maximus.

EDCS-10000494.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Crescens vixit annis XXXV PTE \n
EDCS-10000494.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Titus Flavius Crescens lived 35 years.
EDCS-10000494.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the age at death of Titus Flavius Crescens.

EDCS-10000496.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Crispo veterano patri et Sallustiae Epictuni matri et Ulpiis Caio et Herculiano fratribus hic siti sunt Marcus Ulpius Serulenus bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-10000496.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Marcus Ulpius Crispus, veteran, father, and Sallustia Epictunia, mother, and Ulpius Caius and Ulpius Herculianus, brothers, are buried here; Marcus Ulpius Serulenus placed this for them who deserved well.
EDCS-10000496.summary: This is a family tomb inscription set up by Marcus Ulpius Serulenus for his deceased parents and brothers.

EDCS-10000503.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Claudius Diogenes hastatus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10000503.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, Claudius Diogenes, hastatus of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, willingly fulfilled his deserved vow.
EDCS-10000503.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Mithras by Claudius Diogenes, a soldier in the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion.

EDCS-10000504.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Valerius Liberalis legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10000504.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, Valerius Liberalis, of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, willingly fulfilled his deserved vow.
EDCS-10000504.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Mithras by Valerius Liberalis, a soldier in the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion.

EDCS-10000505.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Mercatori veterano ex imaginifero legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos L et Aurelio Marciano militi legionis eiusdem vixit nnos XXX militavit nnos V Titus Aurelius Quintus veteranus legionis eiusdem hic positi sunt\n
EDCS-10000505.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Titus Aurelius Mercator, a veteran, formerly an imaginifer of the VII Claudia legion, lived 50 years, and Aurelius Marcianus, a soldier of the same legion, lived 30 years and served 5 years; Titus Aurelius Quintus, a veteran of the same legion, are buried here.
EDCS-10000505.summary: This tombstone inscription records the burial of two men, a veteran and a soldier, from the VII Claudia legion.

EDCS-10000506.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Calliope ancillae Luci P Gaiani |(centurionis) legionis IIII Flaviae Lucius P Huacinthus sic coniugi dulcissimae monumentum posuit vixit annos XVIII\n
EDCS-10000506.translation: To the spirits of the departed, to Calliope, slave of Lucius P. Gaianus, centurion of the IIII Flavia legion; Lucius P. Huacinthus placed this monument for his most beloved wife, who lived 18 years.
EDCS-10000506.summary: This tombstone inscription is dedicated by Lucius P. Huacinthus to his deceased wife, Calliope, who was a slave of a centurion in the IIII Flavia legion.

EDCS-10000507.text_edition: \n IDAVNI Apollini Q S Dianae Silvano Aelius Valentinus IIvir coloniae votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-10000507.translation: To the god Iavolus, to Apollo, to Diana, to Silvanus, Aelius Valentinus, duovir of the colony, willingly placed his vow.
EDCS-10000507.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aelius Valentinus, a local magistrate, to several deities.

EDCS-10000508.text_edition: \n CC AA Alexander et M-- quinquennalis collegii I\n
EDCS-10000508.translation: Alexander and M---, quinquennales of the guild...
EDCS-10000508.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records officials of a collegium (guild).

EDCS-10000509.summary: This inscription records a gift given by T. Epagathus, likely to a deity or a public building.

EDCS-10000510.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Maximus to his father, also named Maximus.

EDCS-10000517.text_edition: \n Invicto et super omnes indulgentissimo Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano nobilissimo principi dedicante Titieno Vero viro egregio procuratori mmetallorum DDardanorum ordo colonorum devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-10000517.translation: Dedicated to the Invincible and most indulgent Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus, most noble prince, by Titienus Verus, distinguished procurator of the metal mines of the Dardani, the council of colonists devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-10000517.summary: This inscription is a dedication from the colonists of the Dardani province to Emperor Gordian III, presented by their procurator Titienus Verus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10000518.text_edition: \n IN PI tribunicia potestate imperatori consuli III patri patriae Amandus Augusti libertus procurator patrono indulgentissimo sua pecunia posuit\n
EDCS-10000518.translation: Amandus, freedman of the Emperor, procurator, to his most indulgent patron, erected this at his own expense.
EDCS-10000518.summary: This inscription records a monument erected at his own expense by Amandus, an imperial freedman and procurator, to his patron, likely an emperor or high official, and dates to the imperial period.

EDCS-10000519.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britnnicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIIII imperator III consul IIII pater patriae proconsul temporibus suis TIVS TAT \n
EDCS-10000519.translation: Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of Parthia, conqueror of Britain, conqueror of Germany, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 19th time, Imperator for the 3rd time, Consul for the 4th time, father of the country, proconsul, in his own times, TIVS TAT...
EDCS-10000519.summary: This inscription is a lengthy titulature of Emperor Caracalla, listing his imperial titles and powers, with an incomplete continuation, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10000520.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning Antoninus Augustus, possibly referring to Elagabalus or Caracalla, from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10000523.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titiae Tigridis vixit nnos XXXV Sempronii \n
EDCS-10000523.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Titiia Tigridis lived 35 years. Sempronius...
EDCS-10000523.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Titiia Tigridis at the age of 35, dedicated by Sempronius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000524.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Catti Lam vixit annos RAM \n
EDCS-10000524.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Catti Lam lived ... years...
EDCS-10000524.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription honoring Catti Lam, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000526.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Risco Abudi Dia...
EDCS-10000526.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Risco Abudi Dia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000527.text_edition: \n Aurelio liberto vixit annos defuncto DI AV VVA VI VGII HANO posuit\n
EDCS-10000527.translation: Aurelius, freedman, lived ... years. DI AV VVA VI VGII HANO erected this...
EDCS-10000527.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the life of Aurelius, a freedman, and mentioning the person who erected the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000529.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus lis Auggustorum v<e=B>rna L vixit annos XXXV mana coniugi bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-10000529.translation: To the Divine Shades. Verna lived 35 years. Her husband placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-10000529.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Verna, set up by her husband, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000539.text_edition: \n et Silvano sacrum Marcus Aurelius Silvester decurio quinqennalis votum solvit merito Maximo et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-10000539.translation: Sacred to Silvanus. Marcus Aurelius Silvester, a quinquennial decurion, fulfilled his vow, during the consulship of Maximus and Aelianus.
EDCS-10000539.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Silvanus by Marcus Aurelius Silvester, a decurion, dated to the consulship of Maximus and Aelianus (likely 2nd century AD).

EDCS-10000540.text_edition: \n Publius Licinius Publi filius Papiria Aelianus decurio municipiorum Ulpiani et Aeli Viminacii et Aeliani et Ulpia Cassa eius porticum incendio consumptam sua pecunia restituerunt\n
EDCS-10000540.translation: Publius Licinius Aelianus, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, a decurion of the municipia of Ulpiana and Aelius Viminacium, and Aelianus and Ulpia Cassa, his wife, restored with their own money the portico which had been destroyed by fire.
EDCS-10000540.summary: This is a dedication recording the rebuilding of a portico by Publius Licinius Aelianus and his wife, Ulpia Cassa, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-10000545.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Martinus Septimius sonius icanus ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-10000545.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Martinus Septimius sonius icanus erected this according to their vow.
EDCS-10000545.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Martinus Septimius sonius icanus, likely set up in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000546.text_edition: \n Fortunae Dominae Aurelius Cas decurio posuit\n
EDCS-10000546.translation: To Fortuna Domina. Aurelius Cas, a decurion, placed this.
EDCS-10000546.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Aurelius Cas, a decurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000547.translation: Sacred to the god Silvanus. Rufus [dedicated this].
EDCS-10000547.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Silvanus by Rufus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000548.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimius Dassius veteranus cohortis primae Montanorum et Aelia Nanea coniux eius et Aeliae Celsinae quae vixit annis XXX et Aeliae Romanae quae vixit annis XXV filiabus pientissimis et sibi vivi faciendum curarunt et Annio Timenti adfini nostro\n
EDCS-10000548.translation: To the Divine Shades. Septimius Dassius, a veteran of the First Cohort of Montani, and his wife Aelia Nanea, and for their most devoted daughters Aelia Celsina, who lived 30 years, and Aelia Romana, who lived 25 years, they arranged for this to be made while alive, and for our relative Annius Timentus.
EDCS-10000548.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Septimius Dassius and his wife for themselves, their two daughters, and a relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000550.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae sacrum Publius Petilius Mercator decurio coloniae IIvir\n
EDCS-10000550.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Augusta. Publius Petilius Mercator, decurion and duumvir of the colony, [dedicated this].
EDCS-10000550.summary: This is a votive dedication to Nemesis Augusta by Publius Petilius Mercator, a decurion and duumvir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000553.text_edition: \n Manius Epidius Mani filius Vicetia Pudens Menenia vixit nnos LX militavit nnos XXX Charitio Voconia coniux coniugi hic situs est\n
EDCS-10000553.translation: Manius Epidius Pudens, son of Manius, from Vicetia, of the Menenia tribe, lived 60 years, served 30 years. Charitio Voconia, his wife, arranged for her husband to be buried here.
EDCS-10000553.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Manius Epidius Pudens, a soldier, set up by his wife Charitio Voconia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000555.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atilius Eucharistus Atiliae Veneriae uxori suae bene merenti posuit hic sita est\n
EDCS-10000555.translation: To the Divine Shades. Atilius Eucharistus placed this for his well-deserving wife, Atilia Veneria. She is buried here.
EDCS-10000555.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Atilia Veneria, set up by her husband Atilius Eucharistus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000558.text_edition: \n Iulia Severae liberta Tyche vixit annis L hic sita est\n
EDCS-10000558.translation: Tyche, freedwoman of Iulia Severa, lived for 50 years and is buried here.
EDCS-10000558.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tyche, a freedwoman, recording her age at death.

EDCS-10000559.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Valens vixit annos XVII et Caius Valerius Maximus vixit annos XVIII hic siti sunt Caius Valerius Lucius pater et Caelia Veneria mater filis at sic spem vitae studiis perductis in qua fortuna et fato deceptis faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-10000559.translation: Gaius Valerius Valens lived for 17 years, and Gaius Valerius Maximus lived for 18 years, and they are buried here. Their father Gaius Valerius Lucius and mother Caelia Veneria had this built for their sons, who were led astray by hopes of life and studies, and deceived by fortune and fate.
EDCS-10000559.summary: This funerary epitaph records the deaths of two sons, Valens and Maximus, with their parents commissioning the monument.

EDCS-10000561.text_edition: \n Mesta Didae Sitae filia vixit nns XX hic sita est Lucius Dicconis filius coniunx et Dida Sitae pater et Mescena mater et Sita Mesti avius et Mania avia piissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-10000561.translation: Didia Sita, daughter of Mesta, lived for 20 years and is buried here. Her husband Lucius, son of Dicconis, and her father Dida Sita, and her mother Mescena, and her grandfather Sita Mesti, and her grandmother Mania, had this built for the most devoted woman.
EDCS-10000561.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Didia Sita, listing her age and the family members who commissioned the monument.

EDCS-10000568.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Claudius Alexander et Petilia Bassilla ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-10000568.translation: Claudius Alexander and Petilia Bassilla set this up in fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina.
EDCS-10000568.summary: This is a dedication of a religious monument to Jupiter and Juno by Claudius Alexander and Petilia Bassilla in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10000572.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Rufus vixit nnos LXV hic situs est Claudia Licinia coniux et Iulii Rufus et Rufinus patri bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-10000572.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Julius Rufus lived for 65 years and is buried here. His wife Claudia Licinia and his sons, Rufus and Rufinus, made this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-10000572.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Gaius Julius Rufus, noting his age and that his wife and sons commissioned the monument.

EDCS-10000577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabino Antio |(centurioni) cohortis I Euporus et Fortunata patrono bene merenti posuerunt vixit annos XXXV\n
EDCS-10000577.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Euporus and Fortunata set this up for their well-deserving patron Sabinus, a centurion of the First Cohort. He lived for 35 years.
EDCS-10000577.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Sabinus, a centurion, commissioned by Euporus and Fortunata, and records his age at death.

EDCS-10000578.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Clemens vixit nnos II hic situs est sit tibi terra levis fui quod tu es eris quod ego sum Rufria Amabilis fratri karissimo suo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10000578.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius Clemens lived for 2 years and is buried here. 'May the earth lie lightly on you. What you are, I shall be; what I am, you are.' His sister Rufria Amabilis had this made for her dearest brother.
EDCS-10000578.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young boy named Ulpius Clemens, with an inscription expressing a common sentiment about mortality and commissioned by his sister.

EDCS-10000580.text_edition: \n annos LX ice coniux etetelix et Herculanus fili et Zosimus lib(ertus) et con<h=I>eres monumentum posuerunt hic situs est \n
EDCS-10000580.translation: His wife and sons, Etelix and Herculanus, and his freedman Zosimus, and their associates, erected this monument. He lived for 60 years and is buried here.
EDCS-10000580.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of an unnamed individual who lived for 60 years, commissioned by his wife, sons, and freedman.

EDCS-10000589.text_edition: \n IC Marg l Valenus domo a |(centurio) legionis \n
EDCS-10000589.summary: This inscription fragment appears to record the origin and rank of a military man named Valenus, likely part of a larger epitaph or dedication.

EDCS-10000590.translation: Melior fulfilled her vow to Attis.
EDCS-10000590.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Melior to the god Attis.

EDCS-10000591.text_edition: \n Deo Mundryto Claudius Rufus votum solvit\n
EDCS-10000591.translation: Claudius Rufus fulfilled his vow to the god Mundrytus.
EDCS-10000591.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Claudius Rufus to the god Mundrytus, likely a local deity, possibly dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10000593.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Flavius Felix veteranus legionis VII Claudiae \n
EDCS-10000593.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus: Caius Flavius Felix, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia.
EDCS-10000593.summary: This is a sacred dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Flavius Felix, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000596.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Valerius veteranus cohortis I Cretum domo Hierapytna vixit annos LX emeruit annos XXXII \n
EDCS-10000596.translation: Tiberius Claudius Valerius, a veteran of the Cohors I Cretum from Hierapytna, lived for 60 years and served for 32 years.
EDCS-10000596.summary: This epitaph records the life and military service of Tiberius Claudius Valerius, a veteran of Cohors I Cretum from Hierapytna, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000598.text_edition: \n Auarelius Firmus pro salute sua Herculi posuit\n
EDCS-10000598.translation: Aurelius Firmus erected this for his own well-being to Hercules.
EDCS-10000598.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Firmus dedicating a monument to Hercules for his personal well-being, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000600.translation: Aelius Saturninus gave this gift to Omphale.
EDCS-10000600.summary: This inscription records Aelius Saturninus presenting a gift to the deity Omphale, probably dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000603.text_edition: \n Flavius Lucius et Fla<vi>us Lucillus Deo Sancto Laeto Lib<e>ro Patri pro salutes sic suas sic et vicanorum posuerunt\n
EDCS-10000603.translation: Flavius Lucius and Flavius Lucillus erected this for the well-being of themselves and of the villagers to the Holy God Laetus Liber Pater.
EDCS-10000603.summary: This dedication, made by Flavius Lucius and Flavius Lucillus, is to the god Laetus Liber Pater for the health of themselves and the villagers, likely dating from the late Roman period.

EDCS-10000605.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani nepos divi Nervae pronepos pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul II milia passuum II\n
EDCS-10000605.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time, [built/maintained] 2 Roman miles.
EDCS-10000605.summary: This milestone inscription records public works completed by Emperor Antoninus Pius during his second consulship and second tribunician power, dating to 139 AD.

EDCS-10000607.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aemilio Aemiliano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli milia passuum XIII\n
EDCS-10000607.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, [built/maintained] 13 Roman miles.
EDCS-10000607.summary: This milestone inscription records public works completed under the authority of Emperor Aemilianus, likely dating to his short reign in 253 AD.

EDCS-10100168.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpio Attico et Ulpiae Ianuariae et Ulpio Thalaso Pasadensis uxor marito et filiae et naepoti sic bene meritis posuit\n
EDCS-10100168.translation: To the departed spirits: Pasadensis erected this for Ulpius Atticus, Ulpia Ianuaria, and Ulpius Thallasus, his wife, daughter, and grandson, who deserved well.
EDCS-10100168.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Pasadensis to his well-deserving family members, Ulpius Atticus, Ulpia Ianuaria, and Ulpius Thallasus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100169.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Iucunda vixit annos XL et Super filius ipsius eius sic vixit nnos XX Flavius Terentinus coniugi et filio posuit\n
EDCS-10100169.translation: To the departed spirits: Flavia Iucunda lived for 40 years and Super, her son, lived for 20 years. Flavius Terentinus erected this for his wife and son.
EDCS-10100169.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Flavia Iucunda and her son Super, and was erected by Flavius Terentinus, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100315.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Aureli vixit annos LX \n
EDCS-10100315.translation: To the departed spirits of Marcus Aurelius, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-10100315.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-10100319.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Valerius Iucundus ex voto posuit Perpetuo et Corne<l=T>iano consulibus \n
EDCS-10100319.translation: Valerius Iucundus set this up for Sol Invictus, in fulfillment of a vow, during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus.
EDCS-10100319.summary: A dedication to the god Sol Invictus made by Valerius Iucundus in fulfillment of a vow, dated by consuls.

EDCS-10100399.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly a funerary monument, mentioning Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-10100400.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus P(ublius) Prusius Maximus vixit annos XLV Iul(ius) Hieronimus secundus heres et Ulpius Maxumus sic iuxta voluntatem testamenti pecunia sua\n
EDCS-10100400.translation: To the departed spirits of Publius Prusius Maximus, who lived 45 years. Julius Hieronimus secundus, the heir, and Ulpius Maximus, according to the will, with their own money.
EDCS-10100400.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Prusius Maximus, stating his age and the fulfillment of his will by his heirs.

EDCS-10100401.text_edition: \n ------ an]nos LXV gener socro et socrae bene merentibus memoriam ponit\n
EDCS-10100401.translation: [---] for 65 years, the son-in-law sets up this memorial for his deserving father-in-law and mother-in-law.
EDCS-10100401.summary: A funerary inscription where a son-in-law commemorates his parents-in-law, specifying their age at death.

EDCS-10100402.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-10100402.text_edition: \n Dassianus senior et Galla posueruerunt titulo sic pueri sic bimato Dassiani sic iuniori Dassianus iunior Christi electus neofitus\n
EDCS-10100402.translation: Dassianus the elder and Galla set up this monument, as the boy, as the two-year-old of Dassianus, as the younger Dassianus, an elected neophyte of Christ.
EDCS-10100402.summary: A funerary inscription for children from the Dassianus family, including one who was a neophyte Christian.

EDCS-10100405.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Ulpius Antonius Quintus decurio aedilicius quaestor municipii Diernensium ob honorem quinquennalitatis portus \n
EDCS-10100405.translation: To Juno Regina, Ulpius Antonius Quintus, a decurion, former aedile, quaestor of the municipality of Diernenses, for the honor of the quinquennial census of the port.
EDCS-10100405.summary: A dedication to Juno Regina by Ulpius Antonius Quintus for achievements in local government and for an honor related to a quinquennial census.

EDCS-10100407.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Ingenuo veterano cohortis III Campestris vixit annos L Noviia Cassia coniugi virginio pientissimo et sibi fecit vol\n
EDCS-10100407.translation: To the departed spirits of Iulius Ingenuus, a veteran of the III Cohort Campestris, who lived 50 years. Noviia Cassia made this for her most devoted husband and for herself.
EDCS-10100407.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulius Ingenuus, a veteran, stating his age and that it was made by his wife for both of them.

EDCS-10100408.text_edition: \n ob salutem Titus Aelius Respectus Oescenses sic votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-10100408.translation: For the health of Titus Aelius Respectus of Oescus, he willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100408.summary: A dedication recording Titus Aelius Respectus fulfilling a vow for his well-being.

EDCS-10100411.translation: To the departed spirits of Caius [---]
EDCS-10100411.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Caius.

EDCS-10100414.text_edition: \n ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus coloniae Flaviae Scupinorum et municipii splendidissimi Ulpianorum filio piissimo loco dato decreto coloniae\n
EDCS-10100414.translation: To his most devoted son, decorated with the curial ornaments of the colony of Flavia Scupinorum and of the most splendid municipality of Ulpiani, a place of honour granted by decree of the colony.
EDCS-10100414.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument honoring a devoted son with civic honors, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100415.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Iucundus hic situs vixit nnos III dies VIII Aelius Felix pater et Co Marcellina mater\n
EDCS-10100415.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelius Iucundus is buried here. He lived 3 years and 8 days. His father Aelius Felix and mother Marcellina had this set up.
EDCS-10100415.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of a young child, Aelius Iucundus, and erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100416.text_edition: \n Herculano vixit nnos VIIII hic siti sunt Aelia Hesperis mater filiis piissimis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10100416.translation: Herculanus lived 9 years. Aelia Hesperis, his mother, had this set up for her most devoted sons.
EDCS-10100416.summary: This tombstone records the death of Herculanus and was erected by his mother, Aelia Hesperis, for her devoted sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Annius Cai libertus Ursio vixit annis XXXXV Annia Fortunata coniugi bene merito fecit\n
EDCS-10100418.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Annius Ursio, freedman of Caius, lived 45 years. Annia Fortunata dedicated this to her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-10100418.summary: This tombstone records the death of Caius Annius Ursio and was dedicated by his wife, Annia Fortunata, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100419.text_edition: \n hic sita est et Aureliae Naneae Aurelius Maximinus filiae bene merenti et coniugi vivae posuit\n
EDCS-10100419.translation: Aurelia Nanea is buried here. Aurelius Maximinus placed this for his well-deserving daughter and for his wife, while he was alive.
EDCS-10100419.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelia Nanea, her father Aurelius Maximinus, and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100426.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo sacrum ex viso Marcus Ulpius Viator beneficiarius consularis ex frumentario\n
EDCS-10100426.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, by vision. Marcus Ulpius Viator, a beneficiarius consularis, formerly a frumentarius.
EDCS-10100426.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Ulpius Viator, a military official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100427.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae sacrum Aurelius Saevinus Proculus beneficiarius consularis votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-10100427.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva. Aurelius Saevinus Proculus, a beneficiarius consularis, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-10100427.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva by Aurelius Saevinus Proculus, a military official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100428.text_edition: \n Nemesi reginae sacrum pro salutem sic dominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Caius Valerius Valens beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Flaviae votum libens laetus merito posuit\n
EDCS-10100428.translation: Sacred to Queen Nemesis for the health of our lords the Augusti. Caius Valerius Valens, a beneficiarius consularis of Legio IIII Flavia, willingly and happily set up this vow as deserved.
EDCS-10100428.summary: This votive inscription to Nemesis was dedicated by Caius Valerius Valens, a military official of Legio IIII Flavia, for the welfare of the emperors, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100429.text_edition: \n Nemesi reginae sacrum Ulpius Martialis beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Flaviae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10100429.translation: Sacred to Queen Nemesis. Ulpius Martialis, a beneficiarius consularis of Legio IIII Flavia, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-10100429.summary: This votive inscription to Nemesis was dedicated by Ulpius Martialis, a military official of Legio IIII Flavia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100430.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Bulbita hic sita est Lucius O Tr coniugi bene merenti faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-10100430.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Bulbita is buried here. Lucius O. Tr. had this set up for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-10100430.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Claudia Bulbita and was erected by her husband, Lucius O. Tr., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100431.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Capitolino Titus Aelius Avitus decurio municipii Ulpianorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10100431.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest of the Capitol, Titus Aelius Avitus, a councilman of the town of Ulpiani, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100431.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Aelius Avitus, a councilman of the Roman town of Ulpiani.

EDCS-10100432.text_edition: \n Have mihi domina et dulcissima kara sic coniunx amantissima? --- \n
EDCS-10100432.translation: Greetings to me, my mistress and sweetest, dearest, most loving wife?
EDCS-10100432.summary: This inscription is a fragmented personal dedication, likely to a deceased loved one, possibly a wife.

EDCS-10100433.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valens Turelli vixit annos LXXX Publius Aelius Turellius nepos et heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10100433.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valens Turelli lived 80 years. Publius Aelius Turellius, grandson and heir, placed this for one well-deserving.
EDCS-10100433.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valens Turelli, erected by his grandson and heir, Publius Aelius Turellius.

EDCS-10100435.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Z Aelius Cosinus decurio libens posuit\n
EDCS-10100435.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aelius Cosinus, a councilman, willingly placed this.
EDCS-10100435.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aelius Cosinus, a councilman.

EDCS-10100436.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Varius Crescens \n
EDCS-10100436.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Varius Crescens.
EDCS-10100436.summary: This is a simple dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Varius Crescens.

EDCS-10100438.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque Marcus Aurelius Marcianus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae Severianae Alexandrianae votum libens persolvit Agricola et Clementino consulibus\n
EDCS-10100438.translation: To the gods and goddesses, Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, a staff officer of the rank of beneficiarius consularis of the Seventh Claudian Legion Severiana Alexandriana, willingly paid his vow, during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.
EDCS-10100438.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to the gods and goddesses by Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, a military officer in the Seventh Claudian Legion, during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.

EDCS-10100440.text_edition: \n Deabus sacrum virgines sic Gaudens Liviae servus pro salute pecunia sua et suis\n
EDCS-10100440.translation: Sacred to the goddesses. Gaudens, slave of Livia, for [her] health and that of [his] family, with his own money.
EDCS-10100440.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication made by Gaudens, a slave of Livia, for health.

EDCS-10100441.text_edition: \n ni Ulpia Proculiana vixit annis XXX Lucius Avillius Pudens filius parentibus pientissimis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-10100441.translation: Ulpia Proculiana lived 30 years. Lucius Avillius Pudens, her son, arranged for this to be made for the most devoted parents.
EDCS-10100441.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ulpia Proculiana, who lived 30 years, and was likely commissioned by her son, Lucius Avillius Pudens, for his parents.

EDCS-10100442.translation: Marcus Ulpius, councilman of the town, aedile [---
EDCS-10100442.summary: This fragmentary inscription begins to record the career of Marcus Ulpius, a councilman and aedile of a Roman town.

EDCS-10100444.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Marullus vixit annos LXXV Caius Valerius Varanus et Nanea Galli sibi vive sic faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-10100444.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Valerius Marullus lived 75 years. Caius Valerius Varanus and Nanea Galli arranged for this to be made for themselves while living.
EDCS-10100444.summary: This funerary inscription records Caius Valerius Marullus, who lived 75 years, and was prepared for himself and Nanea Galli while they were alive.

EDCS-10100445.text_edition: \n cum qua felicem vixit vitam annis XXX Caius Avillius Pudens filius parentibus pientissimis faciendum curavit t Ulpiano hoc sepulcrum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-10100445.translation: Caius Avillius Pudens, son, had this tomb built for his most devoted parents, with whom he lived a happy life for 30 years. It will not pass to an heir, Ulpianus.
EDCS-10100445.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the construction of a tomb by Caius Avillius Pudens for his parents.

EDCS-10100446.text_edition: \n Genio Illyrici pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti et Mamaeae Augustae sub cura Antoni Silvani viri egregii procuratoris Iulianus vilicus stationis Ulpianensis tabularius sumptu suo <fe=IL>cit Albino et Maximo consulibus \n
EDCS-10100446.translation: For the Genius of Illyricum, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus and of Augusta Mamaea, Julianus, a steward of the Ulpianensis station and a clerk, under the supervision of Antonius Silvanus, an excellent man, had this made at his own expense, with Albinus and Maximus as consuls.
EDCS-10100446.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument to the Genius of Illyricum for the imperial family's safety, commissioned by a steward at the Ulpianensis station.

EDCS-10100447.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabusque omnibus Marcus Cocceius Eros ex voto fecit\n
EDCS-10100447.translation: Marcus Cocceius Eros made this from a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest and to all the gods and goddesses.
EDCS-10100447.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Cocceius Eros dedicated to Jupiter and all the gods.

EDCS-10100448.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatorum Augustorum Victor Demetri et Demetrius Ambibi sacerdotes eiusdem dei solverunt votum\n
EDCS-10100448.translation: Victor Demetrius and Demetrius Ambibius, priests of the same god, paid their vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus for the safety of our lords Emperors Augusti.
EDCS-10100448.summary: This is a votive inscription by priests of Jupiter Dolichenus for the safety of the emperors.

EDCS-10100449.text_edition: \n Furiae Titi filiae Varanillae clarissimae feminae sanctissimus ordo municipii Ulpiani ob multa eius bene merita basim et statuam vexatam Furia Caecilia clarissima femina restituit\n
EDCS-10100449.translation: Furia Caecilia, a woman of the highest rank, restored the damaged base and statue for Furia Varanilla, daughter of Titus, a woman of the highest rank, because of her many good deeds, by order of the most sacred municipal council of Ulpianus.
EDCS-10100449.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a base and statue for Furia Varanilla by Furia Caecilia, commissioned by the municipal council of Ulpianus due to her meritorious deeds.

EDCS-10100450.text_edition: \n Marco Pontio Marci filio Pupinia Varano Sabino viro clarissimo tribuno legionis III Augustae quaestori tribuno plebis sanctissimus ordo municipii Ulpianensium ob multa in rem publicam eius merita basim et statuam vextatam Furia Caecilia clarissima femina restituit\n
EDCS-10100450.translation: Furia Caecilia, a woman of the highest rank, restored the damaged base and statue for Marcus Pontius Varus Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Pupinia tribe, a man of the highest rank, tribune of the III Augusta legion, quaestor, tribune of the plebs, because of his many merits to the state, by order of the most sacred municipal council of Ulpianensium.
EDCS-10100450.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a base and statue for Marcus Pontius Varus Sabinus by Furia Caecilia, commissioned by the municipal council of Ulpianensium due to his public service.

EDCS-10100452.summary: This is a short dedication by Celer, who held the office of duumvir.

EDCS-10100453.text_edition: \n Caius Aelius Dasi filius vixit annos CXX Aeli Iustus et Vanno patri pientissimo \n
EDCS-10100453.translation: Caius Aelius Dasi filius lived 120 years. Aelius Iustus and Vanno [erected this] for their most devoted father.
EDCS-10100453.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the long life of Caius Aelius Dasi filius and its erection by his sons.

EDCS-10100457.text_edition: \n Zbeltiurdi Sextius Ba-pecus optio pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10100457.translation: Zbeltiurdus Sextius Bapecus, a optio, gladly and deservedly paid his vow for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-10100457.summary: This is a votive inscription by an optio named Zbeltiurdus Sextius Bapecus for his well-being and that of his family.

EDCS-10100458.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Valentinus vixit annis XV Ulpius Andinus et Aurelia Iulia parentes filio pientissimo et sibi vivis merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-10100458.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Ulpius Valentinus lived 15 years. Ulpius Andinus and Aurelia Iulia, parents, placed this for their most devoted son and for themselves while living and deserving.
EDCS-10100458.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Ulpius Valentinus at a young age and the monument erected by his parents for him and themselves.

EDCS-10100459.text_edition: \n Nymphis Lucius Titovius Pro votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-10100459.translation: To the Nymphs, Lucius Titovius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100459.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the Nymphs by Lucius Titovius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100460.text_edition: \n et Sitae filio Sumi corniculario cohortis primae Dardanorum vixit annos XXXX bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10100460.translation: And to Sitae, his son, a hornblower of the first cohort of Dardanians; he lived 40 years. He set this up for one who deserved well.
EDCS-10100460.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for a hornblower of the first cohort of Dardanians, who lived 40 years, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100461.summary: This is a dedication to the god Serapis Invictus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100462.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valens Timentis nnorum XXXV sibi et Cittuni suae superstiti bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10100462.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valens, son of Timentis, aged 35 years, set this up for himself and for his surviving wife, Cittunia, who deserved well.
EDCS-10100462.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valens, who lived 35 years, set up by himself and his wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100463.text_edition: \n Asclepio Telesphoro Hygiae So Heracliti Surus et pro Genio Dolicheni paterno Deo et Genio cohortis votum libies sic fecit\n
EDCS-10100463.translation: To Asclepius, Telesphorus, and Hygieia. Surus, son of So Heraclitus, also willingly made this vow for the paternal Genius of Dolichenus and for the Genius of the cohort.
EDCS-10100463.summary: This inscription records a vow made by Surus to Asclepius, Telesphorus, Hygieia, and the Genii of his cohort and his paternal god, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100465.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimia Pitta vixit nnos XL Dunnus pater filiae carae posuit\n
EDCS-10100465.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Septimia Pitta lived 40 years. Dunnus, her father, set this up for his dear daughter.
EDCS-10100465.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Septimia Pitta, who lived 40 years, and was set up by her father, Dunnus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100466.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Silvio Cai Praeceptori qui vixit et Andiae matri vivae posuerunt Lucius Silvius Italicus filius cum coheredibus\n
EDCS-10100466.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Silvius Italicus, the son, with his co-heirs, set this up for Lucius Silvius Caius, his teacher, who also lived for his mother Andia, while she was alive.
EDCS-10100466.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Lucius Silvius Caius, a teacher, by his son Lucius Silvius Italicus and co-heirs, and also mentions his mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100469.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Ingenuus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-10100469.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Ingenuus, a deputy officer of the consular legate of the Seventh Claudian Legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100469.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Ingenuus, a deputy officer of the Seventh Claudian Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100470.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Cassius Ulpi Aeli liberto vixit nnos XXVI et matri Cinna filio et uxori se vivo posuit\n
EDCS-10100470.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius Cassius set this up for his freedman Ulpius Aelius, who lived 26 years, and for his mother Cinna, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-10100470.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Ulpius Aelius (a freedman), his mother Cinna, and the dedicator Ulpius Cassius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100471.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the healing deities Asclepius, Hygieia, and Thelesphorus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100472.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aper milis sic exercitus vixit annis XXXV militavit annis XIII Ursus cuntubernalis sic cuntubernali sic bene merito titulum posuit\n
EDCS-10100472.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aper the soldier lived for 35 years, served for 13 years. Ursus, his tent-mate, erected this monument to his well-deserving tent-mate.
EDCS-10100472.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a soldier named Aper, erected by his tent-mate Ursus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100473.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Maurus Kandido sic karo sic filio suo et sibi et coniugi\n
EDCS-10100473.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aelius Maurus to his dear son Kandido, and for himself and his wife.
EDCS-10100473.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Kandido by his father Aelius Maurus, who also provided for himself and his wife, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100474.text_edition: \n Aelius Niceforus libertus ipse sibi et Baebiae PS coniugi \n
EDCS-10100474.translation: Aelius Niceforus, freedman, for himself and his wife Baebia PS.
EDCS-10100474.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aelius Niceforus for himself and his wife Baebia, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100475.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Rufinus Dassi pr et Andiae Dassi coniugi memoriam sibi vivi posuerunt sta dum venio memoria superis vixit nnos LX\n
EDCS-10100475.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Rufinus and his wife Andia, honored senators, erected this memorial for themselves while alive. 'Wait until I come' (a common phrase indicating one's own future death). Lived 60 years.
EDCS-10100475.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rufinus and Andia, a married couple, who set it up for themselves while alive and record Rufinus' age at death, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100476.translation: Ulpius Aper erected this for himself while alive.
EDCS-10100476.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Ulpius Aper for himself during his lifetime, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100480.text_edition: \n Mercurio Marcus Ulpius Severianus libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-10100480.translation: To Mercury. Marcus Ulpius Severianus erected this willingly, as deserved.
EDCS-10100480.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mercury by Marcus Ulpius Severianus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100487.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli \n
EDCS-10100487.translation: For the Emperor, Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix, unconquered Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, consul.
EDCS-10100487.summary: This is a formal dedication to Emperor Trajan Decius, listing his titles and powers, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-10100579.text_edition: \n Quod pater optabat fieri sibi quod pia mater infelices sic nati sic fecit uterque parentum Dis Manibus Aurelio Titiano defuncto annorum et Aurelio Iustiano defuncto annorum et Aurelio Iusto defuncto annorum filiis pientissimis et Suro alumno Aurelius Titus et Aurelia Martilla parentes et sibi vivis posuerunt\n
EDCS-10100579.translation: What the father wished to happen for himself, what the devoted mother did for her unfortunate sons. Aurelius Titus and Aurelia Martilla, the parents, and for themselves while alive, erected this to the Divine Spirits for their most devoted sons Aurelius Titianus, Aurelius Iustianus, and Aurelius Iustus, and for their foster-son Suro.
EDCS-10100579.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Titus and Aurelia Martilla for their deceased sons and foster-son, and also for themselves, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-10100580.text_edition: \n Aurelio Victorino coniugi et Aureliae Victorinae filiae et Aurelio Vindici genero et Iulio Vindiciano nepoti Aurelia Atilia bbene mmerentibus et sibi viva posuit\n
EDCS-10100580.translation: Aurelia Atilia erected this for her husband Aurelius Victorinus, her daughter Aurelia Victorina, her son-in-law Aurelius Vindicius, and her grandson Iulius Vindicianus, who deserved well, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-10100580.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelia Atilia for her family members and herself, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100581.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Baebiae meae matri et filiae Titiae nnrum V Baebius Titianus\n
EDCS-10100581.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For my mother Baebia and for my daughter Titia, aged 5. Baebius Titianus.
EDCS-10100581.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Baebius Titianus to his mother Baebia and his daughter Titia, who died at the age of five, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100582.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Manlio Pontio Valenti Iulia Itache sic coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10100582.translation: To the Divine Manes. Iulia Itache set this up for her deserving husband, Manlius Pontius Valens.
EDCS-10100582.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10200191.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Antonius Gaius pro se et suis ponit Imperatore Severo Alexan[dro] III consule\n
EDCS-10200191.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Antonius Gaius sets this up for himself and his family, during the consulship of Emperor Severus Alexander.
EDCS-10200191.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Antonius Gaius, likely during the reign of Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-10200192.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Bito militi legionis IIII Flaviae s<t>ipendiorum XVIII titulum fecit Titus Valerius Valentinus custos armorum legionis eiusdem haeres sic fecit Bito supra scripto vixit annos XXXVIII bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-10200192.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Valerius Valentinus, armor keeper of the same legion, his heir, set up this monument for Marcus Aurelius Bito, a soldier of Legio IIII Flavia, with 18 years of service. Bito, above mentioned, lived 38 years. He set this up for the deserving.
EDCS-10200192.summary: A funerary monument for a soldier, Marcus Aurelius Bito, erected by his heir and colleague, Titus Valerius Valentinus, of Legio IIII Flavia.

EDCS-10200193.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Glyconiano |(centurioni) legionis Claudiae ex decimipilo legionis IIII Flaviae stipendiorum LII vixit nnos LXX Aelia Severina coniux cum filio sibi et coniugi amantissimo posuit\n
EDCS-10200193.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia Severina set this up with her son for herself and her most beloved husband, Marcus Aurelius Glyconianus, a centurion of Legio Claudia, from the rank of decurio of Legio IIII Flavia, with 52 years of service. He lived 70 years.
EDCS-10200193.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Glyconianus, a centurion and decurio, erected by his wife and son.

EDCS-10200194.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Ampelius, tribune of the Batavorum Cohort, with his son Ampelianus, have offered this vow for the dedication of the house and for their own health and that of all their family.
EDCS-10200194.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by a military tribune and his son, commemorating the dedication of a house and for their well-being.

EDCS-10701875.summary: An inscription that appears to be part of a larger text, likely recording an action or dedication by Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-10701876.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae Marcus Cocceius Valens decurio Augustalis coloniae pro salute sua et Polittae coniugis et Cocceiorum filiorum votum solvit\n
