EDCS-10701876.translation: Marcus Cocceius Valens, decurion of the colony, has fulfilled his vow to Sol Invictus Mithras for his own health, and for that of his wife Politta, and his sons, the Cocceii.
EDCS-10701876.summary: A vow fulfilled to Sol Invictus Mithras by a local official and his family for their health.

EDCS-11201170.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Posidonius decurio municipii Naissi voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201170.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Aurelius Posidonius, decurio of the municipium of Naissus, set this up by vow.
EDCS-11201170.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius Posidonius, a decurio in the Roman town of Naissus.

EDCS-11201172.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Iulius Herculanus b(ene)f(iciarius) consularis votum libens merito posuit pro se et suis Imperatore domino nostro et Titiano consulibus\n
EDCS-11201172.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Publius Iulius Herculanus, a beneficiary of a consular legate, has willingly set this up as a deserved vow for himself and his family, during the consulship of our lord the Emperor and Titianus.
EDCS-11201172.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by a soldier or officer (beneficiarius consularis) and his family during a specific consular period.

EDCS-11201173.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Iulius Satyrus VSIXPS\n
EDCS-11201173.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Paterno. Iulius Satyrus VSIXPS.
EDCS-11201173.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, mentioning Paterno and Iulius Satyrus, with an incomplete or abbreviated phrase following.

EDCS-11201174.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Marcus Ulpius Herculanus posuit\n
EDCS-11201174.translation: Marcus Ulpius Herculanus dedicated this to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Paterno.
EDCS-11201174.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter by Marcus Ulpius Herculanus.

EDCS-11201175.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno C Flavius Sabinus pro se VM \n
EDCS-11201175.translation: C. Flavius Sabinus dedicated this to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Paterno, for himself, having fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11201175.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by C. Flavius Sabinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201176.text_edition: \n Iovi Paterno Aelius Hermogenes ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201176.translation: Aelius Hermogenes dedicated this to Jupiter Paterno, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11201176.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Jupiter Paterno by Aelius Hermogenes.

EDCS-11201179.text_edition: \n Aurelius Lupus decurio ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201179.translation: Aurelius Lupus, a decurion, dedicated this, fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11201179.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication made by Aurelius Lupus, a decurion.

EDCS-11201183.text_edition: \n et sibi viba sic et Aurelius Valentinus veteranus filiae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201183.translation: And Aurelius Valentinus, a veteran, and his wife dedicated this for themselves, for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-11201183.summary: This tombstone inscription records a dedication by Aurelius Valentinus and his wife for their daughter.

EDCS-11201185.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Florentinae dulcissimae et Victorinae et Candidani sic et Urbiano et liberto Severo et sibi et suis posuit \n
EDCS-11201185.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Aurelia Florentina, sweetest, and Victorina and Candidanus, and Urbianus and his freedman Severus, and for themselves and their family, she dedicated this.
EDCS-11201185.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelia Florentina and several other individuals, likely set up by a surviving family member.

EDCS-11201186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Alexander vixit annos LXX Claudia \n
EDCS-11201186.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Gaius Julius Alexander lived 70 years. Claudia [set this up].
EDCS-11201186.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Gaius Julius Alexander, who lived 70 years, and was likely dedicated by Claudia.

EDCS-11201187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Annus vixit annos XX Plannius pater filio bene merenti et sibi et Licinio filio Andiae Rufus Anni vixit annos XXV Annus Dasi pater filio posuit\n
EDCS-11201187.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Gaius Julius Annus lived 20 years. Plannius, father, for his well-deserving son, and for himself, and for Licinius, son of Rufus Andianus; Annus Dasius, father, for his son, lived 25 years, dedicated this.
EDCS-11201187.summary: This complex funerary inscription records the deaths of Gaius Julius Annus (20) and Annus Dasius (25), with dedications from their fathers.

EDCS-11201188.translation: [He was] prefect of the 5th cohort of the Raeti.
EDCS-11201188.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a military officer, the prefect of the 5th cohort of the Raeti.

EDCS-11201190.text_edition: \n Fili meus sic dulcis Antonine quem fata tulerunt cum octavo carperet anno disrupit sic mors invida vitae fecit vero terminum vitae mense Septembri indictione nona\n
EDCS-11201190.translation: My sweet son Antoninus, whom fate took when he was in his eighth year, envious death cut short his life, setting the term of life in the month of September, in the ninth indiction.
EDCS-11201190.summary: This epitaph laments the premature death of a young boy named Antoninus, who died in September in the ninth indiction.

EDCS-11201193.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Hermes votum libens animo posuit\n
EDCS-11201193.translation: Tiberius Claudius Hermes willingly placed this votive offering.
EDCS-11201193.summary: A private votive inscription from Tiberius Claudius Hermes.

EDCS-11201198.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque dis deabusque immortalibus pro salute dominorum nnostrorum Imperatorum Auggustorum Lucius Aurelius Iustinus vir clarissimus numini maiestatique eorum dicatissimus\n
EDCS-11201198.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all the immortal gods and goddesses, for the safety of our lords the Emperors Augusti, Lucius Aurelius Iustinus, vir clarissimus, dedicated this, devoted to their divinity and majesty.
EDCS-11201198.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and other deities for the imperial family's safety by Lucius Aurelius Iustinus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-11201199.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Augusti Atelius Martinus <q=C>uod sic destinavit posuit \n
EDCS-11201199.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of Augustus, Atelius Martinus placed this, as he had resolved.
EDCS-11201199.summary: A dedication to Jupiter for the safety of Augustus by Atelius Martinus, possibly from the early imperial period.

EDCS-11201200.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertenacis sic Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici Maximi filio divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Augusto tribunicia potestate V consuli proconsuli res publica sua Ulpiana curante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-11201200.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul, proconsul, for his own state of Ulpia, with Quintus Anicius Faustus, legate of the Augusti, propraetor, overseeing.
EDCS-11201200.summary: A lengthy dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus, listing his titles and ancestry, set up by the state of Ulpia overseen by his legate Quintus Anicius Faustus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201201.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aemilio Aemiliano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli proconsuli patri patriae\n
EDCS-11201201.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-11201201.summary: A titulary inscription for Emperor Aemilianus, listing his imperial dignities, likely from his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Tatto vixit nnos LXXXVII et ILIVius vixit annosIVI Severinus heredes bene meritis titulum posuerunt VIItina coniux bene merita m marito titulum posuit\n
EDCS-11201203.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Tatto lived 87 years, and Filivius lived 61 years. Severinus, the heirs, placed this monument for those deserving well. Vitina, the wife, placed this monument for her deserving husband.
EDCS-11201203.summary: A funerary inscription recording the ages of Aurelius Tatto and Filivius, with a monument erected by Severinus and his heirs, and a separate monument for the husband by his wife Vitina.

EDCS-11201205.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Aurelianus legionis VII C[laudiae ------\n
EDCS-11201205.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Aurelianus of the VII Claudia legion [---
EDCS-11201205.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription for Aurelius Aurelianus of the VII Claudia legion.

EDCS-11201206.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mal Iulinanus miles legionis VII Claudiae strator legati vixit nnos LX Aelia Fabricia coniux Mal Iulia Mal Ulpianus Mal Iuliana Iulius Valens fili aeius sic superstantes sibi et coniugi bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201206.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Mal Iulinanus, soldier of the VII Claudia legion, strator of the legate, lived 60 years. Aelia Fabricia, his wife, Mal Iulia, Mal Ulpianus, Mal Iuliana, and Iulius Valens, his children, who survived him, placed this monument for him and his deserving wife.
EDCS-11201206.summary: A funerary inscription for Mal Iulinanus, a soldier and legate's strator, recording his age and erected by his surviving wife and children.

EDCS-11201207.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Nundinianus vixit annos LXXX Valerius Martinianus vixit annos XX Dalmana vixit annos V patri marito filiae posuit bene merentibus Vettiola vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-11201207.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Nundinianus lived 80 years, Valerius Martinianus lived 20 years, Dalmana lived 5 years. Vettiola placed this for her father, husband, and daughters, deserving of remembrance. Vettiola lived 60 years.
EDCS-11201207.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Nundinianus, Valerius Martinianus, and Dalmana, with a monument erected by Vettiola, who also appears to have her own age recorded.

EDCS-11201209.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Victor vixit annos XVIII titulum posuerunt Publius Aelius Iustinus et Aelia Claudia parentes filio pientissimo et sibi sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-11201209.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Victor lived 18 years. Publius Aelius Iustinus and Aelia Claudia, his parents, placed this monument for their most dutiful son, and for themselves. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-11201209.summary: A funerary inscription for the 18-year-old Publius Aelius Victor, with the monument erected by his parents who also intended it for themselves.

EDCS-11201220.translation: Saturninus gave this as a gift to the unconquered Hercules.
EDCS-11201220.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules, given by Saturninus, likely a votive offering.

EDCS-11201224.text_edition: \n Lucii Flavii Proculus et Constans patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201224.translation: Lucius Flavius Proculus and Constans erected this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11201224.summary: This monument was set up by Lucius Flavius Proculus and Constans in honor of their father.

EDCS-11201226.text_edition: \n Deo Tatoni Patrio Lucius Petilius Aurelianus miles legionis VII Claudiae votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11201226.translation: Lucius Petilius Aurelianus, a soldier of the 7th Claudian Legion, willingly fulfilled his vow to the Father God Taton.
EDCS-11201226.summary: This is a soldier's vow fulfillment to the god Taton, dated to the period when the 7th Claudian Legion was active.

EDCS-11201228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Andia rarissima vixit annis LXV hic sita est sit illi terra levis Rufria Amabilis matri pientissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201228.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpia Andia, most rare, lived sixty-five years; here she lies. May the earth lie lightly upon her. Rufria Amabilis saw to the construction for her most devoted mother.
EDCS-11201228.summary: This is a tombstone for Ulpia Andia, erected by Rufria Amabilis, her devoted daughter.

EDCS-11201230.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli proconsuli patri patriae et Quinto Herennio Etruscio Messio Decio nobilissimo Caesari et Herenniae Etruscillae coniugi domini nostri Augusti Imperatoris\n
EDCS-11201230.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and to Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius nobilissimus Caesar, and to Herennia Etruscilla, wife of our lord Augustus the Emperor.
EDCS-11201230.summary: This is a lengthy dedication to Emperor Trajan Decius, his son Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius, and his wife Herennia Etruscilla, likely from the period of their reign (249-251 AD).

EDCS-11201232.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Vibio Treboniano Gallo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto et Caio Vibio Anifino sic Volumniano sic Volusiano Augusto\n
EDCS-11201232.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and to Gaius Vibius Anifinus, also known as Volumnianus, also known as Volusianus Augustus.
EDCS-11201232.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, possibly indicating a joint reign or co-rule.

EDCS-11201243.translation: From the metal mine of Emperor Augustus, belonging to Quintus Gnorius.
EDCS-11201243.summary: This inscription likely marks a mine belonging to Emperor Augustus and operated by Quintus Gnorius.

EDCS-11201249.text_edition: \n Deae Bellonae Caius Iulius Longinus decurio quinquennalis ex visu\n
EDCS-11201249.translation: To the goddess Bellona, Gaius Julius Longinus, decurion for five years, [dedicated this] from a vision.
EDCS-11201249.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Bellona by Gaius Julius Longinus, a decurion, possibly made after a divine vision.

EDCS-11201251.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caelo et Terrae Pontoque Beato ob vindictam fecit Cassius Vict Victor Onesimus aram Scupinorum aedilis decurio votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-11201251.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to the Sky and blessed Pontus, Cassius Vict, Victor, Onesimus, aedile and decurion of the Scupini, fulfilled his vow with merit for vengeance, and made this altar.
EDCS-11201251.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Cassius Vict, Victor, and Onesimus to Jupiter, the Sky, and Pontus for vengeance, including the erection of an altar.

EDCS-11201253.text_edition: \n Deo Libero Patri Marcus Ulpius Iulianus ob inmunitatem!) consecravit consacranorum Marciani et Varidi libertorum votum posuit\n
EDCS-11201253.translation: To the Father God Liber, Marcus Ulpius Iulianus consecrated [this] for immunity, and set up the vow of his freedmen Marcianus and Varidus.
EDCS-11201253.summary: This is a dedication to the god Liber by Marcus Ulpius Iulianus, who consecrated it for immunity and also fulfilled a vow for his freedmen.

EDCS-11201256.text_edition: \n Luci filius Publilia Re Verona con decurio quaestor IIvir col(oniae) Fl(aviae) felicis Dardanorum hoc templum impensa sua faciendum curavit deae Syriae\n
EDCS-11201256.translation: Lucius, son of Lucius, of the Publilia tribe, from Verona, a decurion, quaestor, and IIvir of the colony of Flavia Felix of the Dardani, arranged for this temple to be built at his own expense for the goddess Syria.
EDCS-11201256.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple for the goddess Syria by Lucius, a local official in the colony of Flavia Felix of the Dardani, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201257.text_edition: \n Deae Aurelius voto libens posuit NO SEMPRIG et IINFORT\n
EDCS-11201257.translation: Aurelius willingly set up this offering to the goddess, [and to?] Semprig and Iunfort.
EDCS-11201257.summary: This dedication records an offering made by Aurelius to a goddess, possibly along with other deities or individuals named Semprig and Iunfort, with the date not inferable.

EDCS-11201260.text_edition: \n Pro salute Saturninus tribunus militum bis votum solvit libens Legio VII Claudia votum libens posuit et Claudius Divus |(centurio)\n
EDCS-11201260.translation: Saturninus, a military tribune, twice willingly fulfilled his vow for the safety [of the emperor?]. Legio VII Claudia willingly fulfilled its vow and Claudius Divus, a centurion.
EDCS-11201260.summary: This inscription records vows fulfilled by Saturninus, a military tribune, and by Legio VII Claudia and its centurion Claudius Divus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201263.text_edition: \n Rufriae Maximae matri sanctissimae Libonius Severus vir clarissmus consularis Scupinorum Ravennatium Ariminensium Vulsinensium Pisaurensium Arretinensium Clusiensium Asisensium Alisciensium Nucerinorum Attidiensium Beneventanorum\n
EDCS-11201263.translation: To Rufria Maxima, most holy mother, from Libonius Severus, a most distinguished man, consul, [patron?] of the Scupini, Ravennates, Ariminenses, Vulsinenses, Pisaurenses, Arretini, Clusienses, Asisenses, Aliscienses, Nucerini, Attidienses, Beneventani.
EDCS-11201263.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors Rufria Maxima as a most holy mother by Libonius Severus, a distinguished consular official and patron, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201264.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dis Manibus Florentia serva vilica Furi Octaviani clarissimi viri vixit nnos XXII et Eutychius vixit annos XI et Patamilla vixit annos V hic siti sunt Anicetus pater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201264.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Florentia, the slave overseer of the most distinguished man Furius Octavianus, lived 22 years, and Eutychius lived 11 years, and Patamilla lived 5 years. Anicetus, their father, arranged for them to be buried here.
EDCS-11201264.summary: This tomb inscription records the burial of three individuals, Florentia, Eutychius, and Patamilla, with Anicetus as the person responsible for the burial, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201265.text_edition: \n Marco Insteio Marci filio Scaptia Valenti primo pilo praefecto legionis III Augustae\n
EDCS-11201265.translation: To Marcus Insteius Valens, son of Marcus, of the Scaptia tribe, primipilaris, prefect of Legio III Augustae.
EDCS-11201265.summary: This epitaph honors Marcus Insteius Valens, a high-ranking military officer (primipilaris and prefect of Legio III Augustae), likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201266.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Marinus decurio Scupinorum vixit annis LXVIII hic situs est Marcilli Vale<r=K>ii Vitalis eques Romanus et Marinus miles legionis VII Claudiae patri inconparabili sic \n
EDCS-11201266.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Valerius Marinus, decurion of the Scupini, lived 68 years, is buried here. Marcilius Valerius Vitalis, an equestrian, and Marinus, a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, to their incomparable father. Thus.
EDCS-11201266.summary: This tomb inscription honors Marcus Valerius Marinus, a local official, and his father, who was an equestrian and a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201267.text_edition: \n Tito Turranio Luci filio Sergia Proculo Flanona primipilo praefecto castrorum legionis XIIII geminae IIviro quinquennali coloniae Flaviae Scupinorum Lucius Valerius Iustus amico locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-11201267.translation: To Titus Turranius Proculus, son of Lucius, of the Sergia tribe, primipilaris, prefect of the camp of Legio XIIII Gemina, quinquennial IIvir of the colony of Flavia Scupinorum. Lucius Valerius Iustus gave this place to his friend by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-11201267.summary: This inscription records the granting of a burial plot by Lucius Valerius Iustus to his friend Titus Turranius Proculus, a prominent military and civil official in the colony of Flavia Scupinorum, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201268.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Turannis vixit nnos LXX hic sita est Titus Clementianus eques <R=K>(omanus) nutrici bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-11201268.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Tura lived 70 years, is buried here. Titus Clementianus, Roman equestrian, set this up for his well-deserving wet nurse.
EDCS-11201268.summary: This tomb inscription records the burial of Tura, who lived 70 years, and was honored by Titus Clementianus, a Roman equestrian, who placed the monument for his devoted wet nurse, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201269.text_edition: \n tonius Marci filius Maecia Pelagonia Marius veteranus cohortis II praetoriae centuria Valeri Crispini testamento fieri iussit Symphorus libertus faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201269.translation: Antonius, son of Marcus, of the Maecia tribe, Pelagonia, a veteran of Cohors II Praetoria, in the century of Valerius Crispinus, ordered this to be made in his will. Symphorus, his freedman, arranged for it to be built. He is buried here.
EDCS-11201269.summary: This tomb inscription records the posthumous construction of a burial monument for Antonius Pelagonia, a veteran, ordered by his will and executed by his freedman Symphorus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201270.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Victorina viva sibi et Caio Valerio Pud<e=I>nti veterano ex praetorio et Caio Iulio Celeri qui militat praetorianus filiis bene merentibus et Caio Valerio Maximo Augustali coloniae S<c=E>upinorum generi sic bene merenti faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-11201270.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iulia Victorina, while alive, arranged for this to be made for herself, for Gaius Valerius Pudens, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, for Gaius Iulius Celer, who serves in the Praetorian Guard, her sons, who deserved well; and for Gaius Valerius Maximus, Augustalis of the colony of Scupini, her son-in-law, who deserved well.
EDCS-11201270.summary: A funerary monument set up by Iulia Victorina for herself and her family members, including a Praetorian Guardsman and a local Augustalis.

EDCS-11201271.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Terentio Terentiano |(centurioni) legionum qui vixit annis LII hic situs est Iulia Didyme coniugi bene merenti faciendum curavit curante Terente Macrino liberto\n
EDCS-11201271.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Terentius Terentianus, a centurion of legions, who lived 52 years, is buried here. Iulia Didyme arranged for this to be made for her deserving husband, with Terentius Macrinus, a freedman, overseeing it.
EDCS-11201271.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Terentius Terentianus, a centurion, erected by his wife Iulia Didyme, dated to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201273.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Mestrianus veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae vixit annis L hic situs est Aelia Respecta coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201273.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Mestrianus, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, who lived 50 years, is buried here. Aelia Respecta erected this for her deserving wife.
EDCS-11201273.summary: A tombstone for Aurelius Mestrianus, a veteran of the Legio IV Flavia, dedicated by his wife Aelia Respecta.

EDCS-11201274.text_edition: \n Lucius Valeri<u>s Luci filius Galeria Galenus Luco veteranus legionis IIII Macedonicae vixit annos LV militavit annos XXVIII hic situs est heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201274.translation: Lucius Valerius Galenus, son of Lucius, of the Galerian tribe, a veteran of the Fourth Macedonian Legion, lived 55 years and served 28 years. He is buried here. The heir arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11201274.summary: A funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Galenus, a veteran of the Legio IV Macedonica, erected by his heir.

EDCS-11201275.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Voltinia Velox domo Luco Vocontiorum veteranus legionis V Alaudae militavit nnos XXXV vixit annos LX Caius Iulius Valens heres faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201275.translation: Gaius Iulius Velox, son of Gaius, of the Voltinian tribe, from Luco in the territory of the Vocontii, a veteran of the Fifth Alaudae Legion, served 35 years and lived 60 years. Gaius Iulius Valens, the heir, arranged for this to be made. He is buried here.
EDCS-11201275.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Iulius Velox, a veteran of the Legio V Alaudae, set up by his heir, Gaius Iulius Valens.

EDCS-11201276.text_edition: \n Marcus Octavius Marci filius Aemilia Valens Stobis miles legionis V Macedonicae decurio pontifex coloniae Flaviae Felicis Dardanorum vivos sic fecit sibi Octaviae Marcellinae filiae suae defunctae vixit annis XXVI et Catiae Secundae coniugi suae hic se vivo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201276.translation: Marcus Octavius Valens, son of Marcus, of the Aemilian tribe, a soldier of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, a decurion and pontifex of the colony of Flavia Felix Dardanorum, made this while alive for himself, for his deceased daughter Octavia Marcellina, who lived 26 years, and for his wife Catia Secunda. He arranged for this to be made during his lifetime.
EDCS-11201276.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcus Octavius Valens for himself, his deceased daughter, and his wife, mentioning his military service and local priesthood.

EDCS-11201277.text_edition: \n Lucius Apuleius Luci filius Scaptia Valens Berua veteranus legionis V Macedonicae vixit nnos L militavit nnos XXII hic situs est heredes faciendum curaverunt Maxima filia et Hesperis liberta itemque coniunx\n
EDCS-11201277.translation: Lucius Apuleius Valens, son of Lucius, of the Scaptian tribe, from Berua, a veteran of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, lived 50 years and served 22 years. He is buried here. His heirs arranged for this to be made: Maxima, his daughter, and Hesperis, his freedwoman, and also his wife.
EDCS-11201277.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Apuleius Valens, a veteran of the Legio V Macedonica, erected by his daughter, freedwoman, and wife.

EDCS-11201278.text_edition: \n Caio Cornelio Cai filio Fabia Magno Beryto vixit annis LXXV militavit annis XXI in legione VII Claudia pia fideli beneficiarius et inmunis decurio et IIvir coloniae Scupinorum hic situs est Publicia Secunda uxor ex testamento faciendum curavit hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-11201278.translation: To Gaius Cornelius Magnus, son of Gaius, of the Fabian tribe, from Berytus. He lived 75 years, served 21 years in the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion as a beneficiary, immunis, decurion, and duumvir of the colony of Scupini. He is buried here. Publicia Secunda, his wife, arranged for this to be made in accordance with his will; this monument will not follow its heir.
EDCS-11201278.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Cornelius Magnus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia and a duumvir of Scupini, erected by his wife Publicia Secunda according to his will.

EDCS-11201279.text_edition: \n Lucius Marcianius Amandus domo Sa<l=B>onis veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis militavit annis XXII beneficiarius Marci Valeri Etrusci legati consularis quaestor IIv<i=L>r coloniae Fl(aviae) fel(icis) D vixit annis LX titulum <f=E>(ieri) iussit hic situs est Lucius Marcianius Probatus Lucius Marcianius Successus li<b=D>erti faciendum curaverunt \n
EDCS-11201279.translation: Lucius Marcianius Amandus from Sabonis, a veteran of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, served 22 years as a beneficiary of Marcus Valerius Etruscus, consular legate. He was quaestor and duumvir of the colony of Flavia Felix. He lived 60 years. He ordered the monument to be made. He is buried here. Lucius Marcianius Probatus and Lucius Marcianius Successus, his freedmen, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11201279.summary: A funerary monument for Lucius Marcianius Amandus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia and a local official, erected by his freedmen.

EDCS-11201280.text_edition: \n Titus Abudius Titi filius Velina Priscus Aquileia veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis vixit nnos LXXXV hic situs est Felicula coniunx faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201280.translation: Titus Abudius Priscus, son of Titus, of the Velina tribe, from Aquileia, a veteran of the VII Claudia Pia Fidelis legion, lived 85 years. He is buried here. Felicula, his wife, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11201280.summary: A tombstone for Titus Abudius Priscus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, erected by his wife Felicula.

EDCS-11201281.text_edition: \n Publius Caetennius Publi filius Clemens Salonitanus veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis militavit annis XXI vixit annis LXX hic situs est Publius Caetennius Felicianus libertus et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201281.translation: To Publius Caetennius Clemens, son of Publius, from Salona, a veteran of the Seventh Claudian legion, pious and faithful, he served for 21 years and lived for 70 years; he is buried here. Publius Caetennius Felicianus, his freedman and heir, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-11201281.summary: This funerary monument records Publius Caetennius Clemens, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, and was erected by his freedman and heir.

EDCS-11201282.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Pontico veterano legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis deductus Scupos vixit nnos LX hic situs est in praedio suo iugerum in quadratum Iulia Pontinia \n
EDCS-11201282.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Julius Ponticus, a veteran of the Seventh Claudian legion, pious and faithful, discharged to Scupi. He lived 60 years and is buried here on his own farm of one iugerum. Iulia Pontinia [dedicated this].
EDCS-11201282.summary: This tombstone is for Gaius Julius Ponticus, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, who lived and owned land in Scupi.

EDCS-11201284.text_edition: \n Claudia Maxumus sic Curico veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis stipendiorum XX vixit nnos LX Fortunata et Rhoios liberti heredes titulum faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201284.translation: To Claudia Maxumus, veteran of the Seventh Claudian legion, pious and faithful, with 20 years of service. She lived 60 years. Fortunata and Rhoios, her freedpersons and heirs, saw to the erection of this tombstone.
EDCS-11201284.summary: This inscription commemorates Claudia Maxumus, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, and was set up by her freedpersons and heirs.

EDCS-11201285.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Valentina Tiberius Claudius Hermes maritus vivo sic se sic Flavio Turboni nepoti militi cohortis singularium bene merenti posuerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201285.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Tiberius Claudius Hermes erected this for his wife Valeria Valentina, while alive, and for his grandson Flavius Turbonus, a soldier of the cohort of bodyguards, for his good service.
EDCS-11201285.summary: This monument was jointly dedicated by Tiberius Claudius Hermes and his wife Valeria Valentina for their grandson Flavius Turbonus, a soldier.

EDCS-11201286.text_edition: \n Iulius Vanno missicius ex ala Flavia militavit annis XXIII vixit annis XXXXV hic situs est Flavius Antiocus heres faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-11201286.translation: To Iulius Vanno, discharged soldier from the Flavian cavalry wing. He served for 23 years and lived for 45 years; he is buried here. Flavius Antiocus, his heir, saw to its erection.
EDCS-11201286.summary: This tombstone marks the burial of Iulius Vanno, a discharged cavalryman, and was erected by his heir.

EDCS-11201288.text_edition: \n Tito Statilio Titi filio Tauro Quirina Faustiniano ornato a splendidissimo ordine Scupinorum ornamentis\n
EDCS-11201288.translation: To Titus Statilius Taurus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, honored with the finest ornaments by the most distinguished council of Scupi.
EDCS-11201288.summary: This dedication honors Titus Statilius Taurus of Scupi for receiving distinguished civic ornaments.

EDCS-11201290.text_edition: \n Caio Sempronio Cai filio Quirina Lupo decurioni quaestori IIviro coloniae Scupinorum et Terentiae Quinti filiae Quintae parentibus optimis Caius Sempronius Taurus decurio IIvir coloniae Scupinorum\n
EDCS-11201290.translation: To Gaius Sempronius Lupus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, decurion and quaestor, and twice-a-year magistrate of the colony of Scupi, and to Terentia Quinta, daughter of Quintus. For their most excellent parents, Gaius Sempronius Taurus, decurion and twice-a-year magistrate of the colony of Scupi [dedicated this].
EDCS-11201290.summary: This inscription records civic honors for Gaius Sempronius Lupus and his wife Terentia Quinta, with a dedication to their parents by their son Gaius Sempronius Taurus.

EDCS-11201292.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Cati qui et Felix sic decurionis qaestoris coloniae Scupinorum vixit nnos CL sic\n
EDCS-11201292.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Gaius Catius, also called Felix, decurion, quaestor of the colony of Scupi. He lived 150 years.
EDCS-11201292.summary: This funerary inscription is for Gaius Catius Felix, a decurion and quaestor of Scupi, noted for his exceptionally long life.

EDCS-11201293.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Glauce vixit nnos XXVIII hic sita est Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus uxori rarissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201293.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valeria Glauce lived 28 years and is buried here. Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius, adorned with decurional honors, had this made for his most rare wife.
EDCS-11201293.summary: This tombstone honors Valeria Glauce, who died at 28, and was erected by her husband Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius, who held decurional honors.

EDCS-11201294.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Atilius Hilarus IIIIIIvir Augustalis coloniae Scupinorum vixit nnos XXXV hic situs est Lucius Atilius Eucharistus pater\n
EDCS-11201294.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Atilius Hilarus, member of the board of six and Augustalis of the colony of Scupi, lived 35 years and is buried here. Lucius Atilius Eucharistus, his father [dedicated this].
EDCS-11201294.summary: This funerary monument records Lucius Atilius Hilarus, a local magistrate of Scupi, and was erected by his father.

EDCS-11201295.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Iulius Servandus IIIIIIvir Augustalis coloniae Scupinorum qui vixit nnos LXX hic situs defunctus Titus Iulius Servandus filius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201295.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Titus Iulius Servandus, a Septemvir of the Augustales of the colony of Scupinorum, who lived 70 years, is buried here. His son, Titus Iulius Servandus, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201295.summary: A funerary monument for Titus Iulius Servandus, a prominent local official, commissioned by his son.

EDCS-11201296.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Publicius Tertius IIIIIIvir Augustalis se vivo sibi et Statuleae Placidae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201296.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Publicius Tertius, a Septemvir of the Augustales, erected this for himself while alive, and for his deserving wife, Statuleia Placidus.
EDCS-11201296.summary: A funerary monument erected by Gaius Publicius Tertius for himself and his wife, indicating he made provision during his lifetime.

EDCS-11201297.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus IIIIIIvir Augustalis coloniae Scupinorum vixit nnos L hic situs est Valeria Cai filia Asclepiodote nnorum L viva se sibi et infelici marito faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201297.translation: To the spirits of the departed. A Septemvir of the Augustales of the colony of Scupinorum, who lived 50 years, is buried here. Valeria Asclepiodote, daughter of Caius, aged 50, had this made for herself and her unfortunate husband while alive.
EDCS-11201297.summary: A funerary monument for Valeria Asclepiodote, who commissioned it for herself and her husband during her lifetime.

EDCS-11201298.text_edition: \n Marus Ellius Marci libertus Phoebus Augustalis coloniae Scupinorum vixit annos LXXV hic situs est heredes faciendum curaverunt Surus Ianuarius Nereus\n
EDCS-11201298.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Phoebus, freedman of Marus Ellius, an Augustalis of the colony of Scupinorum, lived 75 years, is buried here. His heirs Surus, Ianuarius, and Nereus saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201298.summary: A funerary monument for Phoebus, a freedman and local official, commissioned by his heirs.

EDCS-11201299.text_edition: \n Lib Crescentilla vixit annos XV hic sita est Caius Lib Crescens Augustalis filiae piae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201299.translation: Lib Crescentilla lived 15 years, is buried here. Gaius Lib Crescens, an Augustalis, commissioned this for his pious daughter.
EDCS-11201299.summary: A funerary monument for Lib Crescentilla, commissioned by her father, Gaius Lib Crescens, an Augustalis.

EDCS-11201301.text_edition: \n Publius Aebutius Proclus vixit annis L et Publius Aebutius Proclianus vixit nnos II hic siti sunt Fabricia Lucilia coniunx et Publius Aebutius Rufus et Publius Aebutius Valens patri pientissimo faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201301.translation: Publius Aebutius Proclus lived 50 years, and Publius Aebutius Proclianus lived 2 years, are buried here. Fabricia Lucilia, his wife, and Publius Aebutius Rufus and Publius Aebutius Valens commissioned this for the most devoted father.
EDCS-11201301.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Aebutius Proclus and his young son Proclianus, commissioned by his wife and other family members.

EDCS-11201302.text_edition: \n Publius Aebutius Rufus vixit annis XLV hic situs est Publius Aebutius Proclianus patri pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201302.translation: Publius Aebutius Rufus lived 45 years, is buried here. Publius Aebutius Proclianus commissioned this for the most devoted father.
EDCS-11201302.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Aebutius Rufus, commissioned by his son, Publius Aebutius Proclianus.

EDCS-11201303.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Maxima vixit nnos L hic sita est ius coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201303.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelia Maxima lived 50 years, is buried here. Her husband erected this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11201303.summary: A funerary monument for Aelia Maxima, erected by her husband.

EDCS-11201305.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Afrodisia vixit nnos XXXXIII hic sita est Numerius vixit nnos LX hic situs est sibi et coniugi posuit Numerius haec sunt\n
EDCS-11201305.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Afrodisia lived 43 years, is buried here. Numerius lived 60 years, is buried here. Numerius erected this for himself and his wife.
EDCS-11201305.summary: A funerary monument jointly dedicated by Numerius for himself and his wife Afrodisia.

EDCS-11201306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ambia Longinia qui vixit annos LXXX hic sita est Vi<b=V>ius Valentis maritus coniugi inco<m=N>parabili posuit \n
EDCS-11201306.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ambia Longinia lived 80 years, is buried here. Vi<b=V>ius Valentis, her husband, erected this for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-11201306.summary: A funerary monument for Ambia Longinia, erected by her husband Vi<b=V>ius Valentis.

EDCS-11201307.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Annaea Clementilla vixit nnos XL hic sita est vicus Cavadinus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201307.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Annaea Clementilla lived 40 years, lies here. She arranged for this to be made by the Cavadinus district.
EDCS-11201307.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Annaea Clementilla, who lived 40 years, with the mention of the Cavadinus district overseeing its construction, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201308.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Procula vixit annos XX hic sita est Lucius Valerius Firmus coniugi dulcissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201308.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia Procula lived 20 years, lies here. Lucius Valerius Firmus arranged for this to be made for his most sweet wife.
EDCS-11201308.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Antonia Procula, who lived 20 years, erected by her husband Lucius Valerius Firmus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201309.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Ateius Vitalis vixit annos XVII hic situs est Fabricia Iulia filio carissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201309.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Ateius Vitalis lived 17 years, lies here. Fabricia Iulia arranged for this to be made for her dearest son.
EDCS-11201309.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Ateius Vitalis, who lived 17 years, erected by his mother Fabricia Iulia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201312.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Lucius Attius Severus vixit annis XXXX Valeria Cornelia Lucius Attius Severus et Lucius Severinus patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201312.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Attius Severus lived 40 years. Valeria Cornelia and Lucius Attius Severus and Lucius Severinus set this up for their deserving father.
EDCS-11201312.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Attius Severus, who lived 40 years, erected by his wife Valeria Cornelia and sons Lucius Attius Severus and Lucius Severinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201313.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aufidia Victoria vixit annis XXVIII hic sita est Caius Iulius Iulianus coniugi bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201313.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aufidia Victoria lived 28 years, lies here. Caius Iulius Iulianus arranged for this to be made for his deserving wife.
EDCS-11201313.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aufidia Victoria, who lived 28 years, erected by her husband Caius Iulius Iulianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201314.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Valens vixit nnos XX parentes hoc monumentum faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201314.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Valens lived 20 years. His parents arranged for this monument to be made.
EDCS-11201314.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Valens, who lived 20 years, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201315.text_edition: \n ------ anno]s XL hic situs est Aurelius bene merenti \n
EDCS-11201315.translation: [...] 40 years, lies here. Aurelius [...] for his deserving [...].
EDCS-11201315.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the lifespan and erection by Aurelius for a deserving individual, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201316.text_edition: \n Bassus Apolloni filius domo Antiochia vixit annos LV hic situs est Lucilia Varena faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201316.translation: Bassus, son of Apollonius, from Antioch, lived 55 years, lies here. Lucilia Varena arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-11201316.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bassus, son of Apollonius, from Antioch, who lived 55 years, erected by Lucilia Varena, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201317.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Caecilius Bio vixit annos XXX hic situs est Caecilia Ianuaria patrono bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201317.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Sextus Caecilius Bio lived 30 years, lies here. Caecilia Ianuaria arranged for this to be made for her deserving patron.
EDCS-11201317.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Caecilius Bio, who lived 30 years, erected by his client Caecilia Ianuaria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201318.text_edition: \n Cesus Mestri filius Macedo Apeunenses sic vixit annis LXX hic situs est Delus coniux et Bithus filius Cesunis sic heres bene merito faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201318.translation: Cesus, son of Mestrius, a Macedonian from Apeunenses, lived 70 years, lies here. His wife Delus and son Bithus, son of Cesus, arranged for this to be made for their deserving relative.
EDCS-11201318.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cesus, son of Mestrius, from Apeunenses, who lived 70 years, erected by his wife Delus and son Bithus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201319.text_edition: \n lia Claudia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201319.translation: Lia Claudia placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11201319.summary: This is a funerary epitaph placed by Lia Claudia for her husband, likely dating to the early imperial period.

EDCS-11201320.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Firmina vixit annos XX hic sita est Claudia Valeria mater infelix posuit bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201320.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Firmina lived 20 years; she is buried here. Claudia Valeria, her unfortunate mother, placed this for her well-deserving daughter and saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201320.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Firmina, who died at age 20, erected by her mother Claudia Valeria, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201321.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Lucilia vixit annis LX hic sita est et Titus Flavius Fuscus maritus eius se vivo ambis sic posuit\n
EDCS-11201321.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Lucilia lived 60 years; she is buried here. And Titus Flavius Fuscus, her husband, erected this for both of them while alive.
EDCS-11201321.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Lucilia and her husband Titus Flavius Fuscus, who both lived to old age and commissioned the monument themselves, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201322.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Claudi Crispi filius Quirina Germanus vixit nnos LXV hic situs est Claudia Tuta uxor et Claudius Crispus et Claudia Vera filii faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201322.translation: Tiberius Claudius Germanus, son of Claudius Crispus, of the Quirina tribe, lived 65 years; he is buried here. His wife Claudia Tuta and his children Claudius Crispus and Claudia Vera saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201322.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Germanus, who died at age 65, erected by his wife and children, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201323.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cocceia Marcellina vixit annis LX hic sita est Claudia Octavia matri pientissimae posuit Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Aelianus vixit annis XXX hic situs est mater <qu=C>od petierat de sic filia sua sublato ante diem et iuniori nato obito Claudia Octavia fratri pientissimo posuit \n
EDCS-11201323.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cocceia Marcellina lived 60 years; she is buried here. Claudia Octavia placed this for her most dutiful mother. To the Spirits of the Departed. Tiberius Claudius Aelianus lived 30 years; he is buried here. His mother, because of what she had requested from her daughter, having lost [her son] earlier and her younger born, Claudia Octavia placed this for her most dutiful brother.
EDCS-11201323.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph commemorating Cocceia Marcellina, Tiberius Claudius Aelianus, and an unnamed younger brother, with inscriptions from both a mother and a sister, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201324.text_edition: \n vixit annos XXX Cocceia Luci Lucilia mater filiae piissimae faciendum curavit vixit annos XXX Cornelius Crescentio coniugi piissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201324.translation: Cocceia Luci lived 30 years; she is buried here. Her most dutiful mother Lucilia saw to its construction. Cornelius Crescentio lived 30 years; his most dutiful wife saw to its construction for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11201324.summary: This inscription records two individuals, Cocceia Luci and Cornelius Crescentio, who died at age 30, with their respective family members commissioning the monuments, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201325.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Cornelius Felicio vixit annis LXXX hic situs est Corneficiae Primitibae sic vivae Quintus Cornelius Felicianus parentibus bene merentibus titulum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201325.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Quintus Cornelius Felicio lived 80 years; he is buried here. Quintus Cornelius Felicianus saw to the construction of this tomb for his well-deserving parents, Corneficia Primitiba and [Quintus Cornelius Felicio?], while alive.
EDCS-11201325.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Quintus Cornelius Felicianus for his parents, Quintus Cornelius Felicio and Corneficia Primitiba, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201326.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cornificia Valeria vixit annos XXIII hic sita est Lucius Cornificius Vitales sic Iulia Valentina filiae piissimae posuerunt hoc tu nobis facere debuisti filia cuam sic nos tibi Lucius Seius Maternus vixit annos XXVIIII hic situs est Lucius Cornificius Vitales sic Iulia Valentina genero piissimo bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201326.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Cornificia Valeria lived 23 years; she is buried here. Lucius Cornificius Vitales and Iulia Valentina placed this for their most dutiful daughter. You should have done this for us, daughter! Lucius Seius Maternus lived 29 years; he is buried here. Lucius Cornificius Vitales and Iulia Valentina placed this for their most deserving son-in-law.
EDCS-11201326.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the deaths of Cornificia Valeria (age 23) and her brother-in-law Lucius Seius Maternus (age 29), erected by their parents Lucius Cornificius Vitales and Iulia Valentina, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201327.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dionysius Petroni et Iulia Ingenu vixit annos LX a qui vivo sic se posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201327.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Dionysius and Iulia Ingenu lived 60 years; they placed this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-11201327.summary: This funerary epitaph records Dionysius and Iulia Ingenu, who lived 60 years and erected their monument during their lifetime, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201328.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Domitio ilio annorum \n
EDCS-11201328.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucio Domitio, of [age] ___ years.
EDCS-11201328.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary epitaph for Lucio Domitio, the age of whom is lost, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201329.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Drutie Mestulae filia vixit annos LXX Maema Dioscuridis filia vixit annos hic sitae sunt se vivis sibi faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201329.translation: To the departed spirits. Drutia Mestula, daughter, lived 70 years. Maema, daughter of Dioscurides, lived [here are laid]. They arranged to have this made for themselves while alive.
EDCS-11201329.summary: A funerary epitaph for Drutia Mestula and Maema, who provided for their own burials during their lifetime, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201330.text_edition: \n Eutycho statuario Corneli Magni vixit annis XXV hic situs est Publicia Secunda bene merenti\n
EDCS-11201330.translation: Eutyches the statuary, of Cornelius Magnus, lived 25 years. He is laid here. Publicia Secunda [placed this] for [him], well-deserving.
EDCS-11201330.summary: A tombstone for Eutyches, a statuary employed by Cornelius Magnus, set up by Publicia Secunda, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201331.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Parthenopes liberta Tyche vixit annos LX Flavia Parthenope nutriculae et libertae bonae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201331.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Parthenope, freedwoman of Tyche, lived 60 years. Flavia Parthenope erected [this] for her nurse and good freedwoman, well-deserving.
EDCS-11201331.summary: A funerary monument for Flavia Parthenope, erected by her patron Flavia Parthenope, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201332.text_edition: \n Flavia Postumina Flavi filia vixit annos XXX Publius Aelius Menodotus coniugi pientissimae bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201332.translation: Flavia Postumina, daughter of Flavius, lived 30 years. Publius Aelius Menodotus arranged to have this made for his most dutiful wife, well-deserving.
EDCS-11201332.summary: A tombstone for Flavia Postumina, erected by her husband Publius Aelius Menodotus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201333.text_edition: \n Flavia Urbana Titi filia vixit nnos XXX hic sita est Caius Iulius Lucretius coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201333.translation: Flavia Urbana, daughter of Titus, lived 30 years. She is laid here. Gaius Julius Lucretius placed [this] for his wife, well-deserving.
EDCS-11201333.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia Urbana, set up by her husband Gaius Julius Lucretius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201334.text_edition: \n Manibus Dis Titus Flavius Fyrmus sic hic situs frug<i=I> Vividae amatae aqua sibi <qu=C>od cupierat bonus vir vivens annos LXXV vivo sibi posuit hic pietatis honor haec sunt pia dona mariti qui me diligit Valeriae coniug(i) vixit annos L Flavius Fyrmus sic coniugi bene merenti posuit \n
EDCS-11201334.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Fyrmus, thus laid here, for his dear wife Vivida, with water, and for himself what he had desired, a good man, lived 75 years. He set this up for himself while alive. This honor of piety, these are the pious gifts of a husband who loved me, Valeria, his wife, lived 50 years. Flavius Fyrmus placed [this] for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11201334.summary: A complex funerary epitaph for Titus Flavius Fyrmus and his wife Valeria, who provided for their own burials and remembered each other, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201336.text_edition: \n Fulcinio Euangelo marito et Sempronio Pino et Lupo filiis hic siti sunt Fulcinia Hedyle mater pietatis causa faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201336.translation: For Fulcinius Evangellus, husband, and Sempronius Pinus and Lupus, sons. They are laid here. Fulcinia Hedyle, mother, arranged to have this made out of piety.
EDCS-11201336.summary: A funerary monument for Fulcinius Evangellus and his sons Pinus and Lupus, erected by their mother Fulcinia Hedyle, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201337.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Marcellina vixit annos XXX Caius Iulius Caianus et filius vixit annos XVIII pater titulum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201337.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Marcellina lived 30 years. Gaius Julius Caianus and [his] son lived 18 years. The father arranged to have the monument made.
EDCS-11201337.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Marcellina and her son, erected by Gaius Julius Caianus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201339.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Iulius Clemens vixit annis LXX hic situs est Marci Iuli Fuscus Valens Valentius patri pientissimo bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201339.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Iulius Clemens lived 70 years. He is laid here. Marcus Iulius Fuscus Valens Valentius arranged to have this made for his most dutiful father, well-deserving.
EDCS-11201339.summary: A tombstone for Marcus Iulius Clemens, erected by his sons Marcus Iulius Fuscus Valens and Valentius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201341.text_edition: \n Lucilia vixit nnos XX ex testamento fecit matri Seius Proculus pientissimae\n
EDCS-11201341.translation: Lucilia lived 20 years. Seius Proculus erected [this] by her will for his most dutiful mother.
EDCS-11201341.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucilia, who arranged for her own burial by will, erected by her son Seius Proculus for his mother, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201342.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Manlius Valentinus comes vixit annos XL menses dies V horas B \n
EDCS-11201342.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Manlius Valentinus, a companion, lived 40 years, 5 days, and B hours.
EDCS-11201342.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Manlius Valentinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201343.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maria Maxima vixit annos L Marcus Marius Blicities libertus ex testamento eius ponendum curavit et Valerii Sabinianus et Maximianus filii monumentum parenti bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11201343.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Maria Maxima lived 50 years. Marcus Marius Blicities, her freedman, saw to the erection of this monument according to her will, and Valerius Sabinianus and Maximianus, her sons, erected this monument for their well-deserving parent.
EDCS-11201343.summary: A funerary epitaph for Maria Maxima, erected by her freedman and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201345.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mestylas Momoniae coniugi bene merenti titulum posuit\n
EDCS-11201345.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mestylas placed this monument for his well-deserving wife, Momonia.
EDCS-11201345.summary: A funerary epitaph where Mestylas dedicated a monument to his wife Momonia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201346.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Numerianus vixit nnos XXV hic situs est Numeria vixit nnos XXXIII hic sita est Afrodisia mater supervixit dies XXX Numerius filios sic acerbos sic posuit\n
EDCS-11201346.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Numerianus lived 25 years; he is buried here. Numeria lived 33 years; she is buried here. Afrodisia, their mother, survived them by 30 days. Numerius placed his unfortunate children here.
EDCS-11201346.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating Numerianus and Numeria, placed by their mother Afrodisia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201347.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Obellius Crescens vixit nnos LXX hic situs est Iulia Eutychia et Obelli Alexander et Herculanus patri et coniugi bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201347.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Obellius Crescens lived 70 years; he is buried here. Iulia Eutychia and his sons Obellius Alexander and Herculanus saw to the erection of this monument for their well-deserving father and husband.
EDCS-11201347.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Obellius Crescens, erected by his wife Iulia Eutychia and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201349.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Ostilius Valens vixit annis LXXXVII Maxima coniunx Lucius Ostilius Fronto et Lucius Ostilius <V=M>alens Lucius Ostilius Clemens bene merenti posuerunt \n
EDCS-11201349.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Ostilius Valens lived 87 years. His wife Maxima, Lucius Ostilius Fronto, Lucius Ostilius Valens, and Lucius Ostilius Clemens erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201349.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Ostilius Valens, erected by his wife and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201350.text_edition: \n Petillia sic Clara vixit annis IIII Dis Manibus Lucius Petilius Lupus vixit annis XXV hic situs est Vesidia Ingenua mater filio dulcissimo posuit\n
EDCS-11201350.translation: Petillia Clara lived 4 years. To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Petilius Lupus lived 25 years; he is buried here. Vesidia Ingenua, his mother, placed this for her dearest son.
EDCS-11201350.summary: A funerary epitaph for the child Petillia Clara and the adult Lucius Petilius Lupus, placed by their mother Vesidia Ingenua, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201351.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Petronius Valens vixit annos L et Aurelia Marcia vixit annos XXXX Petroni Lucia Valens Marcus fili ponendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201351.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Petronius Valens lived 50 years and Aurelia Marcia lived 40 years. Their children Lucia Valens and Marcus Petronius saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-11201351.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Petronius Valens and Aurelia Marcia, erected by their children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201352.text_edition: \n hic situs est Lucius Petronius Valens filius et Petronia Marcia nepotia pietatis causa posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201352.translation: Lucius Petronius Valens, the son, and Petronia Marcia, the granddaughter, are buried here; they placed this out of piety.
EDCS-11201352.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Petronius Valens and his granddaughter Petronia Marcia, erected by them, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201353.text_edition: \n Posis Mestylu sic filius Flavia Delus Mucati filia domo Albanopoli ipsa Delus \n
EDCS-11201353.translation: Posis Mestylu, son of [---], and Flavia Delus, daughter of Mucatus, from the city of Albanopolis, she herself [---].
EDCS-11201353.summary: An incomplete funerary inscription recording individuals Posis Mestylu and Flavia Delus, with an indication of their place of origin, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201354.text_edition: \n Quintus Satrius Ferox vixit annis III hic situs est Saturninus et Felicula faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201354.translation: Quintus Satrius Ferox lived for 3 years; he is buried here. Saturninus and Felicula saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201354.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of a child, Quintus Satrius Ferox, with his parents named as the dedicator.

EDCS-11201355.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Sollius Primus vixit annos XXIII hic situs est Quintus Sollius Surus nepoti bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-11201355.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Quintus Sollius Primus lived for 23 years; he is buried here. Quintus Sollius Surus made this for his deserving grandson.
EDCS-11201355.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Quintus Sollius Primus, erected by his grandfather.

EDCS-11201356.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Teufia Nice Teufi Valeri liberta vixit annos LXXX hic sita est Teufia Valeria quae et Ingenua filia faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201356.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Teufia Nice, freedwoman of Teufius Valerius, lived for 80 years; she is buried here. Teufia Valeria, who is also called Ingenua, her daughter, saw to its construction.
EDCS-11201356.summary: A funerary epitaph for Teufia Nice, a freedwoman, with her daughter Teufia Valeria as the dedicator.

EDCS-11201357.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Semele vixit annis XL Epaphroditus coniugi \n
EDCS-11201357.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valeria Semele lived for 40 years. Epaphroditus [made this] for his wife.
EDCS-11201357.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Valeria Semele, with her husband Epaphroditus as the dedicator.

EDCS-11201358.text_edition: \n Valerius Faustus vixit nnos II et Aelia Chr<y=U>sis filio pientissimo faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-11201358.translation: Valerius Faustus lived for 2 years and Aelia Chysis saw to its construction for her most dutiful son.
EDCS-11201358.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Faustus, with his mother Aelia Chysis as the dedicator.

EDCS-11201359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Valeri Pudentis qui vixit nnos XXV et Luci Valeri Aeliani qui vixit nnos VI Lucius Valerius Peculiaris et Vivia Sabina parentes\n
EDCS-11201359.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Lucius Valerius Pudentis, who lived for 25 years, and for Lucius Valerius Aelianus, who lived for 6 years. Lucius Valerius Peculiaris and Vivia Sabina, parents [made this].
EDCS-11201359.summary: A funerary epitaph for two individuals, likely father and son, erected by the parents.

EDCS-11201362.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaius Venuleius Arator vixit nnos LV Valeria Restuta vixit annos XL hic siti sunt Gai Venulei Herculanus Persinus Silvanus fili parentibus pientissimis faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201362.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Venuleius Arator lived for 55 years. Valeria Restuta lived for 40 years. Gaius Venuleius Herculanus and Persinus Silvanus, sons, saw to its construction for their most dutiful parents.
EDCS-11201362.summary: A funerary epitaph for two individuals, likely spouses, erected by their sons.

EDCS-11201363.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus V Hyperephanus vixit annos XXXI hic situs est Tyche coniugi karissimo ponendum c(uravit)\n
EDCS-11201363.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. V. Hyperephanus lived for 31 years; he is buried here. Tyche saw to the placing of this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-11201363.summary: A funerary epitaph for V. Hyperephanus, erected by his wife Tyche.

EDCS-11201364.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Mamae vixit annos XXXV Caius Ulpius Aphr<o>ditus coniugi bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201364.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Ulpia Mame, who lived for 35 years. Caius Ulpius Aphroditus saw to its construction for his deserving wife.
EDCS-11201364.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpia Mame, erected by her husband Caius Ulpius Aphroditus.

EDCS-11201365.text_edition: \n sanius ercus vixit nnos XXXII hic situs est sania genia pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-11201365.translation: Sanius Herclus lived for 32 years; he is buried here. Sania Genia placed this for her most dutiful [son/husband].
EDCS-11201365.summary: A funerary epitaph for Sanius Herclus, with Sania Genia as the dedicator, likely a close relative.

EDCS-11201371.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication likely naming Lucius Vatis Ae.

EDCS-11201379.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIII consuli III Lucio Coelio Rufo legato Augusti pro praetore colonia Scupinorum milia passuum VIIII\n
EDCS-11201379.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, by Lucius Coelius Rufus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, 9 miles [from the colony of] Scupinorum.
EDCS-11201379.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording a road repair or construction by Lucius Coelius Rufus on behalf of Emperor Hadrian, dated to Hadrian's 4th tribunician power (around 120 AD).

EDCS-11201380.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani nepos divi Nervae pronepos Antoninus Augustus Pius restituit milia passuum VIIII\n
EDCS-11201380.translation: Emperor Caesar Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, restored 9 miles [from the colony of] Scupinorum.
EDCS-11201380.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording a road repair by Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-11201381.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto Parthico Maximo tribunicia potestate et Otaciliae Sseverae Augustae mille passus\n
EDCS-11201381.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power, and to Otacilia Severa Augusta, [for the distance of] 1000 paces.
EDCS-11201381.summary: This is a fragmentary milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab and Empress Otacilia Severa, dating to Philip's reign (244-249 AD).

EDCS-11201384.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto patri patriae consuli semel res publica coloniae Scupinorum milia passus sic XX\n
EDCS-11201384.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, father of the country, consul once, the republic of the colony of Scupinorum [dedicates this for] 20 paces.
EDCS-11201384.summary: This is a milestone inscription recording a road repair or distance marker of 20 paces, dedicated by the republic of the colony of Scupinorum to Emperor Trajan Decius, dating to his consulship (250-251 AD).

EDCS-11201387.summary: This is a fragmentary votive inscription dedicated to Apollo by someone named Apollonides.

EDCS-11201388.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto pro salute Auggustorum nnostrorum templum vetustate dilapsum i<m=N>pendio suo restituit Apollonides eorundem servus scrutator stationis Lamud Gentiano et Basso consulibus \n
EDCS-11201388.translation: Apollonides, his slave, a scrutator of the station Lamud, restored the temple of the Unconquered God, which had fallen into ruin from age, with his own expense, for the safety of our emperors, in the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus.
EDCS-11201388.summary: This is a dedication for the restoration of a temple to the Unconquered God by Apollonides, a slave and official, for the emperors' safety, dated by the consuls to 133 AD.

EDCS-11201389.text_edition: \n Fano Magno Severus sacerdos votum posuit ut antiquam legem Augusti servemus\n
EDCS-11201389.translation: Severus, the priest, placed this vow in the Great Shrine, that we may observe the ancient law of Augustus.
EDCS-11201389.summary: This is a votive inscription by a priest named Severus in a 'Great Shrine', expressing a desire to follow the 'ancient law of Augustus'.

EDCS-11201390.text_edition: \n Pro salute Auggustorum Valeri Valentis ex voto posuit Epicadus sacerdos praedicabilis antiquam legem sequamur\n
EDCS-11201390.translation: For the safety of our emperors, Epicadus, priest, praedicabilis, placed this vow, that we may follow the ancient law, from the vow of Valerius Valentis.
EDCS-11201390.summary: This is a votive inscription by Epicadus, a priest, for the safety of the emperors and to follow an 'ancient law', referencing a vow made by Valerius Valentis.

EDCS-11201391.text_edition: \n Deo invicto Mithrae fano magno pro salute Auggustorum nn(ostrorum) Apollonides eorundem vectigalis Illyrici servus |contrascriptor stationis Lamud quam voverat |contrascriptor stationis Viziani Apollonidess Viziani Gentiano et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-11201391.translation: Apollonides, a slave of the same [emperors], a vectigalis of Illyricum, a contrascriptor of the station Lamud, and Apollonides of Viziani, a contrascriptor of the station Viziani, [placed this dedication] to the Unconquered God Mithras in the Great Shrine, for the safety of our emperors, which they had vowed, in the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus.
EDCS-11201391.summary: This is a complex votive inscription dedicated to Mithras in a 'Great Shrine' by two officials named Apollonides for the safety of the emperors, dated by the consuls to 133 AD.

EDCS-11201392.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae Publius Aelius Ballista eques Romanus a militiis pro se suisque votum solvit\n
EDCS-11201392.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, and Minerva, Publius Aelius Ballista, a Roman knight of equestrian rank, fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-11201392.summary: This is a dedication to the Capitoline Triad by Publius Aelius Ballista, a Roman knight, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-11201397.text_edition: \n NK S Gemelinus Dardanos <f=E>ines derexit Q \n
EDCS-11201397.translation: NK S Gemelinus Dardanos Eines derexit Q
EDCS-11201397.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that records a person named Gemelinus Dardanos and a verb, its precise nature is unclear.

EDCS-11201398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Augustalis vixit annos LXXX hic situs est sodales Platiae Superae per stinum libertum bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201398.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Titus Flavius Augustalis lived for 80 years and is buried here. His colleagues from Platiae Superae, through their freedman Peristinus, erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201398.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Titus Flavius Augustalis, erected by his colleagues, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201399.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iucunda Bithi filia vixit nnos L hic sita est Abascantos sic libertus patronae bene merenti posuit Abascantus Iucundae libertus vivo se\n
EDCS-11201399.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Iucunda, daughter of Bithus, lived for 50 years and is buried here. Abascantus, her freedman, erected this for his patroness, who deserved well. Abascantus, Iucunda's freedman, while alive...
EDCS-11201399.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Iucunda, erected by her freedman Abascantus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201400.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Caius vixit annos L hic situs est sodales Platiae Superae per Iulium Castum filium bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201400.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Iulius Caius lived for 50 years and is buried here. His colleagues from Platiae Superae, through his son Iulius Castus, erected this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201400.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Iulius Caius, erected by his colleagues and son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201401.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius L<i=E>bonius Catus vixit nnos L hic situs est Libonia \n
EDCS-11201401.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Caius Libonius Catus lived for 50 years and is buried here. Libonia [erected this].
EDCS-11201401.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Caius Libonius Catus, erected by Libonia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201402.text_edition: \n Publicia Callirhoe vixit annos L hic sita est Lucius Viracius Fortis coniugi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201402.translation: Publicia Callirhoe lived for 50 years and is buried here. Lucius Viracius Fortis arranged for this to be made for his wife.
EDCS-11201402.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Publicia Callirhoe, erected by her husband Lucius Viracius Fortis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201403.translation: Tetricus arranged for this to be made for his mother.
EDCS-11201403.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording that Tetricus arranged for the tomb to be made for his mother, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201404.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Varanus miles legionis IIII Flaviae librarius consularis vixi<t=X> annis XXXV Sta<t=L>ius NALE bene merenti \n
EDCS-11201404.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Marcus Valerius Varanus, a soldier of the Legio IIII Flaviae, a consular clerk, lived for 35 years. Statius NALE [erected this] for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201404.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Marcus Valerius Varanus, a soldier and clerk, erected by Statius NALE, likely from the late 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201405.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Viracius Fortis vixit nnos LXX hic situs est Lucius Viracius Fortis et Viracia Ingenua patri bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201405.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Lucius Viracius Fortis lived for 70 years and is buried here. Lucius Viracius Fortis and Viracia Ingenua arranged for this to be made for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11201405.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Lucius Viracius Fortis, erected by his son and daughter, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201409.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Pudens, though the exact duration is missing.

EDCS-11201410.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Bellus |(centurio) cohortis primae T<hr=RH>acum veteranus cohortis eiusdem vixit annis LX hic situs est Iulius Gallus Lumia Andia Dasi filia fratres et heredes mei faciendum curaverunt \n
EDCS-11201410.translation: Marcus Iulius Bellus, centurion of the First Cohort of Thracians and veteran of the same cohort, lived for 60 years. He is buried here. His brothers and heirs, Iulius Gallus, Lumia Andia, and Dasi, had this made.
EDCS-11201410.summary: This funerary inscription for Marcus Iulius Bellus, a centurion and veteran, indicates he lived to 60 and was buried here, with his heirs commissioning the monument.

EDCS-11201411.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pamphorus hic situs est vixit annos XVI Heracla et Zoe et Pamphorus cognominato posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201411.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Pamphorus is buried here. He lived for 16 years. Heracla, Zoe, and Pamphorus, nicknamed, set this up.
EDCS-11201411.summary: This funerary inscription honors Pamphorus, who died at 16, and was erected by Heracla, Zoe, and another Pamphorus.

EDCS-11201417.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Maximus Timentis hic situs est vixit annos LX\n
EDCS-11201417.translation: Marcus Ulpius Maximus Timentis is buried here. He lived for 60 years.
EDCS-11201417.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Marcus Ulpius Maximus Timentis, who lived to be 60.

EDCS-11201418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Severa vixit annos XXXV Marcus Ulpius Bassus miles legionis IIII Flaviae Antoninianae strator legati coniugi pientissimae posuit manu mea scribsi sic\n
EDCS-11201418.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Severa lived for 35 years. Marcus Ulpius Bassus, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Antoninian Legion, set this up for his most devoted wife, the strator of the legate. I wrote this with my own hand.
EDCS-11201418.summary: This funerary inscription honors Flavia Severa, who died at 35, and was erected by her husband, Marcus Ulpius Bassus, a soldier and aide to a legate.

EDCS-11201419.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Maximianus vixit annis LX Cocceia Sabina vixit annis XXX mater Maximina piissimis bene merentibus fecit\n
EDCS-11201419.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Maximianus lived for 60 years. Cocceia Sabina lived for 30 years. Their mother Maximina made this for her most devoted children who deserved well.
EDCS-11201419.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Publius Aelius Maximianus and Cocceia Sabina, who died at 60 and 30 respectively, and was erected by their mother Maximina.

EDCS-11201422.text_edition: \n Gaius Iulius Titanus sta dum venio et cum venero stabes\n
EDCS-11201422.translation: Gaius Iulius Titanus, stand there until I come, and when I come, you will stand.
EDCS-11201422.summary: This inscription appears to be a playful or perhaps a curse directed at Gaius Iulius Titanus, instructing him to remain in place.

EDCS-11201423.text_edition: \n Caius Granonius Quinti filius hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201423.translation: Caius Granonius, son of Quintus, is buried here.
EDCS-11201423.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription identifying Caius Granonius, son of Quintus, as being buried at this location.

EDCS-11201425.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cilices pro salute dddominorum nnnostrorum posuerunt contirones Dextro et Prisco consulibus\n
EDCS-11201425.translation: The Cilices set this up for the safety of our lords, the contirones [recruits/new soldiers] Dextrus and Priscus, when Dextrus and Priscus were consuls.
