EDCS-11201425.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the Cilices (possibly a group or tribe) for the safety of their leaders, Dextrus and Priscus, during their consulship.

EDCS-11201426.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Severi Alexandri Augusti Claudius Lupercus miles legionis IIII Flaviae Severianae Alexandrianae ex voto posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201426.translation: Claudius Lupercus, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Severan Alexandrian Legion, set this up as a votive offering, willingly and deservedly, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of our lord Severus Alexander Augustus.
EDCS-11201426.summary: This is a votive inscription by Claudius Lupercus, a soldier in the Fourth Flavian Legion, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the well-being of Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-11201427.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum nomine suo Aemilius Dolens optio\n
EDCS-11201427.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, in his own name, Aemilius Dolens, optio.
EDCS-11201427.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by an optio named Aemilius Dolens.

EDCS-11201428.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Barbius Ulpianus cornicularius legionis IIII Flaviae Felicis\n
EDCS-11201428.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Barbius Ulpianus, cornicularius of the Fourth Flavian Legion, Felix.
EDCS-11201428.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Barbius Ulpianus, a cornicularius of the Fourth Flavian Legion.

EDCS-11201429.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Aurelius Crescens votum posuit libens merito Probo consule\n
EDCS-11201429.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno, Aurelius Crescens erected this votive offering willingly and deservedly, under the consulship of Probus.
EDCS-11201429.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno by Aurelius Crescens, dating to the consulship of Probus.

EDCS-11201430.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Aurelius Crescentio lapidarius pro salute sua et suorum\n
EDCS-11201430.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno, Aurelius Crescentio, a stonecutter, for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-11201430.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno by Aurelius Crescentio, a stonecutter, for the health of himself and his family.

EDCS-11201431.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Titus Aurelius Lnus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201431.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno, Titus Aurelius Lnus erected this as a votive offering.
EDCS-11201431.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paterno by Titus Aurelius Lnus.

EDCS-11201432.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunonique Minervae Publius Terentius Aquilinus et Triitenica Sabina conssistentes Aquileiae votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-11201432.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, and Minerva, Publius Terentius Aquilinus and Triitenica Sabina, as residents of Aquileia, fulfilled their votive offering willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11201432.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva by Publius Terentius Aquilinus and Triitenica Sabina in Aquileia.

EDCS-11201433.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Terrae Matri Libero Patri et Lib<e=I>rae sacrum Titus Aurelius Atticus veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae ex signifero praefecti kastrorum quinquennalis Singiduni decurio coloniae Sirmiensium votum libens merito posuit \n
EDCS-11201433.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and Mother Earth, Liber Pater, and Libera; Titus Aurelius Atticus, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, formerly a signifer under the prefect of the camp, a quinquennalis, and a decurion of the colony of Sirmium, erected this votive offering willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11201433.summary: This inscription records a comprehensive dedication to multiple deities by Titus Aurelius Atticus, a veteran with various military and civic roles, in Singidunum.

EDCS-11201435.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that begins with the name Aelius Valentinus, likely a dedication or a funerary record.

EDCS-11201436.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Aufidius Ingenuus optio D P \n
EDCS-11201436.translation: To the August Nemesis, Aufidius Ingenuus, optio, willingly placed this offering.
EDCS-11201436.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Aufidius Ingenuus, an optio.

EDCS-11201437.text_edition: \n Paterno Aurelius Diogenes ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201437.translation: Aurelius Diogenes erected this as a votive offering.
EDCS-11201437.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Paterno made by Aurelius Diogenes.

EDCS-11201439.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Diogeniae vixit nnos IIII et Aurelio Diogeniano vixit annum I Aurelius Demetrius strator consularis legionis IIII Flaviae et Valeria Marcella filiis karissimis et Aurelio Eumeneti stratori legati legionis eiusdem vixit annos XL contirunculo fratri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201439.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Diogenia lived 4 years and Aurelius Diogenianus lived 1 year. Aurelius Demetrius, orderly of the consular legate of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and Valeria Marcella placed this for their dearest sons and for Aurelius Eumenes, orderly of the legate of the same legion, who lived 40 years, and for their deserving brother.
EDCS-11201439.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aurelius Demetrius and Valeria Marcella for their two young sons, Aurelius Eumenes, and a brother, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201440.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Papiae veterano legionis IIII Flaviae ex beneficiario legati vixit annos LXX Aelia Iusta coniux et Aurelia Iustina filia Aelius Herculanus custos armorum legionis eiusdem gener bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201440.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Aurelius Papias, veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, from the staff of the legate, who lived 70 years. Aelia Iusta his wife and Aurelia Iustina his daughter, and Aelius Herculanus, armourer of the same legion, placed this for their deserving benefactor.
EDCS-11201440.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Aurelius Papias, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and was set up by his wife, daughter, and a fellow soldier, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201441.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus perpetuae securitati Marco Ulpio Manliano veterano legionis IIII Flaviae ex beneficiario tribuni vixit nnos XLIIII Aelia Flora coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201441.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for perpetual security. For Marcus Ulpius Manlianus, veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, from the staff of a tribune, who lived 44 years. Aelia Flora placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-11201441.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Aelia Flora for her husband, Marcus Ulpius Manlianus, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201442.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpio Valentino vixit annos III Publius Aelius Valens beneficiarius praefecti filio carissimo\n
EDCS-11201442.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpius Valentinus, who lived 3 years. Publius Aelius Valens, staff member of the prefect, placed this for his dearest son.
EDCS-11201442.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young child, Ulpius Valentinus, and was set up by his father, Publius Aelius Valens, a staff member of a prefect, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201443.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Albanius Luci filius Flavia Septiminus Sirmio imaginifer legionis IIII Flaviae stipendiorum XXIIII iussu ipsius sepulcrum Caius Titius Crescens veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae Lucius Albanius Hermes et Philomusus Andronici heredes fecerunt\n
EDCS-11201443.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Albanius, son of Lucius, of the Flavia family, from Sirmio, standard-bearer of the Fourth Flavian Legion, with 24 years of service, by his own order. The tomb was made by Caius Titius Crescens, veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and by Lucius Albanius Hermes and Philomusus, heirs of Andronicus.
EDCS-11201443.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tomb ordered by Lucius Albanius, a standard-bearer of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and completed by a veteran and the heirs of another, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201444.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crescens Scupis vet(eranus) legionis IIII Flaviae felicis ex equite militavit annos XXVI vixit annos XLV vixit annos XLV Petronia Secunda heres coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201444.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Crescens Scupis, veteran of the lucky Fourth Flavian Legion, from the cavalry, he served 26 years, he lived 45 years. Petronia Secunda, his heir, placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-11201444.summary: This is a funerary monument for Crescens Scupis, a cavalry veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, erected by his wife and heir, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201445.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cassio Ingenuo militi legionis IIII Flaviae qui vixit nnos XL Cassia Atta fratri bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201445.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Cassius Ingenuus, soldier of the Fourth Flavian Legion, who lived 40 years. Cassia Atta placed this for her deserving brother.
EDCS-11201445.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Cassius Ingenuus, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and was set up by his sister, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201446.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Victoria vixit nnos L Aurelius Mercator veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae Antoninianae coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201446.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Valeria Victoria, who lived 50 years. Aurelius Mercator, veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion Antoniniana, placed this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-11201446.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Valeria Victoria and was set up by her husband, Aurelius Mercator, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201447.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus sacrum Aurelius Victor veteranus et Aurelia Victoria coniuges vivi sibi posuerunt titulo suo sic et Aurelio Alpino patri genetivo qui vixit nnos XC et Publio Aurelio Alumno qui vixit annos et Aurelio Apro vixit annos CEFIA ET ICVS annonario posuit\n
EDCS-11201447.translation: Sacred to the Infernal Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Victor and Aurelia Victoria, husband and wife, placed this for themselves while alive, with this inscription: also for Aurelius Alpinus, father by birth, who lived 90 years, and for Publius Aurelius Alumnus, who lived [XX] years, and for Aurelius Afer, who lived [XX] years, the annonary.
EDCS-11201447.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument established by Aurelius Victor and Aurelia Victoria, which also includes provisions for their deceased father Aurelius Alpinus and two others, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201448.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Apphion vixit nnos II menses VIII Caius Valerius Valens veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae et Valeria Elpis parentes filiae carissima[e ------\n
EDCS-11201448.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Valeria Apphion, who lived 2 years and 8 months. Caius Valerius Valens, veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and Valeria Elpis, parents, for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-11201448.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of a young girl, Valeria Apphion, and was set up by her parents, Caius Valerius Valens, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, and Valeria Elpis, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201450.text_edition: \n Dibus Manibus Infernis Nunnio Prisciano decurioni coloniae Singiduni qui vixit nnos XXXVI non dignus c<e=I>lerius inf<r=T>a decessit Aurelius Priscillianus et Septimia Lupercilla \n
EDCS-11201450.translation: To the underworld gods. Nunnio Priscianus, decurion of the colony of Singidunum, who lived 36 years, not unworthy, died. Aurelius Priscillianus and Septimia Lupercilla [placed this].
EDCS-11201450.summary: This funerary inscription honors Nunnio Priscianus, a decurion at Singidunum, set up by Aurelius Priscillianus and Septimia Lupercilla.

EDCS-11201453.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Domitia Ursa vixit nnos XXV Albanius Septiminus hospitae bene merenti posuit hic sita est sit tibi terra levis Dis Manibus Sereniae Quartae interfectae ab hoste vixit nnos L Cocceius Montanus tutor Titianus scribendum curavit\n
EDCS-11201453.translation: To the underworld gods. Domitia Ursa lived 25 years. Albanius Septiminus set this up for his well-deserving hostess; she is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you. To the underworld gods. Serenia Quarta, killed by an enemy, lived 50 years. Cocceius Montanus, her guardian, Titianus, saw to the inscription.
EDCS-11201453.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Domitia Ursa, honored by her hostess, and Serenia Quarta, killed by an enemy, with her guardian seeing to the inscription.

EDCS-11201454.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Postumio Cornuto et Postumiae Caleni Quinto Postumio Felici ti Crescentioni IA qui parentibus\n
EDCS-11201454.translation: To the underworld gods. For Quintus Postumius Cornutus and Postumia Caleni, and Quintus Postumius Felix, and [?] Crescentio, who [set this up] for his parents.
EDCS-11201454.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Quintus Postumius Cornutus, Postumia Caleni, Quintus Postumius Felix, and Crescentio, possibly by a son.

EDCS-11201455.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaius Valerius Maximianus vixit annos XIII Valerius Longinus pater filio bene merito\n
EDCS-11201455.translation: To the underworld gods. Gaius Valerius Maximianus lived 13 years. Valerius Longinus, his father, for his well-deserving son, placed this.
EDCS-11201455.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Valerius Maximianus, who died at 13, set up by his father, Valerius Longinus.

EDCS-11201456.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Asc Leuc Valerius N annos IV\n
EDCS-11201456.translation: To the underworld gods. Publius Asc[?] Leuc[?] Valerius N[?] [lived?] 4 years.
EDCS-11201456.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Publius Asc Leuc Valerius N., who died at the age of 4.

EDCS-11201462.translation: [He] made [this] with his own money for his son.
EDCS-11201462.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a son commissioning a monument with his own funds for his father.

EDCS-11201463.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudius Valentinianus eques Romanus decurio IIIIvir municipii Apulensis Aureliae Probillae coniugi carissimae quae vixit annos XXVIIII bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201463.translation: To the underworld gods. For Claudius Valentinianus, Roman knight, decurion and IIIIvir of the municipality of Apula, and for Aurelia Probilla, his dearest wife, who lived 29 years, a well-deserving woman, he set this up.
EDCS-11201463.summary: This funerary inscription honors Claudius Valentinianus, a Roman knight and magistrate, and his wife Aurelia Probilla.

EDCS-11201464.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Valentinus vixit nnis L vixit nnis LX ius bene merenti posuit Lucilla liberorum III coniux Aureli vixit annis XXX\n
EDCS-11201464.translation: To the underworld gods. Aurelius Valentinus lived 50 years. [He] lived 60 years. [He] set this up for [himself?]. Lucilla, his wife, with three children, Aurelius, lived 30 years.
EDCS-11201464.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelius Valentinus, who lived either 50 or 60 years, and his wife Lucilla, who lived 30 years.

EDCS-11201465.translation: Marcus Ulpius Thallus placed this for the god.
EDCS-11201465.summary: This votive inscription records Marcus Ulpius Thallus setting up a dedication to a god.

EDCS-11201466.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute sua et suorum Aurelius Marcellinus vir egregius voto posuit\n
EDCS-11201466.translation: For Jupiter Best and Greatest, for his own health and that of his family, Aurelius Marcellinus, an excellent man, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11201466.summary: This votive inscription records Aurelius Marcellinus dedicating a monument to Jupiter for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-11201467.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno M V |(centurio) legionis \n
EDCS-11201467.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, a centurion of the legion.
EDCS-11201467.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by a centurion of a Roman legion.

EDCS-11201470.text_edition: \n Dei in domu Hermogenes votum fecit\n
EDCS-11201470.translation: Hermogenes made a vow to the god in his house.
EDCS-11201470.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Hermogenes to a deity in his home.

EDCS-11201471.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Julia Flora.

EDCS-11201472.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Agellius Zoticus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201472.translation: Gaius Agellius Zoticus willingly and deservedly paid a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-11201472.summary: This inscription records Gaius Agellius Zoticus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.

EDCS-11201473.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ediuna frater confratribus\n
EDCS-11201473.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Ediuna for himself and his brothers for his fellow brothers.
EDCS-11201473.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest made by Ediuna on behalf of himself and his fellow brothers.

EDCS-11201474.text_edition: \n Iovi sacrum Iuppiter Crescens Mucati votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201474.translation: Sacred to Jupiter. Iuppiter Crescens Mucati willingly and deservedly paid a vow.
EDCS-11201474.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication and the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Iuppiter Crescens Mucati.

EDCS-11201475.text_edition: \n Iuppiter Auguste Dines Celsi votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-11201475.translation: Dines Celsi willingly paid a vow to Jupiter Augustus.
EDCS-11201475.summary: This inscription records Dines Celsi fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Augustus.

EDCS-11201476.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Sextilianus \n
EDCS-11201476.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Sextilianus.
EDCS-11201476.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Publius Aelius Sextilianus.

EDCS-11201477.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius.
EDCS-11201477.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Aurelius.

EDCS-11201478.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iuppiter Marcus Aurelius Crescentio p<r=P>(o) se et suos sic et cives sic votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-11201478.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Iuppiter Marcus Aurelius Crescentio for himself and his family, and likewise for the citizens, willingly and deservedly paid a vow.
EDCS-11201478.summary: This inscription records Iuppiter Marcus Aurelius Crescentio fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest on behalf of himself, his family, and the citizens.

EDCS-11201479.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Bitio B<i=P>ti libens vo<t=P>um solvit \n
EDCS-11201479.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Bitio and Boetius willingly paid their vow.
EDCS-11201479.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Bitio and Boetius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201480.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Vitalis pro (se) et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201480.translation: Vitalis willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for himself and his family.
EDCS-11201480.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Vitalis for his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201482.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Sentia Flaventilla votum merito libens solvit\n
EDCS-11201482.translation: Paterno and Sentia Flaventilla willingly and deservedly paid their vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-11201482.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Paterno and Sentia Flaventilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201483.text_edition: \n Deo Mithrae S(oli) pro salute Imperatoris Severi Alexandri Augusti Gaius C---ius Marcellus et Marius Victorinus posuerunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-11201483.translation: Gaius C---ius Marcellus and Marius Victorinus willingly and deservedly erected this monument to the god Sol Mithras for the safety of Emperor Severus Alexander Augustus.
EDCS-11201483.summary: This is a dedication to Sol Mithras for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander by Gaius C---ius Marcellus and Marius Victorinus, dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-11201485.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto Conservatori Marcus Ulpius Terentius cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201485.translation: Marcus Ulpius Terentius willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Augustus the Protector, along with his family.
EDCS-11201485.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Augustus by Marcus Ulpius Terentius and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201487.text_edition: \n Caius Cetroniusanus com votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201487.translation: Caius Cetroniusanus willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-11201487.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Cetroniusanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201488.text_edition: \n Marcianus cohortis II Aureliae novae |(miliariae) equitatae Antoninianae votum dedit et votum solvit\n
EDCS-11201488.translation: Marcianus of the II Aurelia Nova milliaria equitata Antoniniana cohort gave and paid his vow.
EDCS-11201488.summary: This is a military dedication by Marcianus of the II Aurelia Nova milliaria equitata Antoniniana cohort, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11201489.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Ascius vixit nnos C et Iulia Proculina vixit nnos XL Licinia Bonosa coniugi et filiae carissimis et Iulio Iuliano genero veterano alae Itureorum qui vixit nnos LX et sibi viva posuit\n
EDCS-11201489.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Iulius Ascius lived 101 years and Iulia Proculina lived 40 years. Licinia Bonosa erected this for her dearest husband and daughter, and for her son-in-law Iulius Iulianus, a veteran of the ala Itureorum who lived 60 years, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-11201489.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Licinia Bonosa for her family members and herself, recording their lifespans and military service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201490.text_edition: \n Valetudinarium cohortis II Aureliae novae |(miliariae) equitatae civium Romanorum Titus Bebenius Iustus praefectus Imperatore Commodo II et Vero II consulibus\n
EDCS-11201490.translation: The hospital of the II Aurelia Nova milliaria equitata civium Romanorum cohort, built by Titus Bebenius Iustus, prefect, when Commodus and Verus were consuls for the second time.
EDCS-11201490.summary: This inscription records the construction of a hospital (valetudinarium) for the II Aurelia Nova cohort by its prefect Titus Bebenius Iustus, dating to the consulship of Commodus and Verus, likely 179 AD.

EDCS-11201491.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Casta vixit nnos XXXII et Aurelia Flora vixit nnos L Aurelius Acutio miles cohortis II Aureliae novae coniugi et matri bene merentis sic et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-11201491.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Claudia Casta lived 32 years and Aurelia Flora lived 50 years. Aurelius Acutius, a soldier of the II Aurelia Nova cohort, made this for his deserving wife and mother, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-11201491.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aurelius Acutius for his wife, mother, and himself, recording their lifespans and his military service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201492.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Victorinus veteranus cohortis II Aureliae novae vixit nnos LX Au<r>elia Rufina coniugi bene merenti et Publius Aelius Acutianus et Publius Aelius Victorinus patri et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201492.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Aelius Victorinus, a veteran of the Second Cohort Aurelia Nova, lived 60 years. Aurelia Rufina, for her well-deserving husband, and Publius Aelius Acutianus and Publius Aelius Victorinus, for their father and for themselves, erected this while alive.
EDCS-11201492.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Aelius Victorinus, a veteran, erected by his wife and sons.

EDCS-11201493.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Luciae vixit annis XLV Aurelius Bardibalus decurio collegii centonariorum coniugi et Aurelius Lucilius matri bene merenti et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201493.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aelia Lucia lived 45 years. Aurelius Bardibalus, a decurion of the guild of clothiers, for his wife, and Aurelius Lucilius, for his well-deserving mother and for himself, erected this while alive.
EDCS-11201493.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelia Lucia, erected by her husband Aurelius Bardibalus and son Aurelius Lucilius.

EDCS-11201494.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelii Vitalis vixit annos XXVII et Firmus vixit nnos XXV et Marcus Aurelius Respectus et Aurelia Veneria filis bene merentibus posuerunt et sibi vivis posuerunt cum Marcis Aureliis Respectiano et Hilerione sic filiis pientissimis\n
EDCS-11201494.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelius Vitalis lived 27 years, and Firmus lived 25 years. Marcus Aurelius Respectus and Aurelia Veneria erected this for their well-deserving sons, and for themselves while alive, together with their most devoted sons Marcus Aurelius Respectianus and Hilerio.
EDCS-11201494.summary: This is a tombstone for Aurelius Vitalis and Firmus, erected by their parents Marcus Aurelius Respectus and Aurelia Veneria, who also provided for themselves and their other sons.

EDCS-11201495.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Chrysophoro et Primitivae Adiiutoris sic servis tus fratris sic bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11201495.translation: To the Divine Spirits. [He/She] placed this for Chrysophorus and Primitiva, slaves of his brother Adiiutor, well-deserving.
EDCS-11201495.summary: This tombstone records the dedication of Chrysophorus and Primitiva, slaves, by their owner, Adiiutor.

EDCS-11201496.text_edition: \n Publio Covirio Gentili Trevero Publius Covirius Optatus frater fecit\n
EDCS-11201496.translation: Publius Covirius Optatus erected this for his brother Publius Covirius Gentilis of Trier.
EDCS-11201496.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Publius Covirius Optatus for his brother, Publius Covirius Gentilis.

EDCS-11201498.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Embasia Antoni filia vicsit sic annis XX posuit Antonius Germani filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-11201498.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Embasia, daughter of Antonius, lived 20 years. Antonius erected this for his well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-11201498.summary: This tombstone records the death of Embasia, daughter of Antonius, and was erected by her father.

EDCS-11201499.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Quinti filio Sergia Rufo Liburno Flavia Marulla coniugi pientissimo vixit annos LXI hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201499.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Julius, son of Quintus Liburnus. Flavia Marulla erected this for her most devoted husband, who lived 61 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-11201499.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Julius, erected by his wife Flavia Marulla.

EDCS-11201500.text_edition: \n Iulius nio posuit sibi et coniugi Pusiae pientissimae et filiae Primitivae karissimae et Diophaneti Dionisi sic genero pio vixit nns XX\n
EDCS-11201500.translation: Iulius erected this for himself and for his most devoted wife Pusia, and for his dearest daughter Primitiva, and for Diophanes, son-in-law of Dionysius, a devoted son-in-law who lived 20 years.
EDCS-11201500.summary: This is a family tombstone set up by Iulius for himself, his wife, daughter, and son-in-law.

EDCS-11201501.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Venusta vixit annos LXX Demetrius \n
EDCS-11201501.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valeria Venusta lived 70 years. Demetrius [erected this].
EDCS-11201501.summary: This tombstone records the death of Valeria Venusta and was likely erected by Demetrius.

EDCS-11201502.text_edition: \n ------ S]uavis Diniceni vixit nnos LX Rhodope Valentis coniugi et sibi viva et Valerius Valens socro bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201502.translation: [She] lived 60 years. Rhodope, for her well-deserving husband Valentis, and for herself while alive, and Valerius Valens, for his well-deserving mother-in-law, erected this.
EDCS-11201502.summary: This tombstone records the death of Diniceni, erected by his wife Rhodope and his father-in-law Valerius Valens.

EDCS-11201503.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Flavius Martinus.
EDCS-11201503.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Flavius Martinus, likely a tombstone inscription from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201504.text_edition: \n I Cornelio Marci filio IV O Iulia Secunda coniugi pientissimo et filio \n
EDCS-11201504.translation: To Julia Secunda, wife of Cornelius Marci, son of I, most devoted, and to her son.
EDCS-11201504.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by I. Cornelius Marci to his devoted wife Julia Secunda and their son, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201505.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus M Pra nnrum XX Ianuarius AVIS \n
EDCS-11201505.translation: To the spirits of the departed, M. Pra[---]nnum XX. Ianuarius AVIS.
EDCS-11201505.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription, likely honoring Ianuarius AVIS, and possibly mentioning a length of life or age.

EDCS-11201524.summary: This is a name inscription, possibly a tombstone or marker for Gaius Julius Maximus from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201526.translation: Approved by Aurelianus, inspector of the Augusti.
EDCS-11201526.summary: This inscription records the approval of an item or work by Aurelianus, an inspector of the Augusti, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201527.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Pinnes Dasi vixit annos L Ceuna coniunx vixit annos XL Sever Proradi vixit annos L Aurelii Dasius Licinius Maxima parentibus pro pietate posuerunt hic situs est!\n
EDCS-11201527.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Pinnes Dasi lived 50 years. Ceuna his wife lived 40 years. Sever Proradi lived 50 years. Aurelii Dasius and Licinius Maxima set this up for their parents out of devotion; here he lies!
EDCS-11201527.summary: This extensive funerary inscription records the lives of Aurelius Pinnes Dasi, his wife Ceuna, and Sever Proradi, and states it was erected by their children Aurelii Dasius and Licinius Maxima in honor of their parents.

EDCS-11201529.translation: Sacred to the god [---] of Victoris.
EDCS-11201529.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicating something sacred to a god associated with Victoris.

EDCS-11201536.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno Publius Aelius Hermes pro salute sua suorumque votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201536.translation: To the Eternal God, Publius Aelius Hermes fulfilled his vow freely and deservedly for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-11201536.summary: This is a votive inscription by Publius Aelius Hermes to the Eternal God, offered for his well-being and that of his household.

EDCS-11201537.text_edition: \n Diis Angelis Marcus Aurelius Celsus votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201537.translation: To the gods and angels, Marcus Aurelius Celsus set up his vow freely and deservedly.
EDCS-11201537.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Celsus to gods and angels, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11201538.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Ceres, addressed as 'our August Empress', likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11201539.text_edition: \n Domino et Dominae sacrum Crescentilla votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201539.translation: Sacred to the Lord and Lady. Crescentilla gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-11201539.summary: This is a dedication to the deities Lord and Lady by Crescentilla, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201542.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Herculi |(centuriae) VIIII principis prioris Ulpius Valens optio legionis VII Claudiae commanipularibus bonis ex voto posuit libens meritO IMP C A DOILLO et MANNI consulibus\n
EDCS-11201542.translation: To the holy god Hercules. Ulpius Valens, centurion of the ninth century of the first centurion, an officer of the Seventh Claudian Legion, dedicated this to his good fellow soldiers, gladly fulfilling his vow. Under Emperors Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius and Lucius Aurelius Verus Caesar, consuls.
EDCS-11201542.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules by Ulpius Valens, an officer of the Seventh Legion, to his comrades, dated to the consulship of Antoninus Pius and Marcus Aurelius (AD 161).

EDCS-11201543.text_edition: \n Herculi Victori Marcus Securius Censorinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201543.translation: To Hercules the Victorious. Marcus Securius Censorinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11201543.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules Victor by Marcus Securius Censorinus, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201544.text_edition: \n Cocceius Cassius cot sic beneficarius legati vovit beneficarius consularis solvit\n
EDCS-11201544.translation: Cocceius Cassius, as a beneficarius of the legate and a beneficarius of the consular governor, vowed and fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11201544.summary: This inscription records a vow made and fulfilled by Cocceius Cassius, a subordinate official of a legate and a consular governor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201545.text_edition: \n Dio sic Heroni Aurelius Gaius veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae Antoninianae votum solvit\n
EDCS-11201545.translation: To the god Heron. Aurelius Gaius, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Antoninian Legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11201545.summary: This is a dedication to the god Heron by Aurelius Gaius, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201546.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Maximus MVA\n
EDCS-11201546.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Maximus, with the gods of his household.
EDCS-11201546.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201548.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Prusius Apollonius v(otum)? s(olvit) libens merito\n
EDCS-11201548.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Prusius Apollonius gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-11201548.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Prusius Apollonius, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201549.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Ulpius.
EDCS-11201549.summary: This is an incomplete dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Ulpius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201550.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Cassius Gemellus aquilifer votum posuit\n
EDCS-11201550.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Preserver. Cassius Gemellus, the standard-bearer, dedicated his vow.
EDCS-11201550.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Conservator by Cassius Gemellus, an aquilifer (eagle-bearer), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201551.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque et Laribus domesticis Lucius Domitius Anthi po\n
EDCS-11201551.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the gods and goddesses, and to the household gods. Lucius Domitius Anthi...
EDCS-11201551.summary: This is an incomplete dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, other deities, and household gods by Lucius Domitius Anthi..., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201557.text_edition: \n Deae Reginae Nemesi Aelius Pompeianus quod adiutor promisi frumentarius posui\n
EDCS-11201557.translation: To the Queen Goddess Nemesis, Aelius Pompeianus, because I promised it, has set this up as the grain official.
EDCS-11201557.summary: This dedication to the goddess Nemesis was set up by Aelius Pompeianus, a grain official, possibly as fulfillment of a vow or promise.

EDCS-11201558.text_edition: \n Scholae Genio mensorum et legionis VII Claudiae pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Alexander Fortunati dispensator horreorum dedicat III Kalendas Decembres Gallo et Volusiano Augustis consulibus\n
EDCS-11201558.translation: To the genius of the schools of the measurers and of the Seventh Claudian Legion, for the health of our lords Emperors Alexander and Volusianus, Alexander Fortunati, steward of the granaries, dedicates this on the third day before the Kalends of December, when Gallo and Volusianus were consuls.
EDCS-11201558.summary: This is a dedication by Alexander Fortunati, a steward of granaries, to the genius of the measurers and the Seventh Claudian Legion for the health of the emperors, dated to 250 AD.

EDCS-11201559.text_edition: \n Deabus Silvestris sic Achilleus ex voto libens posuit\n
EDCS-11201559.translation: To the Mountain Goddesses, Achilleus willingly set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11201559.summary: Achilleus willingly set up this dedication to the Mountain Goddesses in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-11201561.text_edition: \n pro salute Calpurni Honoratiani praefecti legionis VII Claudiae Caius Iulius Ingenuus librarius legionis factus ab eo votum solvit libens merito et exacti consularis beneficiarii et frumentarii no et Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-11201561.translation: For the health of Calpurnius Honoratianus, prefect of the Seventh Claudian Legion, Caius Julius Ingenuus, scribe of the legion, appointed by him, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, and as a beneficiary of the consular exactor and grain official, and when Rufus was consul.
EDCS-11201561.summary: Caius Julius Ingenuus, a scribe of the Seventh Claudian Legion, fulfilled a vow for the health of his prefect, Calpurnius Honoratianus, and other officials.

EDCS-11201564.text_edition: \n Marcus Tertius Chrestus frumentarius et Tertia Val Romana filia eius\n
EDCS-11201564.translation: Marcus Tertius Chrestus, grain official, and his daughter Tertia Val Romana.
EDCS-11201564.summary: This inscription simply records the names of Marcus Tertius Chrestus, a grain official, and his daughter Tertia Val Romana.

EDCS-11201570.text_edition: \n ------ pr]o salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti Valerius Crescentio miles legionis IIII Flaviae Severianae Alexandrianae votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-11201570.translation: For the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus, Valerius Crescentio, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Severian Alexandrian Legion, willingly and deservedly set up this vow.
EDCS-11201570.summary: Valerius Crescentio, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Severian Alexandrian Legion, set up this dedication as a vow for the health of Emperor Alexander Severus.

EDCS-11201571.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti Caius Valerius Vibianus nautarum quinquennalis signum Matris Deum et ad restitutionem templi Neptuni ssestertium IIduo milia nummum dono dedit\n
EDCS-11201571.translation: For the health of the Emperor, Caius Valerius Vibianus, a quinquennalis of the sailors, gave a statue of the Mother of the Gods and two thousand sesterces for the restoration of Neptune's temple.
EDCS-11201571.summary: Caius Valerius Vibianus, a quinquennalis of sailors, dedicated a statue and donated money for the restoration of Neptune's temple, for the emperor's health.

EDCS-11201573.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording the title 'priest'.

EDCS-11201574.text_edition: \n [ Cai Fulvi] [Plautiani] praefecti praetorio clarissimi viri necessarii dominorum nnnostrorum Flavius Optatus procurator Auggustorum\n
EDCS-11201574.translation: --- of Caius Fulvius Plautianus, prefect of the praetorium, a most distinguished man and a familiar of our lords Emperors, Flavius Optatus, procurator of the Emperors.
EDCS-11201574.summary: Flavius Optatus, a procurator, records his association with Caius Fulvius Plautianus, a prominent prefect of the praetorium.

EDCS-11201575.text_edition: \n Egnatius Marinianus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-11201575.translation: Egnatius Marinianus, legate of the Emperor with proconsular power.
EDCS-11201575.summary: This inscription records the name and high official title of Egnatius Marinianus, an imperial legate.

EDCS-11201576.text_edition: \n Marco Antonio Marci filio Fabia Fabiano procuratori XL Galliarum et portus item argentariarum nnum conductori portori Illyrici patrono bono Mercator libertus\n
EDCS-11201576.translation: To Marcus Antonius Fabianus, son of Marcus Antonius, of the Fabian tribe, procurator of the 40 provinces of Gaul and the ports, and also conductor of the Argentariae mines, a toll collector in Illyricum, and a good patron. Mercator, his freedman, [erected this].
EDCS-11201576.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Antonius Fabianus, who held several important financial and administrative posts, likely erected by his freedman Mercator, probably dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Albinae quae vixit annis XV diebus XXXVI Albinus Iuliorum servus vilicus et Irene parentes filiae carissimae posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201577.translation: To the divine shades of Albina, who lived 15 years and 36 days. Albinus, a slave of the Iulii, the steward, and Irene, her parents, placed this for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-11201577.summary: This is a tombstone for Albina, who died at a young age, erected by her parents, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201578.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Baebiae Amoenianae vixit annis XXXIX Caius Cassius Niger decurio municipii Aeli Viminacii coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201578.translation: To the divine shades of Baebia Amoeniana, who lived 39 years. Caius Cassius Niger, decurion of the municipium of Aelia Viminacium, placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11201578.summary: This is a tombstone for Baebia Amoeniana, erected by her husband Caius Cassius Niger, a decurion in Aelia Viminacium, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201579.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Cornelius Rufus decurio augur municipii Aeli Viminacii vixit nnos LXX Ulpia Rufina uxor et Caius Cornelius Pacatus decurio flamen municipii eiusdem et Corneliae Rufina et Bassa filii et heredes bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11201579.translation: To the divine shades of Caius Cornelius Rufus, decurion and augur of the municipium of Aelia Viminacium, who lived 70 years. Ulpia Rufina, his wife, and Caius Cornelius Pacatus, decurion and flamen of the same municipium, and Cornelia Rufina and Bassa, his children and heirs, had this made for him, his well-deserving relative.
EDCS-11201579.summary: This is a tombstone for Caius Cornelius Rufus, a prominent civic official in Aelia Viminacium, erected by his wife, sons, and heirs, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201580.text_edition: \n Lucio Sevio Rufo sacerdoti municipii Aeli Viminacii vixit nnos XLV Saenia Supera coniugi\n
EDCS-11201580.translation: To Lucius Sevius Rufus, priest of the municipium of Aelia Viminacium, who lived 45 years. Saenia Supera [erected this] for her wife.
EDCS-11201580.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Sevius Rufus, a priest in Aelia Viminacium, erected by his wife Saenia Supera, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201583.text_edition: \n Valeriae Hilarae Marcus Aurelius Timon |(centurio) legionis III Gallicae Severianae Alexandrianae coniugi piissimae posuit\n
EDCS-11201583.translation: Marcus Aurelius Timon, centurion of the III Gallica Severiana Alexandriana legion, placed this for his most devoted wife, Valeriae Hilarae.
EDCS-11201583.summary: This is a tombstone for Valeria Hilara, erected by her husband Marcus Aurelius Timon, a centurion in the III Gallica legion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201584.text_edition: \n Quinto Sicinio Quinti filio Sergia Maximo Xviro stlitibus iudicandis tribuno militum legionis IIII Flaviae felicis vixit nnos XXIII\n
EDCS-11201584.translation: For Quintus Sicinius Maximus, son of Quintus, of the Sergia tribe, duovir for judging legal disputes, tribune of the soldiers of the IIII Flavia Felix legion, who lived 23 years.
EDCS-11201584.summary: This is an epitaph for Quintus Sicinius Maximus, a young man who held several significant military and civic roles, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201585.text_edition: \n Lucius Aemilius Luci filius Fabia Flaus Bracara eques legionis IIII Flaviae felicis stipendiorum XIIII vixit nnos XXXII\n
EDCS-11201585.translation: Lucius Aemilius Flaus, son of Lucius, of the Fabian tribe, from Bracara, an equestrian soldier in the IIII Flavia Felix legion, with 14 years of service, who lived 32 years.
EDCS-11201585.summary: This is an epitaph for Lucius Aemilius Flaus, an equestrian soldier in the IIII Flavia Felix legion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201586.text_edition: \n Publius Publicius Fabia Constans Brixxia sic eq(ues) legionis IIII Flaviae felicis testamento fieri iussit hic situs <e=F>st \n
EDCS-11201586.translation: Publius Publicius Constans, of the Fabian tribe, from Brixia, an equestrian soldier in the IIII Flavia Felix legion, ordered this to be made in his will. He is buried here.
EDCS-11201586.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Publicius Constans, an equestrian soldier in the IIII Flavia Felix legion, who arranged for his burial in his will, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201589.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Tettius Euphemianus primus pilus legionis VII Claudiae hic situs est Fulvia Chrysis marito pientissimo\n
EDCS-11201589.translation: To the divine shades of Tiberius Tettius Euphemianus, first-rank centurion of the VII Claudia legion. He is buried here. Fulvia Chrysis [placed this] for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-11201589.summary: This is a tombstone for Tiberius Tettius Euphemianus, a senior centurion in the VII Claudia legion, erected by his wife Fulvia Chrysis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201590.text_edition: \n nociusto ex Carthaginiensi |(centurioni) legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis pro RO I sorores heredes optimo piissimo amantissimo fratri fecerunt pius vixit annos LXV |(centurio) militavit annos XXXV hic situs est ex his agris monimento excepti sunt in fronte pedes XX introitus pedes XX et iter\n
EDCS-11201590.translation: To Justus from Carthage, centurion of the 7th Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, for his sisters. His heirs made this for their most excellent, most devoted, most loving brother. He lived 65 years, served as centurion for 35 years. He is buried here. Excluded from these lands are plots for the monument: 20 feet in front, 20 feet for the entrance, and a path.
EDCS-11201590.summary: This funerary inscription records a centurion of the 7th Claudian Legion, set up by his sisters, and includes details about the plot size. It likely dates to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201592.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Senecianus beneficiarius consularis vixit nnos XLV Ulpia Faustina coniux et Aurelia Firmina filia posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201592.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Senecianus, *beneficiarius consularis*, lived 45 years. His wife Ulpia Faustina and daughter Aurelia Firmina erected this.
EDCS-11201592.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelius Senecianus, a *beneficiarius consularis*, set up by his wife and daughter, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201593.text_edition: \n Iuliae Abitae sic vixit nnos XIIII Iulius Ianuarius beneficiarius consularis libertae bene merenti posuit hic sita est\n
EDCS-11201593.translation: To Julia Abita, who lived 14 years. Iulius Ianuarius, *beneficiarius consularis*, set this up for his freedwoman who deserved well of him. She is buried here.
EDCS-11201593.summary: This funerary inscription records Julia Abita, who lived 14 years, set up by Iulius Ianuarius, a *beneficiarius consularis*, and mentions her status as his freedwoman, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-11201595.text_edition: \n ------ militavit] annos XXIII vixit annos XLVIII Flavius Palma beneficiarius legati heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201595.translation: [... served] 23 years, lived 48 years. Flavius Palma, *beneficiarius legati*, and heir, set this up for the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201595.summary: This funerary inscription records an unnamed individual who served 23 years and lived 48 years, set up by Flavius Palma, a *beneficiarius legati*.

EDCS-11201597.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Maximus veteranus legionis VII Claudiae ex corniculario tribuni vixit nnos LVII Aebutia Taburtilla et Vale bene m(erenti) pos(uerunt)\n
EDCS-11201597.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Valerius Maximus, veteran of the 7th Claudian Legion, formerly *cornicularius tribuni*, lived 57 years. Aebutia Taburtilla and Vale set this up for him who deserved well of them.
EDCS-11201597.summary: This funerary inscription honors Gaius Valerius Maximus, a veteran and former *cornicularius tribuni* of the 7th Claudian Legion, set up by Aebutia Taburtilla and Vale.

EDCS-11201598.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Valenti militi legionis VII Claudiae beneficiario procuratoris stipendiorum XXV vixit nnos L Lucius Iulius Valens filius et heres faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201598.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Iulius Valens, soldier of the 7th Claudian Legion, *beneficiarius procuratoris*, with 25 years of service. He lived 50 years. Lucius Iulius Valens, son and heir, arranged for this to be made. He is buried here.
EDCS-11201598.summary: This funerary inscription records Gaius Iulius Valens, a soldier and *beneficiarius procuratoris* of the 7th Claudian Legion who served 25 years and lived 50 years, set up by his son and heir.

EDCS-11201599.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Dizzoni aquilifero legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos XLVI Flavia Nata coniux et Flavia Viatorina filia heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201599.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Aurelius Dizzon, standard-bearer (*aquilifer*) of the 7th Claudian Legion, who lived 46 years. Flavia Nata, wife, and Flavia Viatorina, daughter and heir, set this up for him who deserved well of them.
EDCS-11201599.summary: This funerary inscription honors Marcus Aurelius Dizzon, an *aquilifer* of the 7th Claudian Legion, set up by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-11201603.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Cassianus Potens miles legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis militavit annis XI vixit annis XXVIII Cassiana Valentina bene merenti fratri posuit\n
EDCS-11201603.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Cassianus Potens, soldier of the 7th Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, served 11 years, lived 28 years. Cassiana Valentina set this up for her brother who deserved well of her.
EDCS-11201603.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Cassianus Potens, a soldier of the 7th Claudian Legion who served 11 years and lived 28 years, set up by his sister.

EDCS-11201604.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Valerio Quintino veterano legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos XLV et Lucio Valerio Celso militi legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos XX militavit annos II Licinia Celsina coniugi karissimo et filio incomparabili bene merentibus posuit h s m e\n
EDCS-11201604.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Valerius Quintinus, veteran of the 7th Claudian Legion, who lived 45 years, and for Lucius Valerius Celsus, soldier of the 7th Claudian Legion, who lived 20 years and served 2 years. Licinia Celsina set this up for her dearest husband and incomparable son, who deserved well of her. Buried here, may he rest in peace.
EDCS-11201604.summary: This funerary inscription honors both a veteran, Lucius Valerius Quintinus, and a soldier, Lucius Valerius Celsus, of the 7th Claudian Legion, set up by Licinia Celsina for her husband and son.

EDCS-11201605.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Valens Fabia Heraclea veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis vixit annos LXX Valeria Veneria liberta patrono bene merenti et sibi pos(uit)\n
EDCS-11201605.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcus Valerius Valens Fabia Heraclea, veteran of the 7th Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, who lived 70 years. Valeria Veneria, freedwoman, set this up for her well-deserving patron and for herself.
EDCS-11201605.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Valerius Valens Fabia Heraclea, a veteran of the 7th Claudian Legion, set up by his freedwoman Valeria Veneria for him and herself.

EDCS-11201608.text_edition: \n IIar nnum XXV hic sit- est Lucius Fabius Crescens |(centurio) legionis XV Apollinaris libert- bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201608.translation: Lucius Fabius Crescens, centurion of the fifteenth Apollinaris legion, set this up for his freedwoman, who deserved well, aged 25.
EDCS-11201608.summary: A tombstone erected by a centurion for his deceased freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201609.text_edition: \n Sabinianus protector ortus in provincia Dardania regione Ulpianensi Dasantis qui vixit annos L qui donec superstaret vitam iucundam exegi sibi et amicis qui a me hodie exoptantur et optantur ibidem terra levis habeat semper vosque iter carpite legite vos vitam iucundam exigatis post mortem nihil est nisi est vilis umbra vox bonae memoriae castaque Sulpitus posuit\n
EDCS-11201609.translation: Sabinianus, a *protector* from the province of Dardania, in the region of Ulpianensis, from Dasantis, who lived 50 years, and lived a pleasant life as long as he survived, for himself and for friends who are desired today and will be desired by me. May the earth rest lightly upon him. And you, read, embark on your journey, and live a pleasant life. After death there is nothing but a fleeting shadow. For good memory and chastity, Sulpitius placed this.
EDCS-11201609.summary: A tombstone set up by Sulpitius, likely commemorating Sabinianus, a soldier (*protector*) from Dardania, and reflecting on life and death, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201611.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Paridi alumno vixit annos XVII Caius Reg Callistianus praep(ositus fecit\n
EDCS-11201611.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Paris, his pupil, lived 17 years. Caius Reg Callistianus, prefect, made this.
EDCS-11201611.summary: A tombstone set up by a prefect for his young pupil named Paris, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201612.summary: A fragmentary inscription recording the name and affiliation of a soldier, Aurelius Vale, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201613.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Secundinus veteranus vixit annos LX Aurelii Secundina Secundio Iulia et Lidira patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201613.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Aurelius Secundinus, veteran, lived 60 years. Aurelii Secundina, Secundio, Iulia, and Lidira placed this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11201613.summary: A tombstone erected by children for their deceased father, Aurelius Secundinus, a veteran, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201616.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Petronius eter vixit annos LXV nia Marcia coniugi et Petronius Cogitatus Petronius Fuscus Marcia Marcellina Petronius Amyntianus fili patri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11201616.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Petronius Pater lived 65 years. Marcia, his wife, and Petronius Cogitatus, Petronius Fuscus, Marcia Marcellina, Petronius Amyntianus, his sons, placed this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-11201616.summary: A tombstone set up by a wife and sons for the well-deserving father, Petronius Pater, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201617.text_edition: \n I et mil nnos Xvixit nnos fili- ETCONAce liberta bene merenti GAIIV et sibi\n
EDCS-11201617.translation: [---] lived 15 years. [---] his son. A freedwoman, for the well-deserving Gaius and for herself.
EDCS-11201617.summary: A fragmented tombstone possibly recording the lifespan of a son and a dedication by a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201618.summary: A very fragmentary inscription referring to a veteran, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201621.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Agathopus vixit nnos XXXV Aelia Valentina uxor bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11201621.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Aelius Agathopus lived 35 years. His wife, Aelia Valentina, placed this for him, the well-deserving.
EDCS-11201621.summary: A tombstone set up by a wife for her deceased husband, Publius Aelius Agathopus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201622.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius orill vivus vivis fecit salvus sis qui legis\n
EDCS-11201622.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Aelius Orill, while alive, made this for the living. Be well, you who read.
EDCS-11201622.summary: A tombstone set up by Publius Aelius Orill for himself during his lifetime, with a greeting to the reader, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201623.text_edition: \n Aelius Meritus avunculus <e>orum E bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11201623.translation: Aelius Meritus, their uncle, set this up for those who deserved well.
EDCS-11201623.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aelius Meritus for deserving relatives, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201624.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Birulius filius Anticho Suro casum sic fecit die sic III Kalendarum sic Septenbrium sic\n
EDCS-11201624.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Birulius, son of Anticho Suro, met his end thus on the third day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-11201624.summary: This epitaph records the death of Aurelius Birulius, son of Anticho Suro, on a specific date in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201625.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Cornelius Quinti libertus Zosimus vixit annos LX Cornelia Zosime filia et Cornelius Trophimus libertus et coheredes fecerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-11201625.translation: To the departed spirits. Quintus Cornelius Zosimus, freedman of Quintus, lived for 60 years. His daughter Cornelia Zosime, and Cornelius Trophimus, freedman, and co-heirs, made this. He is buried here.
EDCS-11201625.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Quintus Cornelius Zosimus, detailing his age at death and the individuals who erected the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201627.text_edition: \n ------] Eusebius filius Antianu sic civis Germaniceus ex vico Abdarmisu sic qui bixit sic annus sic XXIII qui filius patri facere debebat ego iam \n
EDCS-11201627.translation: ... Eusebius, son of Antianu, citizen of Germanicea, from the village of Abdarmisu, who lived for 23 years, who should have erected a monument for his father, I now...
EDCS-11201627.summary: This is a fragment of an epitaph for Eusebius, son of Antianu, a 23-year-old citizen of Germanicea, likely from the Roman Imperial period, whose inscription is incomplete.

EDCS-11201628.summary: This is a fragmentary tomb inscription for Iulius Prispientis, who lived to be 66 years old, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201629.text_edition: \n a XIX cuius post obitum Marcianus pudicitiae vinctus amore dicat hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-11201629.translation: ...at 19 years of age. After whose death, Marcianus, bound by love for her chastity, declares this monument: she will not follow an heir.
EDCS-11201629.summary: This inscription marks a monument set up by Marcianus for a woman who died at 19, expressing his love and her independence from familial inheritance, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201635.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ius filius Iul vixit annos V ia Titia annos IV pat \n
EDCS-11201635.translation: To the departed spirits. ...son of Iulius, lived for 5 years. Titia... lived for 4 years. Father...
EDCS-11201635.summary: This is a fragmented epitaph for two young children, a son of Iulius and Titia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201639.text_edition: \n XV IApollonius coniugi bene merenti cum filia posuit\n
EDCS-11201639.translation: ...for 15 years. Apollonius set this up for his well-deserving wife, with his daughter.
EDCS-11201639.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Apollonius for his wife, mentioning his daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201640.text_edition: \n avi coniugi bene merenti vixit nnos XXXIII\n
EDCS-11201640.translation: ...for his well-deserving wife, lived for 33 years.
EDCS-11201640.summary: This inscription records the death of a woman who lived for 33 years and was erected by her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201646.text_edition: \n CI infelicis c hic situs est tus fratri posuit\n
EDCS-11201646.translation: ...unhappy. ...is buried here. ...set this up for his brother.
EDCS-11201646.summary: This is a fragmentary tomb inscription indicating someone is buried here and that the monument was set up by a brother for his sibling, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11201651.text_edition: \n ani Valgia ima coniugi bene merenti p(osuit)\n
EDCS-11201651.translation: Ani Valgia set up for her deserving husband.
EDCS-11201651.summary: This funerary epitaph records that Ani Valgia dedicated a monument to her husband.

EDCS-11201655.text_edition: \n Christus Deus Dei filius custodiat artefices sic omnes qui hoc opus fecerunt in Domino\n
EDCS-11201655.translation: Christ, God, son of God, protect the craftsmen and all who made this work in the Lord.
EDCS-11201655.summary: This inscription is a Christian invocation for divine protection over the artisans of a project.

EDCS-11201656.text_edition: \n Marina honista sic femina cultrix dei quae vixit cum virginio suo annos XXX Publio <L=I>icinio Constanciolo sic sita est \n
EDCS-11201656.translation: Marina, a respectable woman, worshipper of God, who lived with her husband Virginio for 30 years, is buried here. To Publius Licinius Constantulus.
EDCS-11201656.summary: This epitaph records the death of Marina after 30 years of marriage and is dedicated to Publius Licinius Constantulus.

EDCS-11201657.text_edition: \n Sitha quae quiescit in pace Romae ABIDVA nnorum XLV deposita VI Idus Februarias\n
EDCS-11201657.translation: Sitha, who rests in peace in Rome, Abidva, aged 45, buried on the 8th day before the Ides of February.
EDCS-11201657.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Sitha, aged 45, in Rome on February 6th.

EDCS-11201659.summary: This is a dedication to the god Heron.

EDCS-11201660.summary: This is a short, personal dedication or invocation, possibly to a deity or a respected woman.

EDCS-11201661.text_edition: \n Cai Iuli Iuniani diabsoricum sic ad aspritudinem et claritatem Cai Iuli Iuniani crocodes aspritudinis Cai Iulii? O C on croc(odes CC\n
EDCS-11201661.translation: Of Gaius Julius Iunianus... for roughness and clarity... of Gaius Julius Iunianus... crocus... of roughness... of Gaius Julius? ... croci.
EDCS-11201661.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmented record of materials or expenses, possibly related to a building project or artistic endeavor, mentioning names like Gaius Julius Iunianus.

EDCS-11201666.text_edition: \n nus rcianus enus nus itanus Marcus Aurelius I Marcus Aurelius F Marcus Ulpius Caius Iulius IND\n
EDCS-11201666.translation: [---]rcianus enus [---]tanus Marcus Aurelius I Marcus Aurelius F Marcus Ulpius Caius Julius IND[---]
EDCS-11201666.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to list names, possibly of individuals involved in a commission or family.

EDCS-11201667.text_edition: \n s ns s ns tius Caius Iulius Titus Aelius M Caius Valerius Caius Iulius \n
EDCS-11201667.translation: [---] ns s ns tius Caius Julius Titus Aelius M Caius Valerius Caius Julius [---]
EDCS-11201667.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that lists several Roman names, likely indicating individuals involved in some official capacity or as part of a group.

EDCS-11201668.summary: This inscription begins with a dedication to Mavricius and then names Marcus Valerius, suggesting a dedication or a funerary monument.

EDCS-11201718.text_edition: \n Marti sacrum Titus Flavius Sapi ex viso\n
EDCS-11201718.translation: Sacred to Mars. Titus Flavius Sapi, in fulfillment of a vow seen in a vision.
EDCS-11201718.summary: This is a votive dedication to the god Mars by Titus Flavius Sapi, likely a soldier, recorded on a monument likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201719.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus, possibly by a person named Domestico, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11201722.summary: This is a very brief inscription dedicated to a god named Heron, the full context and date are unclear due to the missing letters.

EDCS-11201723.summary: This inscription dedicates something to Silvanus Domesticus, with a partial name or title following, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301514.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Saluti Titus Flavius Adiectus \n
EDCS-11301514.translation: To Aesculapius and Salus. Titus Flavius Adiectus.
EDCS-11301514.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the gods Aesculapius and Salus by Titus Flavius Adiectus, likely a private citizen in the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301516.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Titus Aelius Mucianus et Tullia coniux ex voto posuerunt et templum fecerunt\n
EDCS-11301516.translation: To Diana Augusta. Titus Aelius Mucianus and his wife Tullia, in fulfillment of a vow, erected this and built a temple.
EDCS-11301516.summary: This monument records the construction of a temple to Diana Augusta by Titus Aelius Mucianus and his wife Tullia, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301517.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae Marcus Aurelius Quadratianus tribunus templum a solo ex voto restituit\n
EDCS-11301517.translation: To Diana Augusta. Marcus Aurelius Quadratianus, tribune, restored the temple from its foundations in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301517.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Diana Augusta by Marcus Aurelius Quadratianus, a military tribune, likely during the High Imperial period.

EDCS-11301518.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Flavius Bitus DD ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-11301518.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Paterno Flavius Bitus willingly dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301518.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Flavius Bitus, identified as Paterno, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301521.text_edition: \n Marti equitum pro salute dominorum nnn(ostrorum) Severi et Antonini impperatorum Gaius Atrius Decoratus tribunus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae |(miliariae) equitatae libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301521.translation: To Mars of the cavalry, for the safety of our lords Severus and Antoninus, emperors. Gaius Atrius Decoratus, tribune of the II Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians Antoniniana (miliaria), equipped with cavalry, willingly dedicated this.
EDCS-11301521.summary: This military inscription records a dedication by Gaius Atrius Decoratus, a tribune, to Mars for the safety of Emperors Severus and Antoninus, dating it to their joint reign (c. 198-211/217 AD).

EDCS-11301523.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Castinus votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301523.translation: To the Unconquered God. Castinus willingly fulfilled a vow.
EDCS-11301523.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to an Unconquered God by Castinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period when such cults were popular.

EDCS-11301524.text_edition: \n Invicto deo pro salute Impperatorum Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Luci Aureli Veri [Augusti ------\n
EDCS-11301524.translation: To the unconquered god, for the health of the Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus [and others].
EDCS-11301524.summary: This is a dedication to an unconquered god for the health of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-11301525.text_edition: \n pro reditu et victoria RV Pii Felicis Augusti anus EXVIIVL Iulianum L Valens V sacerdotes MRIO VIM AELOS\n
EDCS-11301525.translation: For the return and victory of the Pious, Fortunate, Emperor Pius Felix Augustus, from the sixty-first year of the world. Iulianus Valens, priest of the temple, [dedicates this].
EDCS-11301525.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the safe return and victory of an emperor, made by a priest and likely other officials, with an approximate date suggested by 'anus EXVIIVL' (61st year of the world/creation).

EDCS-11301526.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Flavius Clemes Sabazio Paterno Augusto posuit Marco Antonio Gordiano Augusto et Clodio Pompeiano consulibus\n
EDCS-11301526.translation: Flavius Clemes Sabazio Paterno Augustus dedicated this for the health of the Emperor. In the consulship of Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus and Clodius Pompeianus.
EDCS-11301526.summary: This monument was erected by Flavius Clemes Sabazio Paterno Augustus for the health of the Emperor, during the consulship of Marcus Antonius Gordianus and Clodius Pompeianus, placing it in 242 AD.

EDCS-11301527.text_edition: \n Titus Aelius Mucianus et Tullia Augusta ex voto posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301527.translation: Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301527.summary: Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta erected this monument in fulfillment of a votive promise.

EDCS-11301528.text_edition: \n Trevius sic Salustianus sic votum posuit\n
EDCS-11301528.translation: Trevius sic Salustianus sic set up [this] votive offering.
EDCS-11301528.summary: Trevius sic Salustianus erected this monument as a votive offering.

EDCS-11301532.text_edition: \n ------ pro] salute dominorum nnnostrorum Severi et Antonini Impperatorum Caius Atrius Decoratus tribunus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae Mmiliariae equitatae libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301532.translation: [For the] health of our lords Severus and Antoninus Emperors. Caius Atrius Decoratus, tribune of the II Aurelian Cohort of Dacians Antoniniana Milliaria Equitata, willingly dedicated this.
EDCS-11301532.summary: This dedication for the health of Emperors Severus and Antoninus was made by Caius Atrius Decoratus, a tribune in an auxiliary cavalry unit, likely dating to the Severan period (late 2nd to early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-11301534.translation: For the health of Lucius Septim[ius Severus...].
EDCS-11301534.summary: This is a dedication for the health of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-11301535.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici invicto Augusto tribunicia potestate V cons(uli) II patri patriae proconsuli cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum Gordiana devota numini maiestatique eius dedicante Lucio Catio Celere legato Augusti pro praetore Attico et Praetextato consulibus\n
EDCS-11301535.translation: The II Aurelian Cohort of Dacians Gordiana, devoted to his divine majesty and might, dedicated this to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, under the dedication of Lucius Catius Celer, Augusti legatus pro praetore, during the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus.
EDCS-11301535.summary: The II Aurelian Cohort of Dacians Gordiana dedicates this monument to Emperor Gordian III, honoring his imperial titles and authority, during his consulship and under the governorship of Lucius Catius Celer, likely dating to 242 AD.

EDCS-11301536.text_edition: \n ON Clementinus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus coloniae Ratiariae amico bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11301536.translation: On Clementinus, honored with decurional ornaments of the colony of Ratiaria, dedicated [this] to a well-deserving friend.
EDCS-11301536.summary: This monument was erected by On Clementinus, a decurion of Ratiaria, in honor of a deserving friend.

EDCS-11301537.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Nico decurio M municipi vixit annos LXXV Valeria Nicomedia filia et Publius Valerius Gelasio \n
EDCS-11301537.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Nico, decurion of the municipium, lived 75 years. Valeria Nicomedia, his daughter, and Publius Valerius Gelasio [dedicate this].
EDCS-11301537.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Valerius Nico, a decurion who lived to 75, set up by his daughter and another individual.

EDCS-11301538.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Augustinus veteranus ex cohorte praetoria vixit annos LXX Aurelius Candidus fratri et Aurelius Surus patri et Maius C \n
EDCS-11301538.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Augustinus, a veteran from the Praetorian Guard, lived 70 years. Aurelius Candidus set this up for his brother, and Aurelius Surus for his father.
EDCS-11301538.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Augustinus, a Praetorian veteran, set up by his brother and father, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301539.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Aurelianus miles legionis IIII Flaviae vixit annis XXXV Aurelius Bithus veteranus pater et Aelia Aide mater filio bene merenti et sibi se vivis posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301539.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Aurelianus, a soldier of Legio IV Flaviae, lived 35 years. Aurelius Bithus, his father, a veteran, and Aelia Aide, his mother, set this up for their deserving son and themselves while alive.
EDCS-11301539.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Aurelianus, a soldier of Legio IV Flaviae, set up by his parents, dating likely to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301540.text_edition: \n Aurelius V imaginifer cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum militavit nnos XXII et Aurelia Primitiva coniux eius vixit nnos L Aurelius Valens signifer legionis VII Claudiae patri et matri bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301540.translation: Aurelius V, standard-bearer of Cohors II Aureliae Dardanorum, served 22 years, and Aurelia Primitiva, his wife, lived 50 years. Aurelius Valens, a standard-bearer of Legio VII Claudia, erected this for his deserving father and mother.
EDCS-11301540.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius V and his wife Aurelia Primitiva, set up by their son Aurelius Valens, a soldier, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301542.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Aquilinus miles legionis VII Claudiae librarius offici praefecti territorii vixit nnos XXII Aquileiensis et Ulpia Diotima filio dulcissimo bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301542.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulpius Aquilinus, a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, scribe in the office of the prefect of the territory, lived 22 years. Aquileiensis and Ulpia Diotima set this up for their most beloved and deserving son.
EDCS-11301542.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpius Aquilinus, a soldier and scribe, set up by his parents, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301543.text_edition: \n ------] Ingenuus legionis VII Claudiae qui vixit nnos XLV militavit nnos XVI semissem interfectus in expeditione Parthica et Aurelius Valentinus qui et Octavius filius heres et Claudia Cocceia mater filio bene merenti posuit ave Ingenue bene valeas viator\n
EDCS-11301543.translation: [---] Ingenuus of Legio VII Claudia, who lived 45 years and served 16 years, was killed in the Parthian expedition. Aurelius Valentinus, who was also called Octavius, his son and heir, and Claudia Cocceia, his mother, set this up for their deserving son; hail, Ingenuus, farewell traveler.
EDCS-11301543.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ingenuus, a soldier of Legio VII Claudia killed in the Parthian campaign, set up by his son and mother, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301544.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Vibius |(centurio) legionis X fretensis po \n
EDCS-11301544.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Ulpius Vibius, centurion of Legio X Fretensis, [set this up]...
EDCS-11301544.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Vibius, a centurion of Legio X Fretensis, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301545.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Mercurius miles legionis XIII geminae vixit nnos L militavit nnos XVIIII et Aureli-- \n
EDCS-11301545.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Mercurius, a soldier of Legio XIII Gemina, lived 50 years and served 19 years, and Aurelius--
EDCS-11301545.summary: A fragmented funerary epitaph for Aurelius Mercurius, a soldier of Legio XIII Gemina, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-11301546.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Valerius Germanus veteranus cohortis I Thracum militavit annos XXXII Ma posuit\n
EDCS-11301546.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Quintus Valerius Germanus, a veteran of Cohors I Thracum, served 32 years. Ma [set this up].
EDCS-11301546.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Valerius Germanus, a veteran of Cohors I Thracum, set up by someone abbreviated as 'Ma', likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301547.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Marcus eques Romanus vixit annos X et Aurelius Marcianus vixit annos VII Aurelius Martianus vir egregius praepositus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum pater et Aurelia Donata mater et Aurelia Severina soror se sic superviventes filiis bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301547.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Marcus, a Roman knight, lived 10 years, and Aurelius Marcianus lived 7 years. Aurelius Martianus, an outstanding man and commander of Cohors II Aureliae Dardanorum, his father, and Aurelia Donata, his mother, and Aurelia Severina, his sister, set this up for their deserving sons while alive.
EDCS-11301547.summary: A funerary epitaph for two young boys, Aurelius Marcus and Aurelius Marcianus, set up by their father, mother, and sister, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301548.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Salvianus |(centurio) cohortis II Aureliae vixit nnos XLI militavit nnos XXI et Aurelia Salviane sic filia vixit nnos V menses VIIII Cocceia Petilia coniugi et filiae et sibi se viva bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301548.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Salvianus, centurion of Cohors II Aureliae, lived 41 years and served 21 years, and Aurelia Salviane, his daughter, lived 5 years and 9 months. Cocceia Petilia set this up for her deserving husband, daughter, and herself while alive.
