EDCS-11301548.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Salvianus, his daughter Aurelia Salviane, set up by his wife and mother of the daughter, Cocceia Petilia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11301549.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Bitho adiutori cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum marito et Aurelio Hermogeni decurioni cohortis supra scriptae fratri Aurelia Cristina posuit\n
EDCS-11301549.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Cristina set this up for her husband Flavius Bithus, an assistant in the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, and for her brother Aurelius Hermogenes, a decurion in the aforementioned cohort.
EDCS-11301549.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Cristina to her husband and brother, both of whom served in the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort.

EDCS-11301550.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Maximus veteranus ex decurione cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum vixit annos LXX et Flavius Honoratus filius eius vixit annos XXV Aurelia Crescentilla marito et Flavias sic Maximilla Magnilla Quinta patri et fratri bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301550.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Flavius Maximus, a veteran and former decurion of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, lived 70 years, and his son Flavius Honoratus lived 25 years. Aurelia Crescentilla set this up for her husband and son, who deserved well.
EDCS-11301550.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Crescentilla for her late husband, Titus Flavius Maximus (a veteran decurion), and her son Flavius Honoratus.

EDCS-11301551.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Iulius Saturninus veteranus ex decurione cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum sibi vivo et Ovidiae Pudentillae coniugi karissimae cum qua conubio stabili quinquaginta annos sine secessu dulciter egit\n
EDCS-11301551.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Julius Saturninus, a veteran and former decurion of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, for himself in his lifetime and for his most cherished wife Ovidia Pudentilla, with whom he lived happily for fifty years in a stable marriage without separation.
EDCS-11301551.summary: A self-funded funerary monument by Titus Julius Saturninus, a veteran decurion, for himself and his wife Ovidia Pudentilla, celebrating their long and happy marriage.

EDCS-11301552.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Furnia vixit annos XXXVII Rusticianus Valerianus Lucilla Faestus sic matri et Aelius Rusticus decurio duplarius cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum coniugi bene merenti et vivo se sic sibi possuerunt\n
EDCS-11301552.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Furnia lived 37 years. Rusticianus Valerianus and Lucilla Faestus set this up for their mother and for Aelius Rusticus, decurion duplarius of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, their deserving husband, and for himself during his lifetime.
EDCS-11301552.summary: A funerary monument set up by Rusticianus Valerianus and Lucilla Faestus for their mother Valeria Furnia and her husband, Aelius Rusticus, a decurion duplarius in the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort.

EDCS-11301553.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimius Super veteranus ex hastiliario cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum vixit annos LX et Septimius Longinus vixit annos VII et Septimia Firmina vixit mensem et Septimius Exuperatus vixit annum I Flavia Maximilla filiis et marito bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-11301553.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Septimius Super, a veteran hastilarius of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, lived 60 years; Septimius Longinus lived 7 years; Septimia Firmina lived one month; and Septimius Exuperatus lived 1 year. Flavia Maximilla set this up for her deserving children and husband.
EDCS-11301553.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Flavia Maximilla for her deceased husband, Septimius Super (a veteran hastilarius), and their children of varying ages, all associated with the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort.

EDCS-11301554.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aelio Martiali medico cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum stipendiorum XXII mater et Aelia Latina \n
EDCS-11301554.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Titus Aelius Martialis, physician of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, with 22 years of service. His mother and Aelia Latina.
EDCS-11301554.summary: A funerary inscription for Titus Aelius Martialis, a physician of the II Aurelia Dardanorum cohort, set up by his mother and Aelia Latina.

EDCS-11301555.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Plotina vixit nnos LX Aurelius Valens et Petilia Surilla coniuges Lucio Egnatio Aristiano Supero militi cohortis II Dardanorum et <K=R>allistiano et Neglecto filiis eius \n
EDCS-11301555.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Plotina lived 60 years. Aurelius Valens and Petilia Surilla, husband and wife, set this up for their son Lucius Egnatius Aristianus Superus, a soldier in the II Dardanorum cohort, and for his brothers Callistianus and Neglectus.
EDCS-11301555.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aurelius Valens and Petilia Surilla for their son Lucius Egnatius Aristianus Superus, a soldier in the II Dardanorum cohort, and his brothers.

EDCS-11301556.text_edition: \n mens Ccohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae vixit annos XXVIII militavit annos X Flavius Firminus heres iuxta volu<n=P>tate INTR die XXX RENE \n
EDCS-11301556.translation: A member of the II Aurelia Dardanorum Antoniniana cohort lived 28 years and served 10 years. Flavius Firminus, his heir, according to his will, on the 30th day...
EDCS-11301556.summary: A funerary epitaph for a soldier of the II Aurelia Dardanorum Antoniniana cohort, who lived 28 years and served 10 years, with dedication by his heir Flavius Firminus.

EDCS-11301557.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Valerius Iovinus ex ducenario equitum promotorum qui vixit annosaginta duos patrono impromiscuae fidei Nestori sui temporis moribus bene cognito iusto officioso operoso Vibia Sicca et Valeria Marcella et Flavia Iovina ob abstemie nobiliterque facta memoriam fecerunt\n
EDCS-11301557.translation: To the Infernal Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Iovinus, a centurion (ducenarius) of the equites promoti, who lived 82 years, for his patron Nestor, of unblemished loyalty, known in his time for his just, dutiful, and industrious character. Vibia Sicca, Valeria Marcella, and Flavia Iovina made this memorial to him for his abstemious and noble deeds.
EDCS-11301557.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Valerius Iovinus, a centurion of the equites promoti, by Vibia Sicca, Valeria Marcella, and Flavia Iovina, who commemorate his character and deeds.

EDCS-11301559.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Trebia Marcellina vixit annis XLV Aurelius Augustinus veteranus coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11301559.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Trebia Marcellina lived 45 years. Aurelius Augustinus, a veteran, set this up for his deserving wife.
EDCS-11301559.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by the veteran Aurelius Augustinus to his wife Trebia Marcellina.

EDCS-11301560.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Severus veteranus vixit nnos LX et Severianus vixit nnos XX et Crescentilla vixit nnos II Iulia Cassia coniugi et filiis bene merentibus simul et sibi se vivo posuit\n
EDCS-11301560.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Severus, veteran, lived 60 years, and Severianus lived 20 years, and Crescentilla lived 3 years. Iulia Cassia erected this for her husband and sons who deserved well, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-11301560.summary: This tombstone records the ages of death for Marcus Aurelius Severus, Severianus, and Crescentilla, with Iulia Cassia dedicating it to her family and herself.

EDCS-11301561.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Tatius veteranus vixit nnos LX Aurelia Longina coniux una cum filis suis simul et sibi se viva fecit\n
EDCS-11301561.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Tatius, veteran, lived 60 years. Aurelia Longina, his wife, together with their children, made this for him and for herself while alive.
EDCS-11301561.summary: This epitaph is dedicated by Aurelia Longina and her children to her deceased husband Aurelius Tatius, a veteran, and also for herself.

EDCS-11301562.text_edition: \n Dassius veteranus vixit annos LX bene merenti simul et vivae Aureliae Severae coniugi eius siqut sic testamento suo Aelio Ennio primo heredi intercessione Valeri Aquilini defuncti domi delegavit\n
EDCS-11301562.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Dassius, veteran, lived 60 years. For his deserving wife Aurelia Severa, and as stipulated in his will by Aelius Ennius, his heir, he delegated this at home through the intervention of the deceased Valerius Aquilinus.
EDCS-11301562.summary: This inscription concerns the veteran Dassius, his wife Aurelia Severa, and arrangements made for their burial through his heir and the intervention of Valerius Aquilinus.

EDCS-11301563.text_edition: \n Septimius x veteranus vixit annos LVII et Septimio Testae veterano vixit nnos LX \n
EDCS-11301563.translation: Septimius [X], veteran, lived 57 years, and Septimius Testas, veteran, lived 60 years.
EDCS-11301563.summary: This brief inscription records the ages at death for two individuals named Septimius, both veterans.

EDCS-11301565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Crispinilla vixit annis VIII mensibus III Titus Aelius Mucianus et Tullia Augusta filiae dulcissimae simul et sibi se vivi fecerunt et Aeliae Crispinae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit annis IIII mensibus VII\n
EDCS-11301565.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelia Crispinilla lived 8 years and 3 months. Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta made this for their sweetest daughter, and also for themselves while alive, and for their sweetest daughter Aelia Crispina, who lived 4 years and 7 months.
EDCS-11301565.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aelia Crispinilla and Aelia Crispina, with Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta dedicating it to their daughters and themselves.

EDCS-11301566.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Gaia vixit nnos XVIII Flavia Longinilla mater et Aelia Longina filia et Aelius Vitalis vivo suo coniugi carissimae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301566.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelia Gaia lived 18 years. Flavia Longinilla, mother, and Aelia Longina, daughter, and Aelius Vitalis erected this for their dearest wife and mother while alive, who deserved well.
EDCS-11301566.summary: Flavia Longinilla and Aelia Longina, mother and daughter, along with Aelius Vitalis, erected this tombstone for the dearly departed Aelia Gaia.

EDCS-11301567.text_edition: \n vixit annos XI liber infelicissimus Publius Aelius Satreus bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301567.translation: Lived 11 years. The most unfortunate child. Publius Aelius Satreus erected this for him who deserved well.
EDCS-11301567.summary: This inscription records the tragically short life of an unnamed child, dedicated by Publius Aelius Satreus.

EDCS-11301568.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antonia Veneria viva annorum LIII Masculus Licconis annorum LX se vivo sic sibi bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301568.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Antonia Veneria, alive, aged 53 years, and Masculus Licconis, aged 60 years, erected this for themselves while alive, for those who deserved well.
EDCS-11301568.summary: This tombstone was jointly dedicated by Antonia Veneria and Masculus Licconis for themselves while alive.

EDCS-11301569.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attediae Crescentillae vixit annos XVII et Attediae Caeciliae vixit annos VI Attedius Cresces pater et Valeria mater filiabus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301569.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Attedia Crescentilla, who lived 17 years, and for Attedia Caecilia, who lived 6 years. Attedius Cresces, father, and Valeria, mother, erected this for their daughters.
EDCS-11301569.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of Attedia Crescentilla and Attedia Caecilia, with their parents Attedius Cresces and Valeria as dedicants.

EDCS-11301570.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Castor.
EDCS-11301570.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph dedicated to Aurelius Castor.

EDCS-11301571.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus sic vixit nnos XXVII et Valeria Valentina vixit nnos XXVIII et Valeria Alexandria vixit nnos XIII Flavia Crispina mater filiis carissimis simul et sibi se sic viva posuit\n
EDCS-11301571.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus lived 27 years, and Valeria Valentina lived 28 years, and Valeria Alexandria lived 13 years. Flavia Crispina, mother, placed this for her dearest children and for herself while still alive.
EDCS-11301571.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the deaths of Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus, Valeria Valentina, and Valeria Alexandria, with their mother Flavia Crispina setting up the monument.

EDCS-11301572.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Gaianus vixit annos XX\n
EDCS-11301572.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Gaianus lived 20 years.
EDCS-11301572.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Aurelius Gaianus, who lived 20 years.

EDCS-11301573.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Theodosia. Theodosius.
EDCS-11301573.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Theodosia, possibly dedicated by Theodosius.

EDCS-11301574.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudius Cassianus tegitur cum coniuge Flavia iunctaque propinquis virgo his Cassia nata sex denos annos vixit pater bisque vicenos mater ter quinos vixit hos usque puella filii et fratres meritis sollemnia reddunt Cassius et Rufus Marinius sororque \n
EDCS-11301574.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudius Cassianus is buried with his wife, Flavia, and near his relatives. Cassia, the virgin daughter, lived 16 years. Her father lived twice twenty years, her mother thrice five years. Her sons and brothers, Cassius and Marinius Rufus, and her sister, render due solemn rites.
EDCS-11301574.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Cassianus, his wife Flavia, and their daughter Cassia, with details of their ages and remembrance by surviving family members.

EDCS-11301576.text_edition: \n A curaverunt Cornelius Isidoruster et Flav \n
EDCS-11301576.translation: Cornelius Isidoruster and Flav [took care of this].
EDCS-11301576.summary: A fragment of an inscription indicating that Cornelius Isidoruster and another person (Flav-) were responsible for its creation or maintenance.

EDCS-11301577.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Herenia sibi se viva fecit et Flaviae Maximillae filiae quae vixit annos XL\n
EDCS-11301577.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Herenia made this for herself while alive, and for her daughter Flavia Maximilla, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-11301577.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Flavia Herenia for herself and her deceased daughter, Flavia Maximilla.

EDCS-11301578.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Marcia vixit nnos LXX ex quibus nnos XXV mecum dulciter vixit Caius Valerius Aquilinus filius Valeri Viatoris coniugi dulcissimae bene merenti simul et sibi vivo posuit\n
EDCS-11301578.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Marcia lived 70 years, of which she lived 25 years sweetly with me, Caius Valerius Aquilinus. He placed this for his most sweet and deserving wife, and for himself while alive.
EDCS-11301578.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Caius Valerius Aquilinus for his wife Flavia Marcia, noting their 25 years of marriage and also for himself.

EDCS-11301579.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Prisca vixit annos XXVII hic sita est Lucius Aemilius Pulcher \n
EDCS-11301579.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Prisca lived 27 years. Lucius Aemilius Pulcher is placed here.
EDCS-11301579.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia Prisca, who lived 27 years, and also mentions Lucius Aemilius Pulcher.

EDCS-11301580.text_edition: \n PI T vixit annos Flavia Clodia coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-11301580.translation: [...] PI T lived [...] years. Flavia Clodia placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-11301580.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph where Flavia Clodia dedicates a monument to her husband, with some personal names or details lost.

EDCS-11301581.text_edition: \n Flavius Hermes Flavio Dom et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-11301581.translation: Flavius Hermes placed this for Flavius Dom and for himself.
EDCS-11301581.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Flavius Hermes for Flavius Dom and for himself.

EDCS-11301583.text_edition: \n vixit annos Heraclius vixit nnos V et Exsuperatus vixit annos III Septimius Herculanus pater et Aurelia Dinentilla mater filiis dulcissimis et sibi se viventibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301583.translation: Heraclius lived 5 years and Exsuperatus lived 3 years. Septimius Herculanus (father) and Aurelia Dinentilla (mother) set this up for their most beloved sons and for themselves while they were alive.
EDCS-11301583.summary: A funerary monument set up by parents for their deceased children and themselves, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301584.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Antonia vixit annos XXIX et Calbo alumnus vixit annos V Achilleus vilicus coniugi et alumno bene merentibus et Antoni Invictianus Achillius Achillia matri dulcissimae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301584.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Antonia lived 29 years and Calbo, her ward, lived 5 years. Achilleus, the steward, set this up for his deserving wife and ward, and for Antonius Invictianus, his mother, and Achillia, for their most beloved mother who deserved well.
EDCS-11301584.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Antonia and Calbo, set up by Achilleus, their steward, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301585.text_edition: \n vixit annos Marcia quAESPTAE marito et filio bene merentibus posuit et Aureliae Vitaliae filiae et sibi se vivis posuit\n
EDCS-11301585.translation: Marcia set this up for her deserving husband and son, and for Aurelia Vitalis, her daughter, and for herself, while she was alive.
EDCS-11301585.summary: A funerary monument erected by Marcia for her deserving husband, son, daughter, and herself, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301586.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Salvia Ingenua vixit nnos VII hic sita est Salvius Perpetuus et Valeria Musa filiae bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-11301586.translation: To the departed spirits. Salvia Ingenua lived 7 years. Salvius Perpetuus and Valeria Musa made this for their deserving daughter, who is buried here.
EDCS-11301586.summary: A funerary epitaph for Salvia Ingenua, erected by her parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301587.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Septimius Dus.
EDCS-11301587.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph mentioning Lucius Septimius Dus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301589.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Secundus vixit nnos LV Septimia Gallitta coniugi bene merenti VA \n
EDCS-11301589.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Ulpius Secundus lived 55 years. Septimia Gallitta for her deserving wife.
EDCS-11301589.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Secundus, set up by his wife Septimia Gallitta, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301590.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph mentioning Valens, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301591.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextus Valeri[us ...
EDCS-11301591.summary: A fragment of a funerary epitaph mentioning Sextus Valerius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301592.text_edition: \n LLG Marcus Valentinus vixit annos XX fili eius qui et here patri optimo bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-11301592.translation: Marcus Valentinus lived 20 years. His sons, who are also his heirs, arranged for this monument to be made for their most excellent and deserving father.
EDCS-11301592.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons for their deceased father, Marcus Valentinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301593.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Valerianus vixit annos LX Valerius Aquilinus parenti pientissimo una cum Claudio Caecilio ex delegato bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-11301593.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Valerianus lived 60 years. Valerius Aquilinus, together with Claudius Caecilius, by delegated authority, set this up for their most pious parent who deserved well.
EDCS-11301593.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Valerianus, erected by his son Valerius Aquilinus and Claudius Caecilius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301596.text_edition: \n Templum effectum per Cassium Achilleum et Publium Valerium Crescentem opifices inst \n
EDCS-11301596.translation: The temple was constructed by Cassius Achilleus and Publius Valerius Crescens, the craftsmen.
EDCS-11301596.summary: This inscription records the completion of a temple by two craftsmen, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301597.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records an imperial decree or honor, originating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301599.text_edition: \n inus miles legionis X invicto ex voto \n
EDCS-11301599.translation: a soldier of the invincible legion, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301599.summary: This inscription records a soldier's vow to an unnamed deity, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301600.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus securitati perpetuae Aureli ni dignissimi viri qui vixit annos LV perfecit in mil(itia) annos XX fili Licini karissimi vixit annos XXX Zelia Iustina coniux ex inpendio eorum donavit bene merentibus\n
EDCS-11301600.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For perpetual security. Aurelius [son of...] most worthy man, who lived 55 years, completed 20 years in military service. His son Licinius, dearest, lived 30 years. Zelia Iustina, his wife, dedicated this from their resources to the well-deserving.
EDCS-11301600.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Aurelius and his son Licinius, who both served in the military, and was dedicated by Aurelius' wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301601.text_edition: \n Hic situs est nomine Gaudentius vultu ac positione decorus qui annis vitam egit amplius viginta aeternam sedem PIVS LAV\n
EDCS-11301601.translation: Here lies Gaudentius, handsome in face and bearing, who lived for more than twenty years, [entering into] eternal rest, PIUS LAV[restored...]
EDCS-11301601.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Gaudentius, who lived for over twenty years and is described as handsome, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301609.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Iovi Maximo Cohortali Paterno Aurelius Catandio posuit aram libiens sic ex boto sic feliciter\n
EDCS-11301609.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Cohortal Father, Aurelius Catandius willingly set up this altar, in fulfillment of a vow, thus happily.
EDCS-11301609.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Catandius dedicating an altar to Jupiter in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301610.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cohortali Aurelius Ius<ti=IT>nianus pro se et suos sic votum libens posuit \n
EDCS-11301610.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Cohortal Father, Aurelius Iustinianus willingly set up this [altar] for himself and his family, thus fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11301610.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Iustinianus dedicating a vow to Jupiter for himself and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301611.text_edition: \n Aurelius Batao pro se et suos sic votu sic posuit\n
EDCS-11301611.translation: Aurelius Batao set this up for himself and his family, thus fulfilling his vow.
EDCS-11301611.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Batao dedicating something, likely an altar or offering, for himself and his family in fulfillment of a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-11301612.text_edition: \n Aurel pro salute sua et suorum votum libens posuit\n
EDCS-11301612.translation: Aurel [restored...] for his own health and that of his family, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-11301612.summary: This inscription records Aurel dedicating something for his health and that of his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-12100013.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et sex quae appellantur I civium Romanorum et II Flavia Commagenorum sagittaria et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Ituraeorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Vindelicorum civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I nnum veterana et I Montanorum et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Hispanorum et II nnum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Macedonica et III Campestris civium Romanorum et IIII Cypria civium Romanorum et V Gallorum et VIII Raetorum et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione a Iulio Sabino quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas pridie Idus Octobres Caio Iulio Proculo Caio Aburnio Valente consulibus cohortis I Montanorum cui praeest Cornelius Felicior ex pedite Marco Herennio Marci filio Polymitae Berensi et Ianuario filio eius et Marcello filio eius et Lucanae filiae eius <de>scriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et sex quae appellantur I civium Romanorum et I Flavia Commagenorum sagittaria et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Ituraeorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Vindelicorum civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I nnum veterana et I Montanorum et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Hispanorum et II nnum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Macedonica et III Campestris civium Romanorum et IIII Cypria civium Romanorum et V Gallorum et VIII Raetorum et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione a Iulio Sabino quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas pridie Idus Octobres Caio Iulio Proculo Caio Aburnio Valente consulibus cohortis I Montanorum cui praeest Cornelius Felicior ex pedite Marco Herennio Marci filio Polymitae Berensi et Ianuario et Marcello filiis eius et Lucanae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Tiberi Iuli Urbani Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Verecundi Publi Atini Amerimni Cai Iuli Parati Cai Tuticani Saturnini Marci Iuli Clementis\n
EDCS-12100013.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, Imperator for the 6th time, Consul for the 5th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry who served in three alae and sixteen cohorts, which are called I Civium Romanorum, II Flavia Commagenorum Sagittaria, II Vnorum Veterana, I Brittonum (Miliaria) Ulpia Torquata Civium Romanorum, I Britannica (Miliaria) Civium Romanorum, I Ituraeorum, I Thracum Civium Romanorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Vindelicorum Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Vnorum Veterana, I Montanorum, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Hispanorum, II Vnorum (Miliaria) Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, II Gallorum Macedonica, III Campestris Civium Romanorum, IIII Cypria Civium Romanorum, V Gallorum, and VIII Raetorum, and are in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and discharged with an honorable dismissal by Julius Sabinus, to whose names are subscribed, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, provided each man only took one wife. On the day before the Ides of October, in the consulship of Gaius Julius Proculus and Gaius Aburnius Valens. From Cohort I Montanorum, commanded by Cornelius Felicior, to the foot soldier Marcus Herennius, son of Marcus, Polymitas of Bera, and his sons Ianuarius and Marcellus, and his daughter Lucana, this was inscribed and recognized from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the deified Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva. Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, Imperator for the 6th time, Consul for the 5th time, Father of the Fatherland, to the cavalry and infantry who served in three alae and sixteen cohorts, which are called I Civium Romanorum, I Flavia Commagenorum Sagittaria, II Vnorum Veterana, I Brittonum (Miliaria) Ulpia Torquata Civium Romanorum, I Britannica (Miliaria) Civium Romanorum, I Ituraeorum, I Thracum Civium Romanorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Vindelicorum Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Vnorum Veterana, I Montanorum, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Hispanorum, II Vnorum (Miliaria) Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, II Gallorum Macedonica, III Campestris Civium Romanorum, IIII Cypria Civium Romanorum, V Gallorum, and VIII Raetorum, and are in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and discharged with an honorable dismissal by Julius Sabinus, to whose names are subscribed, granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, provided each man only took one wife. On the day before the Ides of October, in the consulship of Gaius Julius Proculus and Gaius Aburnius Valens. From Cohort I Montanorum, commanded by Cornelius Felicior, to the foot soldier Marcus Herennius, son of Marcus, Polymitas of Bera, and his sons Ianuarius and Marcellus, and his daughter Lucana, this was inscribed and recognized from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome on the Tiber, by the order of Urbanus, son of Julius, of Cornelius Alexander, son of Publius, of Verundus, son of Pullus, of Amerimnus, son of Atinius, of Paratus, son of Julius, of Saturninus, son of Tuticanus, of Clemens, son of Julius.
EDCS-12100013.summary: This extensive inscription, issued by Emperor Trajan in 100 AD, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of specific auxiliary units serving in Dacia, with a particular focus on Marcus Herennius and his family from Cohort I Montanorum.

EDCS-12100780.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VI imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VI designatus VII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus decem quae appellantur I Antiochensium et I Sugambrorum veterana et I Raetorum et I Lusitanorum et III et IV et V et VII et VIII Gallorum et Cisipadensium et sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Kalendas Maias Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano III Lucio Pasidieno Firmo consulibus cohortis I Raetorum cui praeest Caius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus pediti Herae Serapionis filio Antiochia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio post piscinam in tribunali deorum Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VI imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VI designatus VII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus decem quae appellantur I Antiochensium et I Sugambrorum veterana et I Raetorum et I Lusitanorum et III et IV et V et VII et VIII Gallorum et Cisipadensium et sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Kalendas Maias Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano III Lucio Pasidieno Firmo consulibus cohortis I Raetoriae sic cui praeest Caius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus pediti Herae Serapionis filio Antiochia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio Luci Domiti Luci fili Collina Veri Publi Coeli Quinti fili Falerna Bruti Rufi Quinti Iuli Lentuli Quinti Aquili Cai fili Voltinia Campani Titi Lossi Titi fili Galeria Severi Sexti Lossi Titi fili Galeria Apollinaris Publi Atini Publi fili Velina Augustalis\n
EDCS-12100780.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 6th time, commander-in-chief for the 14th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 6th time, designated for the 7th time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the foot soldiers and cavalrymen serving in the ten cohorts named I Antiochensium, I Sugambrorum veterana, I Raetorum, I Lusitanorum, III, IV, V, VII, and VIII Gallorum, and Cisipadensium, which are in Moesia under Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis, who had completed twenty-five years of service or more, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, together with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each man only one wife. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, behind the pool, in the tribunal of the gods, on the fourth day before the Kalends of May, during the consulship of Caesar Augustus' son Domitian for the 3rd time, and Lucius Pasidienus Firmus. For the soldier of Cohort I Raetorum, commanded by Gaius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus, the foot soldier Herae, son of Serapion from Antioch. This text is also duplicated for the cohort I Raetoriae, commanded by Gaius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus, for the foot soldier Herae, son of Serapion from Antioch, with further details on the bronze tablet affixed in Rome on the Capitol.
EDCS-12100780.summary: This imperial rescript, issued during the consulship of Domitian and Lucius Pasidienus Firmus, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to soldiers serving in specific cohorts in Moesia, recording the details on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12100784.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100784.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XXII consul XVII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in ala praetoria et cohortibus decem I Lusitanorum et I Cretum et I Montanorum et I Cilicum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et II Flavia Commagenorum et IIII Raetorum et V Hispanorum et VI Thracum et VII Breucorum civium Romanorum quae sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Longino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Idus Iulias Tito Prifernio Paeto Quinto Fabio Postumino consulibus cohortis VI Thracum cui praeest Claudius Alpinus pediti Dolenti Sublusi filio Besso et Valenti filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Quinti Aemili Soterichi Cai Iuli Vitalis Quinti Pompei Homeri Publi Atini Amerimni Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Trophimi Quinti Carrinati Quadrati Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XXII consul XVII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in ala praetoria et cohortibus decem I Lusitanorum et I Cretum et I Montanorum et I Cilicum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et II Flavia Commagenorum et IIII Raetorum et V Hispanorum et VI Thracum et VII Breucorum civium Romanorum et quae sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Longino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meru<e>runt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Idus Iulias Tito Prifernio Paeto Quinto Fabio Postumino consulibus cohortis VI Thracum cui praeest Claudius Alpinus pediti Dolenti Sublusi filio Besso et Valenti filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae\n
EDCS-12100784.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the divine Vespasian, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 15th time, commander-in-chief for the 22nd time, consul for the 17th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers serving in the Praetorian wing and the ten cohorts I Lusitanorum, I Cretum, I Montanorum, I Cilicum, I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria, II Flavia Commagenorum, IIII Raetorum, V Hispanorum, VI Thracum, and VII Breucorum civium Romanorum, which are in Upper Moesia under Cnaeus Aemilius Cicatricula Pompeius Longinus, who have completed twenty-five years of service or more, and also those discharged with an honorable discharge after completing their service, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, together with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each man only one wife. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, on the fourth day before the Ides of July, during the consulship of Titus Prifernius Paetus and Quintus Fabius Postuminus. For the soldier of Cohort VI Thracum, commanded by Claudius Alpinus, the foot soldier Dolens, son of Sublus, and his son Valentus from Bessus.
EDCS-12100784.summary: This imperial rescript, issued during the consulship of Titus Prifernius Paetus and Quintus Fabius Postuminus, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to soldiers serving in various units in Upper Moesia, based on honorable discharge after long service, and records the details on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12200269.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIIII consuli II patri patriae et Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae NE Lucio Catio Celere legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-12200269.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and to Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, and to Lucius Catius Celer, legate of the emperor with praetorian power.
EDCS-12200269.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III and Empress Sabinia Tranquillina, along with their legate Lucius Catius Celer, likely for their well-being or as part of a public religious act.

EDCS-12200270.text_edition: \n Manibus Dis Mucala Dydiae vixit nns sexaginta Pirusala Pirula sic fecit co<niug=LOE>i suo \n
EDCS-12200270.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Dydia Mucala lived for sixty years. Pirusala Pirula erected this for her husband.
EDCS-12200270.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Dydia Mucala, recording her age and that the tomb was erected by her husband, Pirusala Pirula.

EDCS-12300243.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300243.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator XXII consul XVI censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus novem quae appellantur II Pannoniorum et Claudia nova et praetoria et I Cilicum et I Cisipadensium et I Cretum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et I Antiochensium et II Gallorum Macedonica et IIII Raetorum et V Gallorum et V Hispanorum et sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Longino qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XVI Kalendas Domitianas Tito Pomponio Basso Lucio Silio Deciano consulibus cohortis I Cisipadensium cui praeest Lucius Cilnius Luci filius Pomptina Secundus pediti Lucio Cassio Cassi filio Lariseno descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Aucustus sic Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator XXII consul XVI censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibu<s=E> qui militant in alis tribus et cohortibus novem quae appellantur II Pannoniorum et Claudia nova et praetoria et I Cilicum <et=CI> I Cisipadensium et I Cretum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et I Antiochensium et II Gallorum Macedonica et IIII Raetorum et V Gallorum et V Hispanorum et sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Loncino sic qui quina et vicena stipendia Aut plura meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem XVI Kalendas Domitianas Tito Pomponio Basso Lucio Silio Deciano consulibus cohortis I Cisipadensium cui praeest Lucius Cilnius Luci filius Pomptina Secundus pediti Lucio Cassio Cassi filio Lariseno descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae Quinti Orfi Cupiti Cai Iuli Saturnini Quinti Aemili Soterichi Luci Pulli Sperati Cnaei Egnati Vitalis Luci Pulli Heraclaes sic Publi Cauli Vitalis \n
EDCS-12300243.translation: Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of the divine Vespasian, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 13th time, commander-in-chief for the 22nd time, consul for the 16th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers serving in the three wings and nine cohorts named II Pannoniorum, Claudia nova, praetoria, I Cilicum, I Cisipadensium, I Cretum, I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria, I Antiochensium, II Gallorum Macedonica, IIII Raetorum, V Gallorum, and V Hispanorum, which are in Upper Moesia under Cnaeus Aemilius Cicatricula Pompeius Longinus, who have completed twenty-five years of service or more, and also those discharged with an honorable discharge after completing their service, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, together with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each man only one wife. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, on the 16th day before the Kalends of the month of Domitian, during the consulship of Tito Pomponius Bassus and Lucius Silius Decianus. For the soldier of Cohort I Cisipadensium, commanded by Lucius Cilnius Pomptina Secundus, son of Lucius, for the foot soldier Lucius Cassius Larisenus, son of Cassius, from Larisa.
EDCS-12300243.summary: This imperial rescript, issued during the consulship of Tito Pomponius Bassus and Lucius Silius Decianus, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to soldiers serving in various units in Upper Moesia, based on honorable discharge after long service, and records the details on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12300271.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300271.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III peditibus qui militaverunt in cohorte I Flavia Bessorum quae est in Macedonia sub Octavio Antonino quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Kalendas Iulias Caio Publicio Marcello Lucio Rutilio Propinquo consulibus cohortis I Flaviae Bessorum cui praeest Aulus Aelius Sollemnianus ex pedite Marco Antonio Timi filio Timi Hierapoli et Doroturmae Dotochae filiae uxori eius Tricornio et Secundo filio eius et Marcellinae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III peditibus qui militaverunt in cohorte I Flavia Bessorum quae est in Macedonia sub Octavio Antonino quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si \n
EDCS-12300271.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to the foot soldiers who served in Cohort I Flavia Bessorum, which is in Macedonia under Octavius Antoninus, who have completed twenty-five years of service and were discharged with an honorable discharge, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, together with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if any were unmarried, with those they would later marry, each man only one wife. Recorded and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva, on the third day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of Gaius Publicius Marcellus and Lucius Rutilius Propinquus. For the foot soldier Marcus Antonius Timus, son of Timus from Hierapolis, and his wife Dotocha, daughter of Dorotura, and their children Tricornius and Secundus, and their daughter Marcellina, from the cohort I Flavia Bessorum, commanded by Aulus Aelius Sollemnianus.
EDCS-12300271.summary: This imperial rescript, issued during the consulship of Gaius Publicius Marcellus and Lucius Rutilius Propinquus, grants Roman citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of Cohort I Flavia Bessorum in Macedonia, and records the details on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-12700087.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori pro salute Titi Iuli Optati circitoris portorii publici Lucius Licinius Terminalis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12700087.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Repeller. For the health of Titus Julius Optatus, a circuit rider of the public customs dues, Lucius Licinius Terminalis willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-12700087.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Lucius Licinius Terminalis to Jupiter for the well-being of Titus Julius Optatus, an official involved in collecting public tolls.

EDCS-12800392.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Calvinus Marcus Aurelius Severinus bbenefficiarii consulares legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solverunt libentes merito Praesente et Extricato consulibus\n
EDCS-12800392.translation: Publius Aelius Calvinus and Marcus Aurelius Severinus, beneficiarii consulares of Legion II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow. During the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.
EDCS-12800392.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow by two beneficiarii consulares of the Legio II Adiutrix, made during the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.

EDCS-13900438.text_edition: \n Dardaniae sacrum Philoxenus Augggustorum nnnostrorum servus vilicus stationis Aquarum Bassianarum templum ex voto a solo restituit votum libens solvit Augustas Senecione et Aemiliano consulibus\n
EDCS-13900438.translation: Sacred to Dardania. Philoxenus, a servant and steward of our Augusti at the station of the Bassian Waters, restored the temple from the ground up according to his vow. He willingly fulfilled his vow during the consulship of Augustas Senecio and Aemilianus.
EDCS-13900438.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple dedicated to Dardania by Philoxenus, a servant of the emperors, and notes the fulfillment of his vow during the consulship of Augustas Senecio and Aemilianus.

EDCS-13900472.translation: Caise set this up according to a vow.
EDCS-13900472.summary: This is a simple dedication of a monument or object by Caise, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-15800384.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliani Daphni Thyatireni vixit annos XXX hic situs est Crispinus Daphni buleuta Thyatirenus fratri faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15800384.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulianus Daphnis of Thyatira lived for 30 years and is buried here. Crispinus Daphnis, a council member of Thyatira, arranged for this to be made for his brother.
EDCS-15800384.summary: A tombstone for Iulianus Daphnis, set up by his brother Crispinus Daphnis, a local official in Thyatira, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900080.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae Iulius Romanio et Aurelius Constans pro salute sua votum posuerunt\n
EDCS-15900080.translation: To Diana Regina, Iulius Romanio and Aurelius Constans set up this vow for their own well-being.
EDCS-15900080.summary: A dedication to the goddess Diana Regina, made by Iulius Romanio and Aurelius Constans for their health, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-15900082.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ex boto sic Caca et Iulius fratres\n
EDCS-15900082.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, according to a vow. Thus Caca and Iulius, brothers.
EDCS-15900082.summary: A votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, made by two brothers named Caca and Iulius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Atadis Dorani filius qui militavit numero DDalmatarum centuria Calvini vixit annos -XX Romus qui militavit in numerum sic Dalmatarum centuria vi\n
EDCS-15900084.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Atadis, son of Doranus, who served in the unit of Dalmatians, century of Calvinus, lived for over 20 years. Romus, who served in the unit of Dalmatians, century...
EDCS-15900084.summary: A tombstone fragment mentioning Atadis and Romus, who served in a Dalmatian military unit, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Mundo exarcho numeri Dalmatarum vixit annos XXX et Aureliae Surae vixit annos XX et Aureliae Augustae vixit annos VIIII Aurelius Myrcianus sic parentibus bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-15900085.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Mundus, exarch of the Dalmatian unit, lived for 30 years, and Aurelia Sura, lived for 20 years, and Aurelia Augusta, lived for 9 years. Aurelius Myrcianus set this up for his well-deserving parents.
EDCS-15900085.summary: A family tombstone commemorating Aurelius Mundus, Aurelia Sura, and Aurelia Augusta, with Aurelius Myrcianus as the dedicator, likely from the Roman period, possibly late antique.

EDCS-15900086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureli Maximiniani circitoris vixit nnos LII Aurelius Maximus veteranus \n
EDCS-15900086.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Maximinianus, a circuit warden, lived for 52 years. Aurelius Maximus, a veteran...
EDCS-15900086.summary: A tombstone fragment for Aurelius Maximinianus, a circuit warden, and Aurelius Maximus, a veteran, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Valerio Basso veterano legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos LX Lucius Valerius Valens \n
EDCS-15900090.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Valerius Bassus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, lived for 60 years. Lucius Valerius Valens...
EDCS-15900090.summary: A tombstone fragment for Lucius Valerius Bassus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900091.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Cai filio Papiria Firmo veterano legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis conscripto decurioni Ratiariae vixit nnos LXXV Caius Valerius Firmus decurioni coloniae eiusdem patri pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-15900091.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Valerius Firmus, son of Caius, of the Papirian tribe, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, pious and loyal, enrolled as a decurion of Ratiaria, lived for 75 years. Caius Valerius Firmus, a decurion of the same colony, arranged for this to be made for his most devoted father.
EDCS-15900091.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Firmus, a veteran and decurion of Ratiaria, erected by his son of the same name, a decurion in the same colony, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900093.text_edition: \n et Aureli Longini vixit annos VI et Aureli Bessi vixit annos V Aurelia Matiae sic et Aurelius Ursus ducenarius bene merentibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-15900093.translation: ...and Aurelius Longinus lived for 6 years, and Aurelius Bessus lived for 5 years. Aurelia Matia and Aurelius Ursus, a ducenarius, set this up for those who deserved well.
EDCS-15900093.summary: A tombstone fragment commemorating the children Aurelius Longinus and Aurelia Bessus, and possibly their parents or benefactors Aurelia Matia and Aurelius Ursus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-15900094.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Memmi Proculi Iunioris vixit annos II menses IX dies X Caius Memmius Proculus decurio coloniae Ratiariae et Cornelia Eventa filio piissimo\n
EDCS-15900094.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Memmius Proculus the Younger, lived for 2 years, 9 months, and 10 days. Gaius Memmius Proculus, a decurion of the colony of Ratiaria, and Cornelia Eventa for their most devoted son.
EDCS-15900094.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Memmius Proculus the Younger, erected by his parents Gaius Memmius Proculus, a decurion of Ratiaria, and Cornelia Eventa, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-16000547.translation: Of the Seventh Claudian Legion, the Pious, the Severian.
EDCS-16000547.summary: This inscription records the name of the Seventh Claudian Legion, likely part of a larger monument from the Severan period.

EDCS-16100277.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Aurelius Bassus sacerdos servus eius\n
EDCS-16100277.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Dolichenian. Aurelius Bassus, his priest and servant.
EDCS-16100277.summary: This is a dedication to the god Jupiter Dolichenus by his priest, Aurelius Bassus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16200805.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Flaminio Iuliano decurioni coloniae Ratiariae vixit [annos ------\n
EDCS-16200805.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Lucius Flaminius Iulianus, decurion of the colony of Ratiaria, who lived [for ------ years]
EDCS-16200805.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Flaminius Iulianus, a decurion of Ratiaria, who died at an unspecified age.

EDCS-16201641.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luci Tetti Rufi decurionis pontificis coloniae Ratiariae Fonteianus frater\n
EDCS-16201641.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Lucius Tettius Rufus, decurion and pontiff of the colony of Ratiaria. His brother Fonteianus [erected this].
EDCS-16201641.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Tettius Rufus, a decurion and pontiff of Ratiaria, set up by his brother Fonteianus.

EDCS-16300160.text_edition: \n Pali Sancto Pastorali Caius Iulius Valens decurio coloniae Ratiariae \n
EDCS-16300160.translation: To Saint Pastoral Palus. Caius Iulius Valens, decurion of the colony of Ratiaria.
EDCS-16300160.summary: This inscription honors Saint Pastoral Palus and records Caius Iulius Valens, a decurion of Ratiaria.

EDCS-16400137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio Valerio Alexandro qui vixit annos L Iulia Pollitta coniux et Valerii Marcianus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus Marcus Philiscus Alexandria Iulia filii patri bene merenti\n
EDCS-16400137.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Gaius Valerius Alexander, who lived 50 years. His wife Iulia Pollitta and his sons Valerius Marcianus, honored with decurional ornaments, and Marcus Philiscus and Alexandria Iulia, to their deserving father.
EDCS-16400137.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Alexander, set up by his wife and sons, with one son noted for his civic honors, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16400354.text_edition: \n Sub cura Boniti praepositi legionis VII\n
EDCS-16400354.translation: Under the supervision of Bonitus, prefect of the Seventh Legion.
EDCS-16400354.summary: This inscription indicates the supervision of an activity by Bonitus, a prefect of the Seventh Legion.

EDCS-16400355.summary: This fragmented inscription refers to a prefect of cavalry, likely from a military context.

EDCS-16600413.text_edition: \n Legio IIII sub cura Hermae principis\n
EDCS-16600413.translation: The Fourth Legion, under the supervision of Hermes, principalis.
EDCS-16600413.summary: This inscription records the supervision of some affair by Hermes, a principalis of the Fourth Legion.

EDCS-16600414.text_edition: \n Legio VII Claudia sub cura Victorini praepositi EB\n
EDCS-16600414.translation: The Seventh Claudian Legion, under the supervision of Victorinus, prefect.
EDCS-16600414.summary: This inscription notes the supervision of an activity by Victorinus, a prefect of the Seventh Claudian Legion.

EDCS-16600415.text_edition: \n Legio VII Claudia sub cura Victorini praepositi Flavius Vitalianus\n
EDCS-16600415.translation: The Seventh Claudia Legion, under the supervision of Victorinus, commander, by Flavius Vitalianus.
EDCS-16600415.summary: This inscription records the work of the Seventh Claudia Legion, supervised by Victorinus and executed by Flavius Vitalianus.

EDCS-16700053.translation: The Dalmatian Horsemen, at Aureus Mons.
EDCS-16700053.summary: This inscription likely records the presence or a dedication by Dalmatian Horsemen at Aureus Mons.

EDCS-16700327.text_edition: \n Legio VII sub cura Ursaci praefecti Argutio\n
EDCS-16700327.translation: The Seventh Legion, under the supervision of Ursacus, prefect, by Argutius.
EDCS-16700327.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Argutius, under the supervision of the prefect Ursacus, for the Seventh Legion.

EDCS-16700328.text_edition: \n Equites Dalmatae AR sub cura Veraci praepositi fecit Mucianus\n
EDCS-16700328.translation: The Dalmatian Horsemen, auxiliary troops, under the supervision of Veracus, commander, made by Mucianus.
EDCS-16700328.summary: This inscription records the work of Mucianus, supervised by Veracus, for the Dalmatian Horsemen, auxiliary troops.

EDCS-16700329.text_edition: \n Equites sagittari sub cura Italici Br--\n
EDCS-16700329.translation: The archer horsemen, under the supervision of Italicus, son of Br---.
EDCS-16700329.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Italicus, under the supervision of an unnamed commander, for the archer horsemen.

EDCS-16700331.summary: This inscription records a dedication or construction by Valerius, commander, possibly at a place called Tricornium.

EDCS-16700332.text_edition: \n Castra Viminacensia sub cura Senecionis ducis\n
EDCS-16700332.translation: The Viminacensian Castra, under the supervision of Senecio, duke.
EDCS-16700332.summary: This inscription records a dedication or construction at the Viminacensian Castra, supervised by Senecio, duke.

EDCS-16800215.text_edition: \n Iobi sic Optimo Maximo Dulicheno sic ex voto Aelii Silvanus het sic Leonides le<g=C>(ionis) signiferi bene mirenti sic \n
EDCS-16800215.translation: To Iobus, indeed to the Best and Greatest Jupiter Dulichenus, according to a vow; Aelius Silvanus, standard-bearer of the legion, and Leonidas, well deserving, according to a vow.
EDCS-16800215.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Dulichenus by Aelius Silvanus and Leonidas, a legionary standard-bearer, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century.

EDCS-16800239.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto Aurelius Herodes et Iulius Super pro salute collegii votum l(ibens) animo merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-16800239.translation: To Hercules the Unconquered, Aurelius Herodes and Julius Super, for the welfare of the guild, have set up this vow willingly and with a deserving spirit.
EDCS-16800239.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Aurelius Herodes and Julius Super for the health of their guild, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century.

EDCS-21000721.text_edition: \n Legio VII Claudia legatus Augusti S--- Aurelius Herculanus Martinianus fecit XX\n
EDCS-21000721.translation: The Seventh Claudia Legion, governor's legate S---, Aurelius Herculanus Martinianus made this, twenty.
EDCS-21000721.summary: This inscription records a construction or dedication by Aurelius Herculanus Martinianus, legatus Augusti for the Seventh Claudia Legion, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century.

EDCS-24500535.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Genio castrorum legionis IIII Flaviae Nassius Dexter praefectus [castrorum ------\n
EDCS-24500535.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Genius of the camp of the Fourth Flavia Legion, Nassius Dexter, camp prefect, [restored...]
EDCS-24500535.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and the Genius of the camp by Nassius Dexter, a camp prefect of the Fourth Flavia Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600680.text_edition: \n Dis deabus Placidi filius Ni Ulpianus laetus ex voto pro libens posuit\n
EDCS-26600680.translation: Ni Ulpianus, son of Placidas, gladly set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-26600680.summary: This is a votive dedication to the gods by Ni Ulpianus, son of Placidas, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600681.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Aurelius Gaius miles legionis VII strator consularis posuit\n
EDCS-26600681.translation: Aurelius Gaius, a soldier of the Seventh Legion and a quaestor's orderly, set this up to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of our lords the Augusti.
EDCS-26600681.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Aurelius Gaius, a soldier and orderly of the Seventh Legion, for the safety of the emperors, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600682.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Marcus Aurelius Valens miles legionis IIII strator consularis posuit\n
EDCS-26600682.translation: Marcus Aurelius Valens, a soldier of the Fourth Legion and a quaestor's orderly, set this up to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of our lords the Augusti.
EDCS-26600682.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Marcus Aurelius Valens, a soldier and orderly of the Fourth Legion, for the safety of the emperors, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600683.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute domini nostri Augusti Marcus Ulpius Martinus strator consularis legionis VII C<l=I>(audiae) Severianae Fusco et Dextro consulibus \n
EDCS-26600683.translation: Marcus Ulpius Martinus, a quaestor's orderly of the Seventh Claudian Severian Legion, set this up to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of our lord Augustus, in the consulship of Fuscus and Dexter.
EDCS-26600683.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Marcus Ulpius Martinus, an orderly of the Seventh Claudian Severian Legion, for the safety of the emperor, dated by the consuls Fuscus and Dexter, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-26600684.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo F Maximo Capitolino ex <v=B>oto posuerunt Nica Hermes pro salute domini nostri \n
EDCS-26600684.translation: Nica Hermes set this up as a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest Capitolinus, for the safety of our lord.
EDCS-26600684.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus by Nica Hermes for the safety of the emperor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600685.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Capitolino Deo Ulpius Rufus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-26600685.translation: Ulpius Rufus set this up as a vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest Capitolinus.
EDCS-26600685.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus by Ulpius Rufus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600687.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulguratori et Iunoni Reginae et Deo Libero et Hilarae Gaius Iulius Servandus veteranus et Flavii Numisius et Numisianus \n
EDCS-26600687.translation: Gaius Julius Servandus, a veteran, and Flavii Numisius and Numisianus, set this up to Jupiter Best and Greatest Fulgurator, and to Juno Regina, and to Liber Pater, and to Hilara.
EDCS-26600687.summary: This is a dedication to multiple deities by Gaius Julius Servandus, a veteran, and Flavii Numisius and Numisianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600688.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caia Aurelia Rufina vixit nnos XXX Titus Claudius Valerianus cornicularis tribuni coniugi pientissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-26600688.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caia Aurelia Rufina lived 30 years. Titus Claudius Valerianus, a cornicularius of the tribune, arranged for this to be made for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-26600688.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Caia Aurelia Rufina, set up by her husband Titus Claudius Valerianus, a military official, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600689.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Vindici veterano vixit nnos LX et Aeliae Serenae eius vixit annos XXX Cai Iuli Hermocora sic Nicias Florus Mestrius et Tzitzis liberti et Aelius Fecundus Cosmus bene merentibus faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-26600689.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Caius Julius Vindicus, a veteran, lived 60 years, and his wife Aelia Serena lived 30 years. Caius Julius Hermocora, Nicias Florus Mestrius, and Aelius Fecundus Cosmus, his freedmen, arranged for this to be made for those who deserved well.
EDCS-26600689.summary: This is a tomb inscription for the veteran Caius Julius Vindicus and his wife Aelia Serena, set up by their freedmen, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600690.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpius Aelianus vixit nnos XXV Atilia Ingenua coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-26600690.translation: To the departed spirits. Ulpius Aelianus lived 25 years. Atilia Ingenua placed this for her deserving husband.
EDCS-26600690.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpius Aelianus, set up by his wife, Atilia Ingenua.

EDCS-26600691.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Valentina vixit annis XX Aurelius Mi\n
EDCS-26600691.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Valentina lived 20 years. Aurelius Mi...
EDCS-26600691.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Aurelia Valentina, begun by Aurelius Mi[...].

EDCS-26600692.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertenaci sic Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate X imperatori XI consuli III proconsuli res publica sua Ulpiana curante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-26600692.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 11th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva; the state of Ulpiana, under the administration of Quintus Anicius Faustus, imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-26600692.summary: A dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Septimius Severus, likely set up by the city of Ulpiana during the prefecture of Quintus Anicius Faustus.

EDCS-26600693.text_edition: \n Iuliae Domnae Augustae matri castrorum res publica sua Ulpiana curante Quinto Anicio Fausto legato Augustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-26600693.translation: For Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp. The state of Ulpiana, under the administration of Quintus Anicius Faustus, imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-26600693.summary: A dedicatory inscription to Julia Domna, mother of the camp, set up by the city of Ulpiana during the prefecture of Quintus Anicius Faustus.

EDCS-26600694.text_edition: \n Maximo et super omnes fortissimo Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo patri patriae\n
EDCS-26600694.translation: To Maximus and, above all, the most courageous Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, father of the country.
EDCS-26600694.summary: A dedicatory inscription to Emperor Philip the Arab, praising his virtues.

EDCS-26600696.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Valens miles legionis IIII Flaviae vixit nnos XXXI militavit nnos VII Valerius Valerianus et Valeria Candida fratri carissimo et sivi sic vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600696.translation: To the departed spirits. Valerius Valens, a soldier of the IIII Flavia legion, lived 31 years, served 7 years. Valerius Valerianus and Valeria Candida placed this for their dearest brother, while still alive.
EDCS-26600696.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Valens, a soldier, erected by his siblings.

EDCS-26600698.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris kastrorum Achilleus eorundem servus posuit Kalendis Novembribus Sabino II et Anulino consulibus\n
EDCS-26600698.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and Julia Augusta, mother of the camp. Achilleus, their slave, dedicated this on the Kalends of November, during the consulship of Sabinus II and Anulinus.
EDCS-26600698.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus for the well-being of Emperor Caracalla and Julia Domna, set up by a slave named Achilleus in 199 AD.

EDCS-26600699.text_edition: \n Tiberio Caesare Augusti filio Augusto imperatore pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXXV legio IIII Scythica legio V Macedonica\n
EDCS-26600699.translation: Under Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 35th time. The IIII Scythica legion, the V Macedonica legion.
EDCS-26600699.summary: A military inscription listing legions under the authority of Emperor Tiberius during his 35th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-26600700.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII pater patriae consul III montibus excisis anconibus sublatis viam refecit\n
EDCS-26600700.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, rebuilt the road by cutting through mountains and removing obstacles.
EDCS-26600700.summary: An inscription detailing Emperor Trajan's public works, specifically the rebuilding of a road, likely from the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-26600702.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo vexillatio legionis VII Claudiae sub cura Gai Licini Rufini |(centurionis)\n
EDCS-26600702.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. A detachment of the VII Claudia legion, under the supervision of Gaius Licinius Rufus, centurion.
EDCS-26600702.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a vexillation of the VII Claudia legion, supervised by a centurion.

EDCS-26600703.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Valenti veterano legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos X\n
EDCS-26600703.translation: Tiberius Claudius Valens, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, lived 10 years.
EDCS-26600703.summary: This epitaph records the life of Tiberius Claudius Valens, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-26600704.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Ceionio Rufo nnorum VIII filio dulcissimo et Petissenae Magnae nnorum XXVIII mensium III coniugi karissimae Publius Ceionius Eunus fecit\n
EDCS-26600704.translation: To the divine spirits of Publius Ceionius Rufus, 8 years old, his dearest son, and Petissena Magna, 28 years and 3 months old, his dearest wife. Publius Ceionius Eunus made this.
EDCS-26600704.summary: This epitaph, set up by Publius Ceionius Eunus, commemorates his young son Publius Ceionius Rufus and his wife Petissena Magna.

EDCS-26600705.text_edition: \n Genio castrorum legionis IIII Flaviae felicis et ddominorum nnostrorum Diocletiani et Maximiani Auggustorum Aurelius Maximianus ex praefecto legionis eiusdem votum posuit\n
EDCS-26600705.translation: To the genius of the camp of the Legio IIII Flavia Felix and to our Lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti, Aurelius Maximianus, formerly prefect of the same legion, set up this vow.
EDCS-26600705.summary: Aurelius Maximianus, a former prefect of Legio IIII Flavia Felix, dedicated this vow to the genius of the legion and to the emperors Diocletian and Maximian.

EDCS-26600706.text_edition: \n splendidissumi sic bectigalis sic Ill<y=V>rici sub cura Iucundi Marci viri egregii procuratoris Augusti Be<ll=IE>icus Caesaris nostri verna vilicus Idibus Septembribus FANA LI \n
EDCS-26600706.translation: Of the most distinguished Be(ll)icus, of the fisc, under the care of Iucundus Marcius, a distinguished man, procurator of our Augustus. Bellicus Caesaris, our villa manager, on the Ides of September.
EDCS-26600706.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the role of Bellicus Caesaris as a villa manager and mentions a procurator, Iucundus Marcius, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-26600707.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Genis Domiti vixit nnos LX et Affuti Aristonis coniugi eius vivo Aelius Domitianus immunis librarius legionis IIII Flaviae parentibus piissimis posuit\n
EDCS-26600707.translation: To the divine spirits of Genis Domitius, who lived 60 years, and to Affutia Aristo, his wife, Aelius Domitianus, a clerk in the Legio IIII Flavia, set this up for his most dutiful parents while he was alive.
EDCS-26600707.summary: Aelius Domitianus, a clerk of Legio IIII Flavia, erected this tomb for his parents, Genis Domitius and Affutia Aristo.

EDCS-26600708.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Victorino viro <e=F>(gregio) infelicissimo iuveni qui vixit annos XXV menses VIIII dies VII Aelius Proculinus signifer legionis IIII Flaviae fratri piissimo bene merenti \n
EDCS-26600708.translation: To the divine spirits of Aelius Victorinus, an excellent man, a most unhappy youth who lived 25 years, 9 months, and 7 days. Aelius Proculinus, standard-bearer of the Legio IIII Flavia, set this up for his most dutiful and well-deserving brother.
EDCS-26600708.summary: Aelius Proculinus, a standard-bearer of Legio IIII Flavia, erected this tomb for his brother, Aelius Victorinus, who died young.

EDCS-26600709.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Blassius Nigellio speculator legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos XXXV \n
EDCS-26600709.translation: To the divine spirits of Lucius Blassius Nigellio, a speculator of the Legio VII Claudia, who lived 35 years.
EDCS-26600709.summary: This epitaph records the death of Lucius Blassius Nigellio, a speculator of the Legio VII Claudia, at the age of 35.

EDCS-26600710.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Oppiae Agatheni matri vixit annos LX et Iulio Quadrato patri veterano legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos LXV Herculano fratri vixit annos VIII Iulius Bassus miles legionis III Parthicae strator consularis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-26600710.translation: To the divine spirits of Oppia Agathene, mother, who lived 60 years, and of Julius Quadratus, father, veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, who lived 65 years, and of Herculanus, brother, who lived 8 years. Julius Bassus, soldier of the Legio III Parthica, strator consularis, set this up for them, who deserved well.
EDCS-26600710.summary: Julius Bassus, a soldier of Legio III Parthica, erected this tomb for his parents, Oppia Agathene and Julius Quadratus (a veteran of Legio VII Claudia), and his brother Herculanus.

EDCS-26600711.text_edition: \n Caius Refidius Cai libertus Eutychus faber argentarius vixit hic situs est Caius Refidius Rufus optio legionis IIII Flaviae felicis heres posuit\n
EDCS-26600711.translation: Caius Refidius Eutychus, freedman of Caius Refidius, a silversmith, lived here. Caius Refidius Rufus, optio of the Legio IIII Flavia Felix, his heir, set this up.
EDCS-26600711.summary: This epitaph states that Caius Refidius Eutychus, a silversmith and freedman, is buried here, and his heir, Caius Refidius Rufus, an optio of Legio IIII Flavia Felix, erected it.

EDCS-26600712.text_edition: \n Lucio Valerio Sexti filio Voltinia Serano Luco |(centurioni) legionis IIII Flaviae felicis Lucius Valerius Privatus et Lucius Valerius Martialis liberti et heredes a se faciendum curaverunt non fui maritus e<t=I> r<e=F>liqui liberos servi domin<o=IS> macte <tu=IN> vale Valerius Secundus Valerius Cerdo liibertus sic Valerius Crescens liibertus sic Valerius Verecundus liibertus sic Valerius Gemelinus liibertus sic Valerius Abrio sic liibertus sic Valeria Aphrodisia liiberta sic Valerius Amp<l=E>iatus heres libertus \n
EDCS-26600712.translation: To Lucius Valerius Seranus, son of Lucius Valerius Sextus, centurion of the Legio IIII Flavia Felix.
