EDCS-29700317.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Antonini filius divi Veri Parthici maximi frater divi Hadriani nepos divi Traiani Parthici pronepos divi Nervae abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XXXIII imperator VIII consul III pater patriae Imperatoris Caesaris Flavi Valeri Constantini Pii Felicis maximi victoris ac trimphatoris semper Augusti milia XII M\n
EDCS-29700317.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, brother of the deified Verus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus Sarmaticus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 33rd time, imperator for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. [Dedication] for Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Maximus Victor and Triumpher Always Augustus, 12 miles.
EDCS-29700317.summary: This is a milestone inscription for Marcus Aurelius, noting his titles and tribunician power, and mentioning a distance related to Constantine the Great, likely indicating a road section, dated to around 191 CE.

EDCS-29700318.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Traiani Parthici filio divi Nervae nepoti Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli ICIIIOR a colonia Scupinorum milia passuum I\n
EDCS-29700318.translation: To Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul for the 103rd time, [from] the colony of the Scupinorum, 1 mile.
EDCS-29700318.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, indicating a distance of one mile from the colony of Scupi, dated to his reign (117-138 CE).

EDCS-29700319.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus [Antoninus ------\n
EDCS-29700319.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus [Antoninus ------
EDCS-29700319.summary: This is the beginning of an inscription honoring Emperor Antoninus Pius, son of the deified Aelius Hadrianus, dating to his reign (138-161 CE).

EDCS-29700322.summary: This inscription records an approval by Gaianus, likely a formal endorsement or judgment, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100673.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Gaius Iulius Valens veteranus legionis VII Claudiae ex beneficiario consularis nunc decurio Viminaci templum a solo refecit\n
EDCS-30100673.translation: To the Unconquered god Mithras, Gaius Iulius Valens, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, formerly a beneficiary of a consular legate, and now a decurion of Viminacium, rebuilt the temple from its foundations.
EDCS-30100673.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a temple to Mithras by Gaius Iulius Valens, a former soldier and now a decurion in Viminacium, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100675.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Procula vixit nnos XXXIII titulum posuit Aurelius Victor miles cohortis II Aureliae novae Sacorum coniugi bene merenti\n
EDCS-30100675.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Aurelia Procula lived 33 years. Aurelius Victor, a soldier of the II Aurelia Nova cohort of the Sacae, placed this monument to his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-30100675.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Aurelia Procula at age 33 and was erected by her husband, Aurelius Victor, a soldier, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100848.text_edition: \n Marcus Titius Marci filius Quirina Proculus Fabia Roma praefectus cohortis I Alpinorum tribunus militum legionis IIII Flaviae felicis vixit annos XXX menses IIII dies XIII Marcus Titius Zosimus patrono bene merenti fecit Dis Manibus\n
EDCS-30100848.translation: Marcus Titius Proculus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, from Fabia in Rome, prefect of the I Alpinorum cohort, military tribune of the IIII Flavia Felix legion, lived 30 years, 4 months, and 13 days. Marcus Titius Zosimus made this for his well-deserving patron. To the Spirits of the departed.
EDCS-30100848.summary: This tombstone inscription records the life of Marcus Titius Proculus, who held several military commands, and was erected by his patron, Marcus Titius Zosimus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30100849.text_edition: \n Deo Totovitioni Aurelius Agathominus sic miles legionis IIII Flaviae cataractarum stationis Dianae honeste votum libiens posuit\n
EDCS-30100849.translation: To the god Totovitus, Aurelius Agathominus, a soldier of the IIII Flavia legion at the Diana outpost, willingly set up this honorable vow.
EDCS-30100849.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Agathominus, a soldier stationed at Diana, to the god Totovitus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200391.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Augustianus decurio duumviralicius municipii Celegerorum vixit nnos XXXV Bella coniugi rarissimo pientissima sic et filii patri bene merito memoriam\n
EDCS-30200391.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Aurelius Augustianus, a decurio and duumviral official of the municipium of Celegerorum, lived 35 years. Bella, his most devoted and rare wife, and the sons for their well-deserving father, established this memorial.
EDCS-30200391.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Aurelius Augustianus at age 35 and was jointly erected by his wife Bella and their sons, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200712.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Flavianus decurio coloniae Ratiariae vixit annos L stat titulus bene meriti filii coniuxque po<s=B>uerunt \n
EDCS-30200712.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Aurelius Flavianus, a decurio of the colony of Ratiaria, lived 50 years. His devoted son and wife placed this monument for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-30200712.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Aurelius Flavianus, a decurio of Ratiaria, at age 50, and was erected by his son and wife, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200717.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Alexander |(centurio) legionis VII Claudiae et Iulia Onesime coniunx eius Aurelianeni alumnae suae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200717.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Aurelius Alexander, a centurion of the VII Claudia legion, and Iulia Onesime, his wife, placed this for Aurelianen, their well-deserving foster-child.
EDCS-30200717.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Aurelius Alexander, a centurion, and his wife Iulia Onesime, who jointly erected it for their foster-child Aurelianen, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200718.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Heliodorus veteranus legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos XXXXVI militavit nnos XXVI Marcus Calidius Bassus et Marcus Ulpius Maximus heredes bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200718.translation: To the Spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Heliodorus, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, lived 46 years and served 26 years. Marcus Calidius Bassus and Marcus Ulpius Maximus, the heirs, placed this for the well-deserving man.
EDCS-30200718.summary: This tombstone inscription records the death of Marcus Aurelius Heliodorus, a veteran of the VII Claudia legion, at age 46 after 26 years of service, and was erected by his heirs, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200719.text_edition: \n Lucius Caesius Luci filius Aniensi Flaccus Caesara sic Augusta |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae felicis vixit annos X\n
EDCS-30200719.translation: Lucius Caesius Flaccus, son of Lucius, of the Aniensi tribe, from Caesarea Augusta, a centurion of the IIII Flavia Felix legion, lived 10 years.
EDCS-30200719.summary: This tombstone inscription records the short life of Lucius Caesius Flaccus, a young centurion of the IIII Flavia Felix legion, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200720.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Fabio Capreolo liberto optimo Marcus Fabius Paternus tribunus legionis VII Claudiae patronus\n
EDCS-30200720.translation: To the divine shades. Marcus Fabius Paternus, tribune of the VII Claudia legion and patron, [erected this] to Marcus Fabius Capreolus, his excellent freedman.
EDCS-30200720.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a patron to his freedman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200721.text_edition: \n Valerius Isauricus Collina Isaur<a=O> decurio ex singularibus militavit nnos XV vixit nnos XXXIIII Lucius Nonius Marcellinus fratri pientissimo posuit \n
EDCS-30200721.translation: Valerius Isauricus. Collina Isaura, a decurion from the select soldiers, served for 15 years and lived for 34 years. Lucius Nonius Marcellinus erected [this] to his most devoted brother.
EDCS-30200721.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Isauricus, a soldier who served in the Roman army, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200722.text_edition: \n Lucius Oppius Titi filius Claudia Secundus Varvaria primus pilus legionis VII Claudiae praefectus kastrorum legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis\n
EDCS-30200722.translation: Lucius Oppius, son of Titus, Claudia Secundus, primus pilus of the VII Claudia legion, prefect of the camp of the VII Claudia pia fidelis legion.
EDCS-30200722.summary: An honorific inscription for Lucius Oppius, detailing his distinguished military career within the VII Claudia legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200723.text_edition: \n NI ETVI IR legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos XXXVII Laricia quae et Meter coniugi piissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30200723.translation: [Name illegible, possibly military rank or unit identifier], of the VII Claudia legion, lived for 37 years. Laricia, who is also called Meter, erected [this] to her most devoted husband.
EDCS-30200723.summary: A funerary inscription for an individual serving in the VII Claudia legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200726.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Manius Servius Mani libertus Thallus Augustalis municipii Aeli Viminacii vixit nnos LXX Manius Servius Silvanus filius et heres bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30200726.translation: To the divine shades. Manius Servius Thallus, freedman of Manius Servius, Augustalis of the municipality of Aelia Viminacium, lived for 70 years. Manius Servius Silvanus, son and heir, erected [this] to him as one who deserved well.
EDCS-30200726.summary: A funerary epitaph for Manius Servius Thallus, an Augustalis from Aelia Viminacium, erected by his son and heir, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200728.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Aurelius Lucidus vixit nnos L \n
EDCS-30200728.translation: To the divine shades. Titus Aurelius Lucidus lived for 50 years.
EDCS-30200728.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Titus Aurelius Lucidus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200730.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Proculae vixit annos XI menses III dies II Caius Iulius Herculanus et Iulia Longina filiae bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200730.translation: To the divine shades. Julia Procula lived for 11 years, 3 months, and 2 days. Caius Iulius Herculanus and Iulia Longina erected [this] to their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-30200730.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl, Julia Procula, erected by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200731.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-30200731.text_edition: \n Lupus anemola sic hic havetat sic quot sic comidi sic mecum haveo sic ego Maurentia in hunc sic monumentum sic titulum posui Lupo virginio meo cum quem sic quinquaginta annis beni sic laboravi adque inculpatim convixi et Argenteo Samarconi fratris sic vel subulellae matris meae ipsum titulum feci\n
EDCS-30200731.translation: Lupus, may you live well! So, I ate well, so I have [things] well with me. So, I set up this monument and inscription for Lupus Virginius, my beloved, with whom for fifty years I lived and worked blamelessly, and for Argenteus Samarconis, my brother, or my mother's [relation]. I made this inscription myself.
EDCS-30200731.summary: A personal and affectionate funerary monument erected by a woman for her beloved Lupus Virginius, with whom she shared a long life, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200732.translation: To the divine shades. Marcus [Rest of inscription missing].
EDCS-30200732.summary: A partial funerary inscription beginning with a dedication to the divine shades and the name Marcus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200733.text_edition: \n pietati IR Pudenti et Varena Crispinill \n
EDCS-30200733.translation: [Erected to] Pudenti and Varena Crispinilla for [their] devotion [Rest of inscription missing].
EDCS-30200733.summary: A fragmentary inscription possibly honoring or recording a dedication to Pudenti and Varena Crispinilla, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30200734.summary: A short funerary inscription, likely a tombstone, dedicated to the Manes.

EDCS-30200736.text_edition: \n Parvolus hic situs est Ulpius Iovinus nomine qui vixit nnos V menses VI dies XXIII hic solus pater hunc humavit parvolum cuius desiderio lumen intentus dolet Ulpius Valerianus beneficiarius legati pater a quo sibi sperabat\n
EDCS-30200736.translation: Parvolus, named Ulpius Iovinus, lies here, who lived for 5 years, 6 months, and 23 days. His father alone buried this little one, from whom, in his grief, Ulpius Valerianus, a beneficiarius of the legate, whose father he hoped for.
EDCS-30200736.summary: A poignant epitaph for a young boy, Ulpius Iovinus, who died at the age of 5 years, 6 months, and 23 days, buried by his father, Ulpius Valerianus, who is identified as a beneficiarius of a legate.

EDCS-30200740.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Soianus Paterionis vixsit sic annis LXXX Po coniugi rarissimo et sibi et Marco Aurelio Paterioni filio filio merenti p(osuit)\n
EDCS-30200740.translation: To the departed spirits. Soianus lived 80 years. He placed this for Po, his most rare wife, and for himself, and for his deserving son, Marcus Aurelius Paterioni.
EDCS-30200740.summary: A funerary inscription by Soianus for his wife Po and their son Marcus Aurelius Paterioni, who lived to be 80 years old.

EDCS-30200742.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Gellius Exoratus praefectus cohortis V Lucensium votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-30200742.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius Gellius Exoratus, prefect of the 5th Cohort of Lucensium, paid his vow willingly, joyfully, as was deserved.
EDCS-30200742.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Gellius Exoratus, prefect of the 5th Cohort of Lucensium, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-30200743.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Scribonius Faustus veteranus e<t=I> Aurelia Pasina VCRCX \n
EDCS-30200743.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Scribonius Faustus, a veteran, and Aurelia Pasina, VCRCX.
EDCS-30200743.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Scribonius Faustus, a veteran, and Aurelia Pasina, possibly a priestess or official.

EDCS-30200745.text_edition: \n di opus perfectum curante Ulpio Ulpiano Albino II et Maximo consulibus\n
EDCS-30200745.translation: The work was completed under the supervision of Ulpius Ulpianus Albinus (as consul II) and Maximus (as consul).
EDCS-30200745.summary: A record of the completion of a project during the consulship of Ulpius Ulpianus Albinus and Maximus.

EDCS-30200746.text_edition: \n Dei Zbelsurdi SIL Catulus Munatianus G pro salute parentium et fratrum\n
EDCS-30200746.translation: To the god Zbelsurdus, SIL Catulus Munatianus G, for the safety of his parents and brothers.
EDCS-30200746.summary: A dedication to the god Zbelsurdus by Catulus Munatianus for the well-being of his family.

EDCS-30200748.text_edition: \n vixit annos LXXX Marcus Ulpius Surio matri pientissimae posuit\n
EDCS-30200748.translation: Marcus Ulpius Surio placed this for his most devoted mother, who lived 80 years.
EDCS-30200748.summary: A funerary inscription by Marcus Ulpius Surio honoring his mother, who lived to be 80 years old.

EDCS-30200751.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Lucius miles legionis IIII Flaviae Alexandrianae votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200751.translation: Marcus Aurelius Lucius, a soldier of the 4th Flavian Alexandrian Legion, placed his vow willingly, as was deserved.
EDCS-30200751.summary: A votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Lucius, a soldier of the 4th Flavian Alexandrian Legion.

EDCS-30200752.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Attianus miles cohortis Aureliae D[ardanorum ------\n
EDCS-30200752.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Attianus, a soldier of the Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, [died].
EDCS-30200752.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Attianus, a soldier of the Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, with a lacuna indicating missing information.

EDCS-30200753.text_edition: \n vixit annos IIII positus est Aurelius Bitus pater et Aurelia Candida mater filio carissimo et nepoti et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200753.translation: He lived for 4 years. Aurelius Bitus the father and Aurelia Candida the mother placed this for their dearest son, and grandson, and for themselves while living.
EDCS-30200753.summary: A tombstone recording the death of a child, erected by his parents for themselves and their grandson.

EDCS-30200754.text_edition: \n us Bassi fratri bene merenti hic situs est\n
EDCS-30200754.translation: Here lies Bassus, a brother well-deserving.
EDCS-30200754.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of Bassus, a well-deserving brother.

EDCS-30200755.translation: Candidus placed this for his well-deserving son.
EDCS-30200755.summary: A tombstone recording a son erected by his father, Candidus.

EDCS-30200756.text_edition: \n Deae Fortunae pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Augusti et Caesaris Aelius Flavius Restutus sua pecunia Grato et Sel<e=A>uco consulibus Aurelius Dexter sacerdos posuit <l=I>(ibens) merito \n
EDCS-30200756.translation: To the Goddess Fortune, for the health of our lords Augustus and Caesar. Aelius Flavius Restutus placed this at his own expense, with Gratus and Seleuco as consuls, Aurelius Dexter the priest willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-30200756.summary: A dedication to the goddess Fortuna for the safety of the emperors, funded by Aelius Flavius Restutus and placed by the priest Aurelius Dexter, dating to the consulship of Gratus and Seleuco.

EDCS-30200757.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius C S votum posuit\n
EDCS-30200757.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius C S placed this vow.
EDCS-30200757.summary: A votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius C S.

EDCS-30200758.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque diis deabusque omnibus Marcus Aurelius Marcianus beneficiarius consularis legionis I Se[verianae Ale][xandrianae] piae vindicis Maximo et Urbano consulibus\n
EDCS-30200758.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to all the other gods and goddesses, Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, beneficiary of the consul, of the I Severiana Alexandriana Pia Vindex legion, placed this during the consulship of Maximus and Urbanus.
EDCS-30200758.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and all gods and goddesses by Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, a beneficiary of the consul from the I Severiana legion, dating to the consulship of Maximus and Urbanus.

EDCS-30200759.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Aepilofio sic Sanc Oriens Cornelius Mide Publius Aelius Cocaius veterani legionis VII Claudiae Severianae ex voto posuerunt Maximo et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-30200759.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Paterno Aepilofio, and to Sanc Oriens, Cornelius Mide and Publius Aelius Cocaius, veterans of the VII Claudia Severiana legion, placed this in fulfillment of a vow during the consulship of Maximus and Aelianus.
EDCS-30200759.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other deities by veterans of the VII Claudia Severiana legion, dating to the consulship of Maximus and Aelianus.

EDCS-30200761.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Cassius Candidus miles legionis VII Claudiae discens epibeta sic vixit annos XXI menses dies IIII militavit annos \n
EDCS-30200761.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Cassius Candidus, a soldier of the VII Claudia legion, a student of epibeta, lived for 21 years, 4 months, and days; he served for years.
EDCS-30200761.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Cassius Candidus, a soldier of the VII Claudia legion, recording his age and service duration.

EDCS-30200763.text_edition: \n ? posuerunt Vitalis us Iulite Iulius Iulianus patri bene merenti et matri vivae\n
EDCS-30200763.translation: Vitalis... placed this for his well-deserving father and his mother while alive.
EDCS-30200763.summary: A tombstone erected by Vitalis for his deserving father and living mother.

EDCS-30200766.text_edition: \n Caius Cassius Lo veteranus cohortis I Thracum Syriacae vixit annis C Caius Cassius Alexander Titus Flavius Feliculanus Flavia Prima heredes patrono bene merenti posuerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-30200766.translation: Caius Cassius Lo, a veteran of the I Cohort of Thracians from Syria, lived for 100 years. Caius Cassius Alexander, Titus Flavius Feliculanus, and Flavia Prima, his heirs, placed this for their well-deserving patron. Here he lies.
EDCS-30200766.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Caius Cassius Lo, a veteran of the I Cohort of Thracians, who lived to 100, and erected by his heirs.

EDCS-30200767.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Valens miles cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum vixit annis L militavit annis XVIII Rustia Respecta coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30200767.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavius Valens, a soldier of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, lived for 50 years and served for 18 years. Rustia Respecta placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-30200767.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavius Valens, a soldier, set up by his wife Rustia Respecta.

EDCS-30200768.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Herculanus veteranus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis se vivo posuit et Antoniae Vibiae coniugi bene merenti vixit annos L hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-30200768.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Julius Herculanus, a veteran of the Seventh Claudian legion, Pious and Faithful, set this up for himself while alive, and for his well-deserving wife Antonia Vibis. He lived 50 years. This monument will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-30200768.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by a veteran, Gaius Julius Herculanus, for himself and his wife, specifying it is not inheritable.

EDCS-30200769.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Florius Lib vixit annos XXV et Aurelia Imation vixit annos XXX Publius Floria Quarta fratri et matri bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-30200769.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Florius Lib lived 25 years and Aurelia Imation lived 30 years. Publius Floria Quarta placed this for her well-deserving brother and mother.
EDCS-30200769.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Florius Lib and Aurelia Imation, set up by their sister and daughter, Publius Floria Quarta.

EDCS-30200770.text_edition: \n inus Dinitralis Tarsa es eques cohortis I Thracum Syriacae turma Longi vixit nnos LXI meruit stipendia XXVIII ex testamento eius heres faciendum curavit h(ic) situs e(st)\n
EDCS-30200770.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Tarsus son of Dinitralis, a cavalryman of the First Thracian Cohort of Syria, belonging to the troop of Longus, lived 61 years and served for 28 years. His heir arranged for this to be made according to his will. He lies here.
EDCS-30200770.summary: A funerary epitaph for Tarsus, a cavalryman, set up by his heir according to his will, indicating service and age.

EDCS-30200772.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cale vixit annis XXII Aelius Fortunatus uxori bene merenti posuit locum \n
EDCS-30200772.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Cale lived 22 years. Aelius Fortunatus placed this for his well-deserving wife. He dedicated the place.
EDCS-30200772.summary: A funerary epitaph for Cale, a wife, set up by Aelius Fortunatus, who also dedicated the burial plot.

EDCS-30200773.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Severa sibi se vivo sic simul et Aelis Secundino et Ianuario et Suro filiis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-30200773.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Claudia Severa set this up for herself while alive, and also for her well-deserving sons, Aelis Secundinus, Ianuarius, and Sura.
EDCS-30200773.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Claudia Severa for herself and her sons while she was alive.

EDCS-30200774.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Iulius Rufus vixit nnos XIX et Iulia Iulitta vixit annos V hic siti sunt Lucius Iulius Valens pater filiis bene merentibus et sibi vivus posuit\n
EDCS-30200774.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Lucius Julius Rufus lived 19 years and Julia Iulitta lived 5 years. They lie here. Lucius Julius Valens, their father, set this up for his well-deserving children and for himself while alive.
EDCS-30200774.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Julius Valens for his children, Lucius Julius Rufus and Julia Iulitta, and for himself while alive.

EDCS-30200775.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Trebia Lucia vixit annos XXXV Sall Valens veteranus cum filiis eius coniugi bene merenti et sibi vivo posuit\n
EDCS-30200775.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Trebia Lucia lived 35 years. Sall [Valens?], a veteran, set this up with his sons for his well-deserving wife and for himself while alive.
EDCS-30200775.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Sall Valens, a veteran, with his sons for his wife Trebia Lucia and for himself.

EDCS-30200776.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Marcus vixit annis XVIIII a latronibus interfectus Valerius Eutychus et Sextilia Frontina filio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-30200776.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Marcus lived 17 years and was killed by brigands. Valerius Eutychus and Sextilia Frontina placed this for their well-deserving son.
EDCS-30200776.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valerius Marcus, who was killed by brigands, set up by his parents Valerius Eutychus and Sextilia Frontina.

EDCS-30200777.text_edition: \n vitam insidiis in sacra urbe finivit Publius Maius Clementinus eques Romanus filio dulcissimo bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-30200777.translation: He ended his life by treachery in the holy city. Publius Maius Clementinus, a Roman knight, placed this for his dearest, well-deserving son.
EDCS-30200777.summary: A funerary epitaph for the son of Publius Maius Clementinus, a Roman knight, noting the son's death by treachery in Rome.

EDCS-30200778.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Tiberi filio Quirina Mercuriali praefecto cohortis I Montanorum Claudia Aristonice marito suo cum Claudio Aristonicole li S\n
EDCS-30200778.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Mercurialis, son of Tiberius, of the Quirina tribe, Prefect of the First Cohort of Montani, Claudia Aristonice to her husband.
EDCS-30200778.summary: This is a dedication on a monument honoring Tiberius Claudius Mercurialis, a military officer, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200780.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Let vixit nnos LX TVA OV \n
EDCS-30200780.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Marcus Aurelius, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200781.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, Cemeia Mes[--] [illegible lacuna]
EDCS-30200781.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Cemeia, likely from the Roman imperial period, with a significant portion illegible.

EDCS-30200782.text_edition: \n L Valeriae Ser coniugi pientissimae bene meritae memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-30200782.translation: For L. Valeria Ser, his most devoted and well-deserving wife, he erected this memorial.
EDCS-30200782.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by an unnamed individual for his wife, L. Valeria Ser, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200783.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Martialis ex protectoribus vixit annos centum bene merito patrono suo posuit Maxentius gener et servius suus\n
EDCS-30200783.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Martialis, formerly one of the Protectors, lived 100 years. Maxentius, his freedman and servant, erected this for his well-deserving patron.
EDCS-30200783.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Martialis, a former soldier, set up by his freedmen, likely dating to the late Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200785.text_edition: \n Licini Auguste semper vincas SIC X SIC XX Naiss-\n
EDCS-30200785.translation: Licinius, August, may you always conquer! [---]
EDCS-30200785.summary: This is likely an inscription related to a chariot race or other competition, referencing an emperor or imperial figure, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200794.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Cretus Papiria lateres cusit sic e Ratiaria\n
EDCS-30200794.translation: Lucius Valerius Cretus of the Papiria tribe made bricks at Ratiaria.
EDCS-30200794.summary: This inscription records that Lucius Valerius Cretus was a brickmaker and potentially identifies the location of production in Ratiaria, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30200797.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Atalantes vixit annis L Publius Aelius Augusti libertus Menander |(centurio) o<f=E>ficinarum coniugi pientissimae bene merenti hic sita est \n
EDCS-30200797.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia Atalantes lived 50 years. Publius Aelius Menander, freedman of Augustus, a centurion of the workshops, erected this for his most devoted and well-deserving wife, who is buried here.
EDCS-30200797.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Atalantes, erected by her husband, a centurion of the workshops, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30300712.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae IF LAP Iulius Eutychus patronus et contubernalis - faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30300712.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Julia, [---]. Iulius Eutychus, her patron and fellow-soldier, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-30300712.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julia, arranged by her patron and fellow-soldier Iulius Eutychus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30300713.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio R Caius Iulius Ingenuus patri et fratribus bene merentibus fecit et Iulia T<hr=RH>epte vixit annos LX mater \n
EDCS-30300713.translation: For Gaius Iulius Rufus. Caius Iulius Ingenuus erected this for his father and brothers, who deserved well. And Iulia Tep[--] lived 60 years, mother.
EDCS-30300713.summary: This is a funerary monument for Gaius Iulius Rufus and his relatives, erected by Caius Iulius Ingenuus, and also records the lifespan of Iulia Tep[--], likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-30300714.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Marci filio Sergia Apollinari hic situs Apollo\n
EDCS-30300714.translation: Here lies Marcus Ulpius Apollinaris, son of Marcus, of the Sergia tribe.
EDCS-30300714.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Apollinaris, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300715.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus MarIo vernae vixit annos Aelia Cai liberta marito optimo \n
EDCS-30300715.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelia, freedwoman of Caius, erected this for her husband Mario, a freedman, who lived X years.
EDCS-30300715.summary: This funerary inscription honors Mario, the husband of Aelia, and was likely erected in the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300716.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Marcia ipsa viva sibi et caro coniugi Ravio defuncto hanc memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-30300716.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Marcia, while still alive, set up this memorial for herself and for her dearly departed husband Ravius.
EDCS-30300716.summary: This funerary inscription records Aurelia Marcia setting up a memorial for herself and her late husband Ravius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300717.text_edition: \n LDIas qui s vixit annos Aurelius Ep coniugi et sibi suis pient[issimis ------\n
EDCS-30300717.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Ep[...], who lived X years, for his most dutiful wife and for himself and his family.
EDCS-30300717.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription likely commemorates Aurelius Ep[...] and his family, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300718.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eutychus Atili Theofae sic servus <Q=A>uartae fecit \n
EDCS-30300718.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Eutychus made this for Atilia Theopha, his most devoted wife, who lived X years.
EDCS-30300718.summary: This funerary inscription records Eutychus dedicating a monument to his wife Atilia Theopha, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300719.text_edition: \n AN Alexander filius matri bene merenti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30300719.translation: Alexander, son of [...], took care to have this made for his deserving mother.
EDCS-30300719.summary: This funerary inscription records Alexander commissioning a monument for his mother, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300724.translation: Commander of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, Oesco.
EDCS-30300724.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military officer of the Legio V Macedonica, possibly naming his post or a location, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300725.text_edition: \n Praepositus ripae legionis V Macedonicae Variana\n
EDCS-30300725.translation: Commander of the bank of the Fifth Macedonian Legion, Variana.
EDCS-30300725.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military official in charge of the riverbank defenses of the Legio V Macedonica at a place called Variana, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300726.summary: This is a simple designation of the Legio V Macedonica, likely found on military equipment or a building, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300727.translation: The Fifth Macedonian Legion, Second Cohort.
EDCS-30300727.summary: This inscription identifies a specific unit, the Second Cohort of the Legio V Macedonica, likely on military property or equipment, from the Imperial period.

EDCS-30300728.translation: The Third Cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica.
EDCS-30300728.summary: This is a military inscription identifying the third cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, likely indicating its location or a detachment. It is from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300729.translation: The Fourth Cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica.
EDCS-30300729.summary: This is a military inscription identifying the fourth cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, likely indicating its location or a detachment. It is from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30300730.translation: The Fifth Cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica.
EDCS-30300730.summary: This is a military inscription identifying the fifth cohort of the Fifth Legion Macedonica, likely indicating its location or a detachment. It is from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30600116.text_edition: \n Lucili Flavi Valentis praepositi cohortis Flaviae\n
EDCS-30600116.translation: From Lucilius Valentis, prefect of the Cohors Flavia.
EDCS-30600116.summary: This is a dedication or attribution by Lucilius Valentis, prefect of the Cohors Flavia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30800054.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aemilio Aemiliano Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae cons(uli) proconsuli ab Viminacio milia passuum CC\n
EDCS-30800054.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus, pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, 200 Roman miles from Viminacium.
EDCS-30800054.summary: This milestone records a dedication of 200 miles from Viminacium to Emperor Aemilianus, dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-30800337.text_edition: \n Indolis egregiae et pius at morior Iuvenilem eripuere cito rumpentes stamina Parcae quem supremam artem docuit dulcis Cytherea fecerat is doctus rapidas tam naviter artes Pieridum Aonia manantes vertice Tymbrio heu facinus rapido caruisti tramite lucem ter quinos uno crescens anno amplius annos emensus senio accumulato in membris imis Marcus Messius et tata Maxima heredes duo monumentum memoriae\n
EDCS-30800337.translation: He died young, snatched away by the Fates who were breaking the threads of life, although skilled in the sweet art taught by Venus, and having mastered the swift arts of the Muses flowing from Mount Aonia. Alas, the deed, you were snatched from the light of life in a swift path, having completed fifteen years and more, accumulated in old age in his limbs. Marcus Messius and his father Maxima, the two heirs, erected this monument to his memory.
EDCS-30800337.summary: This is a funerary epigram lamenting the premature death of a young man named Marcus Messius, who was skilled in arts and died at fifteen, with the monument erected by his heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30800342.text_edition: \n Amphilochii Fortunae aeternae domus Furianae procuratores Cai Furi Octaviani clarissimi viri Furius Alcimus et Pontius Uranius pecunia Octavianina faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-30800342.translation: To Amphilochius, of the eternal fortune of the house of Furianus, the procurators Caius Furius Octavianus, a most distinguished man, Furius Alcimus, and Pontius Uranius, had this made with Octavianus's funds.
EDCS-30800342.summary: This inscription records the construction of a monument funded by procurators of the Furius family, dedicated to Amphilochius and the eternal fortune of the Furianus house, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30800343.text_edition: \n Deo Andino sacrum Tiberius Claudius Certus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum solvit libens merito Clemente et Prisco consulibus\n
EDCS-30800343.translation: Sacred to the god Andinus. Tiberius Claudius Certus, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the Seventh Claudian Legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, when Clemente and Prisco were consuls.
EDCS-30800343.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Andinus by Tiberius Claudius Certus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion, during the consulship of Clemente and Prisco, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30800344.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Auggustorum Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum Thallion eorundem vilicus votum solvit libens merito Pompeiano et Avito consulibus\n
EDCS-30800344.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augusti, and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps. Thallion, steward of them, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, when Pompeianus and Avitus were consuls.
EDCS-30800344.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter for the safety of the emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, and Julia Domna, erected by their steward Thallion during the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus, dating to their reign (likely 198-211 AD).

EDCS-30800345.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Catulla vixit annis XX posuerunt parentes filiae bene merenti\n
EDCS-30800345.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Catulla lived 20 years. Her parents erected this monument to their deserving daughter.
EDCS-30800345.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Catulla, who died at age 20, erected by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30800346.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Scerulaedus Sitaes sic vixit annos XXX interfectus a latronibus Sita Dasi pater filio pientissimo et sibi et Caiae Dasi coniugi bene merenti vivus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30800346.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Scerulaedus, son of Sita, lived 30 years, killed by robbers. Sita, his father, had this made for his most devoted son and for himself and for his well-deserving wife, Caia Dasius, during his lifetime.
EDCS-30800346.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Scerulaedus, who was killed by robbers, set up by his father who also provided for himself and his wife.

EDCS-30900018.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto pro salute domini nostri Augusti Aurelius Herodes pro salute sua et suorum ex viso posuit\n
EDCS-30900018.translation: To the Unconquered Hercules. Aurelius Herodes set this up by vision for the safety of our lord Augustus, and for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-30900018.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules, made by Aurelius Herodes for the safety of himself, his family, and Emperor Augustus.

EDCS-30900019.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Popilius Hermes votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30900019.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Lucius Popilius Hermes willingly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-30900019.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Lucius Popilius Hermes.

EDCS-30900020.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cereri Augustae sacrum Publius Aelius Martinus votum posuit\n
EDCS-30900020.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to Ceres Augusta. Publius Aelius Martinus set up this sacred offering and fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30900020.summary: This is a dedication of a sacred offering to Jupiter and Ceres, with a vow fulfilled by Publius Aelius Martinus.

EDCS-30900021.text_edition: \n Sedato Augusto pro salute Impperatorum Severi et Antonini Auggustorum et Genio collegii fabrum Quintus Aelius Antoninus decurio primus biselliarius magister collegii supra scripti dono dedit\n
EDCS-30900021.translation: To the Pacified Augustus. Quintus Aelius Antoninus, first decurion of the bisellarii and master of the aforementioned college, dedicated this as a gift for the safety of the Emperors Severus and Antoninus Augusti, and for the Genius of the college of artisans.
EDCS-30900021.summary: This is a dedication to the deified Emperor Augustus, made by Quintus Aelius Antoninus on behalf of the college of artisans, for the safety of Emperors Severus and Antoninus and the college's Genius.

EDCS-30900023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Chresto decurioni aedilicio coloniae Ulpiae Ratiariae vixit nnos LX et Marco Aurelio Iuliano decurioni ae<d>ili coloniae Ratiariae data immunitate munerum ordinariorum ob instantiam aquaeducti vixit annos XL Flavia Iulia socro et marito bene merentibus\n
EDCS-30900023.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Iulia arranged this for her well-deserving stepson, Marcus Aurelius Chrestus, a decurion and former aedile of the colony of Ulpia Ratiaria, who lived 60 years, and for her well-deserving husband, Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, a decurion and aedile of the colony of Ratiaria, who lived 40 years, granted immunity from ordinary duties for his work on the aqueduct.
EDCS-30900023.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two men, Marcus Aurelius Chrestus and Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, both decurions and aediles in Ratiaria, set up by Flavia Iulia for her stepson and husband, with details about their service and honors.

EDCS-30900024.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Ponti Silvini decurionis aedilis coloniae Ratiariae Iulia Marcia coniugi \n
EDCS-30900024.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Caius Pontius Silvinus, decurion and aedile of the colony of Ratiaria. Iulia Marcia for her husband.
EDCS-30900024.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Pontius Silvinus, a decurion and aedile of Ratiaria, set up by his wife, Iulia Marcia.

EDCS-30900025.text_edition: \n Serviliae Valeriae uxori Iuli Valentis IIviri AL \n
EDCS-30900025.translation: For Servilia Valeria, wife of Julius Valens, one of the two chief magistrates.
EDCS-30900025.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Servilia Valeria, wife of Julius Valens, who held a high magistracy in the colony.

EDCS-31100116.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Flavius Omn libens votum posuit\n
EDCS-31100116.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Flavius Omnes willingly set up his vow.
EDCS-31100116.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Flavius Omnes.

EDCS-31100117.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Valerius Secundinus libens votum solvit\n
EDCS-31100117.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Gaius Valerius Secundinus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31100117.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Gaius Valerius Secundinus.

EDCS-31100118.summary: This is a simple epitaph or dedication by a priest named Caisius.

EDCS-31100119.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto legio IIII Flavia felix Gordiana devota numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-31100119.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, from the Second Fourth Flavia Felix Gordiana Legion, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-31100119.summary: A dedication by the Fourth Flavia Felix Gordiana Legion to Emperor Gordian III, likely from the period of his reign (238-244 CE).

EDCS-31100120.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpia Licinia coniunx Ulpi Adiutoris vixit nnos LXXXX Ulpii Equester et Decimus matri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-31100120.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpia Licinia, wife of Ulpius Adiutor, lived 90 years. Ulpii Equester and Decimus set this up for their well-deserving mother.
EDCS-31100120.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ulpia Licinia, set up by her sons Ulpius Equester and Decimus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31100122.text_edition: \n Inpia fata mea Falconi mortemorte qui plus non vixi quam Romulus \n
EDCS-31100122.translation: My impious fate. For Falcon, who died young, and who did not live longer than Romulus.
EDCS-31100122.summary: A sorrowful epitaph, possibly for a child, lamenting the premature death of Falcon and comparing his short life to that of Romulus.

EDCS-31100139.text_edition: \n Invicto deo Ision Caesaris nostri servus vilicus vectigalis Illyrici templum omni re instructum a solo pecunia sua faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-31100139.translation: To the unconquered god. The steward of the Imperial revenue from Illyricum, acting as the overseer, arranged for this temple, fully equipped with all things, to be built from the ground up with his own money.
EDCS-31100139.summary: A dedication by a steward (vilicus) of Illyricum for a temple built at his own expense to an unidentified 'unconquered god', likely during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31100140.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et felicitati stationis Herculanus vilicus \n
EDCS-31100140.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the prosperity of the station. Herculanus, the steward.
EDCS-31100140.summary: A dedication by a steward named Herculanus to Jupiter and the 'prosperity of the station', likely a military or administrative post, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31100142.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo VPP dis deabusque et Genio stationis pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Severi Alexandri Augusti Valerianus speculator legionis IIII Flaviae Severianae Alexandrianae votum solvit libens merito Augusto Severo Alexandro Augusto II et Aufidio Marcello II consulibus\n
EDCS-31100142.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the gods and goddesses, and the Genius of the station, for the safety of our lord Emperor Severus Alexander Augustus. Valerianus, a scout of the Fourth Flavia Severiana Alexandriana Legion, paid his vow willingly and deservedly, under the consulship of Augustus Severus Alexander Augustus II and Aufidius Marcellus II.
EDCS-31100142.summary: A military dedication by Valerianus of the Fourth Flavia Severiana Alexandriana Legion to Jupiter, various deities, and the Genius of the station for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander, dated to his second consulship (222 CE).

EDCS-31100143.text_edition: \n pro salute sua et suorum Eburniani et Gauri et Attae filiorum et Aeliae Bonae coniugis et Antoniae Victorinae nurus et Corneliae Lucae sororis ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-31100143.translation: For his own safety and that of his family. Eburnianus and Gaurus and Atta, his sons, and Aelia Bona, his wife, and Antonia Victorina, his daughter-in-law, and Cornelia Luca, his sister, he set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-31100143.summary: A dedication made in fulfillment of a vow by Eburnianus for the safety of himself and his extended family, including his wife, sons, daughter-in-law, and sister, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31100145.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Aelius Maximus.
EDCS-31100145.summary: A simple funerary dedication for Publius Aelius Maximus.

EDCS-31100146.text_edition: \n Ulpie Ionice have bene valeas qui me salutas Dis Manibus Claudia Rufina vixit annis XXX Ulpius Ionicianus vixit annis XXV Ulpius Rufinus vixit annis V hic siti sunt Marcus Ulpius Ionicus coniugi et filiis bene merentibus et sibi vivus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-31100146.translation: To Ulpia Ionica, farewell, be well, you who greet me. To the Divine Shades. Claudia Rufina lived 30 years. Ulpius Ionicianus lived 25 years. Ulpius Rufinus lived 5 years. They are buried here. Marcus Ulpius Ionicus arranged to have this built for his deserving wife and sons, and for himself, while alive.
EDCS-31100146.summary: A funerary monument set up by Marcus Ulpius Ionicus for himself and his deceased wife, Claudia Rufina, and sons, Ulpius Ionicianus and Ulpius Rufinus, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31100147.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a tombstone, recording the age of a person named Anoiclus.

EDCS-31100150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nanea Prudentis filia posuit coniugi suo caro militi legionis quartae vixit annos XXX Aelio Paeto bene merenti\n
EDCS-31100150.translation: To the Divine Manes. Nanea, daughter of Prudentis, set this up for her dear husband, Aelius Paetus, a soldier of the Fourth Legion, who lived 30 years, well-deserving.
EDCS-31100150.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated by Nanea to her husband, Aelius Paetus, a soldier of the Fourth Legion, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-31100151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Anna Furiae Caeciliae serva vixit annis XXX Prisca soror eius vixit nnis XX Firmus filiis piissimis posuit\n
EDCS-31100151.translation: To the Divine Manes. Anna, servant of Furiae Caeciliae, lived 30 years. Prisca, her sister, lived 20 years. Firmus set this up for his most dutiful children.
EDCS-31100151.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Firmus for his sister Anna and another sister Prisca, providing their ages at death.

EDCS-31100152.text_edition: \n Sancto Casebono sacrum pro salutem sic Marci Antoni Rufi Felicissimus libertus\n
EDCS-31100152.translation: Sacred to Sanctus Casebonus. For the health of Marcus Antonius Rufus. Felicissimus, his freedman.
EDCS-31100152.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Felicissimus, a freedman of Marcus Antonius Rufus, for the health of his patron.

EDCS-31200131.text_edition: \n Posuerunt Herculi Bitus Biti Sautes Pa Conus Coni Tato Postumi\n
EDCS-31200131.translation: Bitus, son of Biti, Sautes, Paetus, Conus, Conius, Tato, son of Postumus, set this up to Hercules.
EDCS-31200131.summary: This is a dedication to Hercules by a group of individuals, likely setting up a shrine or offering.

EDCS-31200891.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Voconiae Epicharis vixit annis XV Eros MAEI sibi et coniugi pientissimae\n
EDCS-31200891.translation: To the Divine Manes. Voconia Epicharis lived 15 years. Eros set this up for himself and his most devoted wife.
EDCS-31200891.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Eros for himself and his wife, commemorating the life of Voconia Epicharis who died at 15.

EDCS-31200892.text_edition: \n STALAAFAVE vixit nnnos VI Marcus Aurelius Felicianus decurio municipii D<o=D>(mavianorum) coniugi rarissimae bene merenti fecit \n
EDCS-31200892.translation: Stalaa Fave lived 6 years. Marcus Aurelius Felicianus, decurion of the municipality of Domavianorum, made this for his most rare and well-deserving wife.
EDCS-31200892.summary: This tombstone was erected by Marcus Aurelius Felicianus, a decurion, for his wife and also commemorates their daughter Stalaa Fave who died at 6.

EDCS-31400056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Annaeus Saturnini filius vixit annos XXX Marcellina Annaei filia vixit annos VII Iusta Annaei filia vixit annos III titulum posuit Marcella Verecunndia marito et filiis communibus\n
EDCS-31400056.translation: To the Divine Manes. Annaeus, son of Saturninus, lived 30 years. Marcellina, daughter of Annaeus, lived 7 years. Iusta, daughter of Annaeus, lived 3 years. Marcella Verecundia set up this monument for her husband and their common children.
EDCS-31400056.summary: This tombstone commemorates Annaeus, his daughters Marcellina and Iusta, and was erected by his wife Marcella Verecundia.

EDCS-31400147.text_edition: \n Deo Marcus Sulpicius Zoticus Augustalis coloniae Ratiariae templum a solo restituit\n
EDCS-31400147.translation: To the god. Marcus Sulpicius Zoticus, Augustalis of the colony of Ratiaria, restored the temple from the ground up.
EDCS-31400147.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple by Marcus Sulpicius Zoticus, an Augustalis in the colony of Ratiaria.

EDCS-31400148.text_edition: \n O EANIAmina pro salute suam sic suorumque votum posuerunt Antiochiano et Orfito consulibus\n
EDCS-31400148.translation: The goddesses. For their own health and that of their kin, they set up this vow. Antiochianus and Orfitus were consuls.
EDCS-31400148.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to goddesses for the health of the dedicator and their family, dated by the consulship of Antiochianus and Orfitus.

EDCS-31400149.text_edition: \n Publius Iulius Secundus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus dedit |(denarios) II milia se vivo collegio fabrorum\n
EDCS-31400149.translation: Publius Iulius Secundus, honored with decurional decorations, gave 2,000 denarii during his lifetime to the college of craftsmen.
EDCS-31400149.summary: This inscription records a donation of 2,000 denarii by Publius Iulius Secundus to the college of craftsmen during his lifetime, likely for a public building or endowment.

EDCS-31400151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeli Salvi Silvini vixit annos XIIII hic situs est us \n
EDCS-31400151.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelius Salvinus Silvinus lived for 14 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-31400151.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aelius Salvinus Silvinus, who died at the age of 14.

EDCS-31400152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Fidelicula sibi se viva fecit ITHDVP \n
EDCS-31400152.translation: To the Divine Manes. Fidelicula made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-31400152.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fidelicula, who commissioned her own tomb during her lifetime.

EDCS-31400154.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Larsinia Ingenua vixit annis XXXX Publius Aelius Dionysius veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae felicis ex signifero coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-31400154.translation: To the Divine Manes. Larsinia Ingenua lived for 40 years. Publius Aelius Dionysius, a veteran of the IV Flavia Felix legion and a standard-bearer, set this up for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-31400154.summary: This funerary inscription honors Larsinia Ingenua, who died at 40, and was erected by her husband, Publius Aelius Dionysius, a veteran and standard-bearer.

EDCS-32500074.text_edition: \n IA IFALAECL militavit annos XXXIII coloniae Ulpiae Traianae Ratiariae vixit annos LXX ma patrono faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32500074.translation: She served in the army for 33 years and lived for 70 years in the colony of Ulpia Traiana Ratiaria; she arranged for this to be made for her patron.
EDCS-32500074.summary: This inscription records the military service and lifespan of a woman in the colony of Ulpia Traiana Ratiaria, who arranged for a monument to be made for her patron.

EDCS-32500075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Valeri Felicis ornati ornamentis decurionalibus qui vixit A nnis L et Cassiae Alexandrae quae vixit annis XXX Aurelius <G=C>aianus amicis bene merentibus posuit \n
EDCS-32500075.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Gaianus set this up for his well-deserving friends Caius Valerius Felix, honored with decurional decorations, who lived for 50 years, and Cassia Alexandra, who lived for 30 years.
EDCS-32500075.summary: This funerary inscription honors Caius Valerius Felix, who died at 50, and Cassia Alexandra, who died at 30, and was erected by Aurelius Gaianus.

EDCS-32500078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Ploto vixit annos V dies LVII Marcus Ulpius Trophimus Augustalis et ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus municipii Singiduni et Claudia Valentina filio\n
EDCS-32500078.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Ulpius Plotius lived for 5 years and 57 days. Marcus Ulpius Trophimus, an Augustalis and honored with decurional decorations of the municipium of Singidunum, and Claudia Valentina, set this up for their son.
EDCS-32500078.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Marcus Ulpius Plotius, who died at age 5, and was erected by his parents, Marcus Ulpius Trophimus and Claudia Valentina.

EDCS-32500079.text_edition: \n coniugi eius quae vixit nnos LXXXX Ulpia Primitiva patronis bene merentibus posuit\n
EDCS-32500079.translation: to his wife, who lived 90 years. Ulpia Primitiva set this up for her well-deserving patrons.
EDCS-32500079.summary: This inscription records Ulpia Primitiva setting up a monument for her patrons, one of whom lived to be 90 years old.

EDCS-32500081.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attius Victoris vixit annis LXX Aia Atti filia vixit annis XXX Propingus sic Atti filius vixit annis XXV Gresa Dasantis vixit annis LXXX titulum posuit Zaca Vaenti coniugi filiis et matri et sibi vivae bene merentibus\n
EDCS-32500081.translation: To the Divine Manes. Attius Victor lived for 70 years. Aia, daughter of Attius, lived for 30 years. Propingus, son of Attius, lived for 25 years. Gresa Dasantis lived for 80 years. Zaca Vaenti set up this monument for her well-deserving husband, sons, mother, and herself while alive.
EDCS-32500081.summary: This funerary inscription honors Attius Victor, Aia, Propingus, and Gresa Dasantis, and was set up by Zaca Vaenti for her family and herself.

EDCS-32500082.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Probus vixit annos LXXX Marcus Aurelius Prisgianus sic vixit annos XX Aurelia Maxima coniugi karissimo et filio pientissimo et si<b=S>i viva posuit Marcus Aurelius Capericus et Aurelia M<a=X>ximina patri et fratri bene merentibus posuerunt \n
EDCS-32500082.translation: Marcus Aurelius Probus lived for 80 years. Marcus Aurelius Prisgianus lived for 20 years. Aurelia Maxima set this up for her most beloved husband, her most dutiful son, and for herself while alive. Marcus Aurelius Capericus and Aurelia Maximina set this up for their father and well-deserving brother.
EDCS-32500082.summary: This inscription commemorates multiple family members: Aurelia Maxima erected a tomb for her husband and son, while Marcus Aurelius Capericus and Aurelia Maximina erected one for their father and brother.

EDCS-32500084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimius LVSANVS T cohortis II Aur \n
EDCS-32500084.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Septimius Lusanus of the Second Cohort of Aur[eliani].
EDCS-32500084.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Septimius Lusanus, a soldier in the Second Cohort, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500085.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Valerius decurio collegii fabrum A B H renovavit votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-32500085.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Aurelius Valerius, a decurion of the guild of builders, repaired this and set up this offering willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-32500085.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Valerius, a guild decurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period, who renovated a structure and fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-32500086.text_edition: \n Aurelius Demas vixit nnos LXXX Aurelius Lucidus et Aurelia Aquilina et Aurelius Vitalis nepotes et heredes avio bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-32500086.translation: Aurelius Demas lived 80 years. Aurelius Lucidus, Aurelia Aquilina, and Aurelius Vitalis, grandchildren and heirs, set this up for their well-deserving grandfather.
EDCS-32500086.summary: This tomb inscription records the life and death of Aurelius Demas, who lived to 80 years old, and was commemorated by his grandchildren and heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500087.text_edition: \n Dianae Antonia Procula ex viso votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32500087.translation: To Diana. Antonia Procula fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly after a vision.
EDCS-32500087.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Diana by Antonia Procula, who fulfilled a vow after receiving a vision, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500089.summary: This inscription simply records the name of Emperor Hadrian, likely a dedication or indication of imperial property from his reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-32500157.text_edition: \n Iulius Valerius Iuli filius Valens qui et Iulius Fanius Papiria \n
EDCS-32500157.translation: Julius Valerius Valens, son of Julius, of the Papiria tribe, also known as Julius Fanius.
EDCS-32500157.summary: This fragment is the beginning of a Roman full titulature, likely for an individual of some standing, giving his name, filiation, tribal affiliation, and an alternative cognomen.

EDCS-32602031.text_edition: \n Iovi et Herculi templum fecit Vecilia Tyranni Augusti liberti procuratoris locus datus ab Appaeo Hermete et Fabiis tribus\n
EDCS-32602031.translation: Vecilia built this temple to Jupiter and Hercules. It was built with land granted by Appaeus Hermetes and the Fabii. She was the freedwoman of the procurator.
EDCS-32602031.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to Jupiter and Hercules by Vecilia, with land provided by Appaeus Hermetes and the Fabii, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Filete vixit annos LX et Sergius Sultitus Iunior vixit annos XXV hic siti sunt Sergius Sultitianus miles legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis adiutor prior corniculariorum consularis matri et fratri bene merentibus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-34100027.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Filete lived 60 years and Sergius Sultitus Junior lived 25 years. They are buried here. Sergius Sultitianus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, assistant senior to the standard-bearers of the consular governor, arranged for this to be made for his well-deserving mother and brother.
EDCS-34100027.summary: This tomb inscription commemorates Valeria Filete and Sergius Sultitus Junior, and was commissioned by Sergius Sultitianus for his mother and brother, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-34100028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcia vixit annos LX Marcellina vixit annos XXX Agricola vixit annos XXI Servius Aelius Valerianus vibus! matri et sorori et fratri bene merentibus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-34100028.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcia lived 60 years, Marcellina lived 30 years, Agricola lived 21 years. Servius Aelius Valerianus arranged for this to be made for his well-deserving mother, sister, and brother.
EDCS-34100028.summary: This tomb inscription honors Marcia, Marcellina, and Agricola, and was arranged by Servius Aelius Valerianus for his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Gaianus vixit nnos LX Aurelia Surisca patrono bene merenti et sibi vibe sic posuit\n
EDCS-34100029.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Gaianus lived 60 years. Aurelia Surisca placed this for her well-deserving patron and for herself.
EDCS-34100029.summary: This tomb inscription records the death of Aurelius Gaianus, who lived to 60 years, and was set up by Aurelia Surisca for her patron and herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100030.text_edition: \n thae filius Bessus bene audivit vixit annos L titulum sibi in testamento \n
EDCS-34100030.translation: Bessus, son of Thae, lived a good life, [he] lived 50 years; he ordered this monument to be erected for himself in his will.
EDCS-34100030.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Bessus and indicating it was commissioned in his will.

EDCS-35100001.translation: The Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the sixth time, consul for the third time, proconsul, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to those serving in the fleet at Ravenna under Numerius Albanus who had completed twenty-six or more years of service, and to their children and descendants, as well as to those who were unmarried and would later marry, one wife each, at the time citizenship was granted.
EDCS-35100001.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights issued by Emperor Hadrian to veterans of the Ravenna fleet, likely dating to Hadrian's reign (117-138 AD).

EDCS-36500001.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36500001.text_edition: \n Imperator Vespasianus Caesar Augustus tribunicia potestate consul II causaris qui militaverunt in legione II adiutrice pia fidele qui bello inutiles facti ante emerita stipendia ex auctorati sunt et dimissi honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem Nonas Martias Imperatore Vespasiano Caesare Augusto II Caesare Augusti filio Vespasiano consulibus is filio Daco descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio ante aram gentis Iuliae ad podium latere dexteriore Civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes <e=I>ssent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem Nonas Martias Imperatore Vespasiano Caesare Augusto II Caesare Augusti filio Vespasiano consulibus is filio Daco descriptum et recognitum ex tabula ae[nea ------ \n
EDCS-36500001.translation: Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus, holding tribunician power, consul for the second time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to those who served in the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis, who had been rendered unfit for service in war and were discharged before completing their full term of enlistment, and to their children and descendants. For those who were unmarried, it was granted with the women they would subsequently marry, one wife each. This was inscribed and verified on a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, before the altar of the Julian family, at the base on the right side, on the Nones of March, during the consulship of Emperor Vespasian Caesar Augustus II and Caesar Augustus's son Vespasian.
EDCS-36500001.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Vespasian to veterans of the legion II Adjutrix, dated to March 71 AD, recorded on a bronze tablet in Rome.

EDCS-36600045.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36600045.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate X imperator XI consul III pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caesar Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate V consul proconsul iis qui militaverunt in classe praetoria Ravennate quae est sub Aemilio Sulleptino praefecto senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis filiisque eorum quos susceperint ex mulieribus quas secum concessa consuetudine vixisse probaverint civitatem Romanam dederunt et conubium cum iisdem quas secum habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui tunc non habuissent cum iis quas postea uxores duxissent dumtaxat singulis singulas ante diem XIII Kalendas Ianuarias Tito Murrenio Severo Caio Cassio Regalliano consulibus ex gregale Caio Iulio Valentis filio Iuliano Novioduno ex Moesia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Commodi frater divi Antonini Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate X imperator XI consul III pater patriae et Imperator Caesar Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici maximi filius divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici nepos divi Antonini Pii pronepos divi Hadriani abnepos divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepos Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate V consul proconsul iis qui militaverunt in classe praetoria Ravennate sub Aelio Sulleptino praefecto senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione nomina subscripta sunt ipsis filiisque eorum quos susceperint ex mulieribus quas secum concessa consuetudine vixisse probaverint civitatem Romanam dederunt et conubium quas tunc secum habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui tunc non habuissent cum iis quas postea uxores duxissent dumtaxat singulis singulas Ante diem VIII Kalendas Ianuarias Severo et Regalliano consulibus ex gregale Caio Iulio Valentis filio Iuliano Novioduno ex Moesia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Luci Pulli Marcionis Cai Publici Luperci Marci Iuni Pii Tiberi Claudi Iuliani Tiberi Claudi Cassandri Luci Pulli Benigni Tiberi Iuli Dativi\n
EDCS-36600045.translation: The Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the tenth time, imperator for the eleventh time, consul for the third time, father of the country, and proconsul; and Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, holding tribunician power for the fifth time, proconsul; granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage to those who served in the praetorian fleet at Ravenna under the prefect Aemilius Sulleptinus, who had completed twenty-six or more years of service and were discharged with an honorable dismissal, and to their children whom they had by women whom they had proven to have lived with by established custom, and to those who had no wives at the time, with those whom they would marry later, one wife each. This was inscribed and verified on a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva, on the 13th day before the Kalends of January, during the consulship of Titus Murrenius Severus and Gaius Cassius Regallianus, from the soldier Gaius Julius Valentis filius Julianus from Noviodunum in Moesia.
EDCS-36600045.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla to veterans of the Ravenna fleet, dated to December 205 AD, specifying conditions for the women they married.

EDCS-36600046.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-36600046.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Pius Felix Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus Brittannicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII imperator VIII cons(ul) VII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appellantur praetoria civium Romanorum et I Thracum sagittaria veterana et I Flavia Augusta Brittannica |(milliaria) et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I civium Romanorum et cohortibus X et VII et III Batavorum II Aurelia Dacorum et |(milliaria) Hemesenorum et I Lusitanorum II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et |(milliaria) Maurorum III Lusitanorum et I Thracum et VII Breucorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Noricorum et I Alpinorum peditum et I Alpinorum equitum et II Novae et I Campanorum et |(quingenaria) Hemesenorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Pomponio Basso legato quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis ante diem III Idus Augustas Lucio Iulio Messala Rutiliano et Caio Aemilio Severo Cantabrino consulibus cohortis I Montanorum equitatae cui praeest Valerius Victor ex pedite Tito Flavio Titi filio Titiano Bassiana descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Imperator Caesar divi Marci Antonini Pii Germanici Sarmatici filius divi Pii nepos divi Hadriani pronepos divi Traiani Parthici abnepos divi Nervae adnepos Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Pius Felix Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus maximus Brittannicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIII imperator VIII consul VII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V quae appellantur praetoria civium Romanorum et I Thracum sagittaria veterana et I Flavia Augusta Brittannica |(milliaria) et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I civium Romanorum et cohortibus X et VII III Batavorum II Aurelia Dacorum et |(milliaria) Hemesenorum et I Lusitanorum II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et |(milliaria) Maurorum et III Lusitanorum et I Thracum et VII Breucorum et I Thracum Germanica et I Noricorum et I Alpinorum peditum et I Alpinorum equitum et II Novae et I Campanorum et D Hemesenorum et sunt in Pannonia inferiore sub Caio Pomponio Basso legato quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quo quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas is iis data aut cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singulis\n
EDCS-36600046.translation: The Emperor Caesar Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus Pius Felix Augustus Sarmaticus Germanicus Maximus Britannicus, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the deified Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the eighteenth time, imperator for the eighth time, consul for the seventh time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in the five alae and seventeen cohorts listed, and the right of marriage with the wives they then had, or with those they would marry later, one wife each. This was inscribed and verified on a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva, on the 11th day before the Ides of August, during the consulship of Lucius Julius Messala Rutilianus and Gaius Aemilius Severus Cantabrinus, from the infantryman Titus Flavius Titi filius Titianus of cohort I Montanorum equitata, from Bassiana, originating from Moesia.
EDCS-36600046.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Commodus to veterans of various cavalry and infantry units serving in Pannonia Inferior, dated to August 13, 181 AD.

EDCS-37200034.summary: A short dedication fragment mentioning Constantine, likely a religious or commemorative inscription.

EDCS-38100340.text_edition: \n Lucius Tettius Fonteianus decurio pontifex ex voto\n
EDCS-38100340.translation: Lucius Tettius Fonteianus, decurion and pontifex, by vow.
EDCS-38100340.summary: A dedication recording Lucius Tettius Fonteianus, a decurion and pontifex, making an offering in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-38100341.text_edition: \n Herculi Victori Titus Titius Herculanus Augustalis coloniae Ratiariae ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus testamento poni iussit\n
EDCS-38100341.translation: To Hercules Victor, Titus Titius Herculanus, an Augustalis of the colony of Ratiaria, honored with decurional ornaments, ordered this to be set up by his will.
EDCS-38100341.summary: A dedication to Hercules Victor by Titus Titius Herculanus, a prominent citizen of Ratiaria, commissioned in his will.

EDCS-38100347.summary: A simple inscription recording the name Lucius Coelius Saturninus, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-38100348.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Aurelius Iulianus Iuliani sacerdos eiusdem dei ex provincia Syria Coele regione Cyrrhensi vico Capersina \n
EDCS-38100348.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Aurelius Iulianus, his priest from the province of Syria Coele, from the region of Cyrrhus, from the village of Capersina.
EDCS-38100348.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Aurelius Iulianus, a priest of the deity, identifying his origin from Syria Coele.

EDCS-38100349.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Timoni Dassi decurioni cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum interfecti sic a Costobocos sic vixit annis L militavit annis XXIII Postumia Spes coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-38100349.translation: To the departed spirits of Timon, son of Dasius, decurion of the II Aurelia cohort of Dardanians, killed by the Costoboci. He lived for 50 years and served for 23 years. Postumia Spes placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-38100349.summary: A tombstone recording the death and service of a decurion named Timon, who died in conflict with the Costoboci, dedicated by his wife.

EDCS-38100350.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Arteas vixit nnos XLV sodales a genio macel<l=I>i curante genero Fortunato \n
EDCS-38100350.translation: To the departed spirits of Gaius Julius Arteas, who lived for 45 years. Erected by the association of the genius of the market, with his son-in-law Fortunatus overseeing.
EDCS-38100350.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Arteas, set up by a collegium (association) and supervised by his son-in-law.

EDCS-38100351.inscription_type: tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
